topics.title: Estimation of Theoretical Number of Stages Using McCabe Thiele Method – Tray Design – Tray Efficiency – Pressure Drop
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT601 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Theoretical Number of Stages Using McCabe Thiele Method – Tray Design – Tray Efficiency – Pressure Drop

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 McCabe Thiele Method — The Graphical Workhorse of Distillation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PCCHT601 / Module 1):**
> The **McCabe Thiele Method** is a rigorous graphical design procedure proposed by Warren L. McCabe and Ernest W. Thiele (1925) for determining the **theoretical number of equilibrium stages** required to achieve a specified separation of a binary liquid mixture in a continuous distillation column. It is constructed on an **x–y equilibrium diagram** using the rectifying-section operating line, the stripping-section operating line, the feed line (q-line), the 45° diagonal (y = x), and the equilibrium curve.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Staircase & Stairs" Analogy

Imagine a **multi-storey building** with a staircase connecting the ground floor to the roof.

- The **roof** = the distillate purity (very pure light component, e.g., 95% ethanol).
- The **ground floor** = the bottoms purity (impure heavy component, e.g., 5% ethanol).
- The **staircase** = the series of trays inside a distillation column.
- **Each step you climb** = one **theoretical (equilibrium) stage**, where the rising vapor and descending liquid achieve perfect thermodynamic equilibrium.
- The **equilibrium curve** = the architectural maximum height you *could* climb with a perfect step. Reality (with imperfect mass transfer) means each real step is shorter — that gap is the **Murphree tray efficiency**.

The **McCabe Thiele plot** is simply drawing that staircase between the operating lines and the equilibrium curve on a piece of graph paper.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Students must be able to *construct* the McCabe Thiele diagram given **α (relative volatility), xD, xB, xF, q, R, Rmin**, and to *step off* the theoretical stages starting from the top (distillate composition) and ending below the feed point.

---

## 1.2 Tray Design — The Hardware Realization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** Tray (or plate) design is the **mechanical sizing** of an actual distillation column internal. It involves selecting the tray type (sieve, valve, bubble-cap), determining **column diameter (Dc)**, **tray spacing (TS)**, **weir length (lw)**, **downcomer area (Ad)**, **hole area (Ah)**, **submergence / weir height (hw)**, and the **number of real trays** from the theoretical count using an appropriate **overall column efficiency (Eo)**.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Office Cubicle Farm"

A tray column is like a **multi-floor office building** where:
- Each floor (tray) is a **meeting room** where vapor (sales team) and liquid (operations team) interact.
- The **ceiling** = the downcomer entrance; the **floor** = the tray deck.
- The **bubblers on the desk** = sieve holes or valves that disperse vapor into liquid.
- The **meeting duration (residence time)** = weir height / liquid rate.
- The **energy required to host each meeting** = **pressure drop per tray**.

A **good tray design** = enough meetings, but no flooding, no weeping, and not too much energy cost.

---

## 1.3 Tray Efficiency — How Real Trays Underperform the Ideal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** Tray efficiency quantifies the **deviation of a real tray from theoretical (equilibrium) behavior**. Three principal types are used in KTU curriculum:
> 1. **Murphree Tray Efficiency (E_MV or E_mV)** — for the vapor phase on a single tray.
> 2. **Murphree Point Efficiency (E_OG or E_mV,point)** — at a single point on the tray.
> 3. **Overall Column Efficiency (E_o)** — relates *theoretical stages* to *actual trays* directly.

### Analogy — "The Leaky Bucket"

A theoretical tray is a **bucket that catches 100% of the water it should** (perfect equilibrium). A real tray is a **bucket with small leaks** and uneven water distribution. The fraction of water it *actually* catches versus what it *should* catch = the **efficiency**.

---

## 1.4 Pressure Drop — The Energy Cost Per Tray

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** **Tray pressure drop** is the total hydraulic head loss experienced by the vapor as it passes upward through one tray and the liquid residing on it. It is the sum of **dry tray pressure drop**, **hydraulic head of liquid on the tray**, and **residual (surface tension) head**. Total column ΔP = (pressure drop per tray) × (number of actual trays).

### Analogy — "The Bathroom Shower"

Water (vapor) sprayed through a perforated disc (sieve tray) against a layer of soap suds (liquid on tray) loses pressure. **More suds (deeper liquid) + smaller holes (lower % hole area) = higher pressure drop**, just like a low-pressure shower head.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Benchmarks:**
> - Tray spacing (TS) typically **0.45 m to 0.91 m** (commonly 0.61 m = 24 in).
> - Typical **dry tray pressure drop** = **50 to 100 mm of liquid** (or 0.5–1.0 kPa).
> - Typical **total pressure drop per tray** = **70 to 125 mm of liquid**.
> - Acceptable **Eo** for hydrocarbon systems = **60% to 80%**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of the McCabe Thiele Method

1. **Constant molar overflow (CMO)** — sensible heats approximately cancel; molar latent heats of vaporization of the two components are equal; heat of mixing is negligible; sensible heat changes are small compared with latent heats. This makes operating lines **straight** rather than curved.
2. **Equilibrium data** is available (relative volatility α or full y*–x curve).
3. **Binary system** only (the method is *not* directly applicable to multi-component mixtures).
4. **Adiabatic operation** — negligible heat loss to surroundings.
5. Each stage is a **theoretical (equilibrium) stage**.

> [!WARNING]
> If molar latent heats differ by more than ~10% or sensible heat effects dominate, the operating lines **curve** and the McCabe Thiele method gives an approximate (but still useful) answer. A more rigorous method (e.g., Lewis-Matheson, Thiele-Geddes) is then required.

---

## 2.2 The Five Key Graphical Elements

| # | Element | Equation | Slope / Location |
|---|---------|----------|------------------|
| 1 | 45° Diagonal | $y = x$ | Reference line |
| 2 | Equilibrium Curve | $y^* = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1+(\alpha - 1)x}$ | Above diagonal |
| 3 | Rectifying Line (above feed) | $y_{n+1} = \dfrac{R}{R+1}\,x_n + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ | Slope $R/(R+1)$, y-intercept $x_D/(R+1)$ |
| 4 | Stripping Line (below feed) | $y_{m+1} = \dfrac{L'}{V'}\,x_m - \dfrac{B x_B}{V'}$ | Slope $L'/V'$, x-intercept $x_B$ when $y=0$ |
| 5 | q-line | $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1}\,x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | Slope $q/(q-1)$ |

where $R = L/D$ is the **reflux ratio**, $V = L + D$ is the **vapor flow in rectifying section**, $V' = L' + B$ in stripping section, and $L' = L + qF$.

---

## 2.3 Special Operating Conditions

- **Total Reflux Condition ($R = \infty$):** No products withdrawn. Operating lines coincide with the diagonal $y = x$. The minimum number of stages $N_{min}$ is stepped off between the diagonal and the equilibrium curve. Computed by the **Fenske Equation**:
$$N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\cdot\dfrac{1-x_B}{x_B}\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$$

- **Minimum Reflux Condition ($R_{min}$):** An infinite number of stages. The intersection of the q-line with the equilibrium curve (or its pinch) determines $R_{min}$:
$$R_{min} = \dfrac{x_D - y_q^*}{y_q^* - x_q}$$
where $(x_q, y_q^*)$ is the intersection of the q-line and equilibrium curve.

- **Operating Reflux:** Typical KTU design practice: $R = (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5)\,R_{min}$.

---

## 2.4 Feed Thermal Condition (q-value)

| Feed State | q | q-line Slope | y-intercept |
|------------|---|--------------|-------------|
| Subcooled liquid | >1 | 1/(q-1) finite |  |
| Saturated liquid | 1 | Vertical (∞) | x = z_F |
| Saturated vapor | 0 | Horizontal (0) | y = z_F |
| Saturated liquid+vapor | 0 to 1 | Negative |  |
| Superheated vapor | <0 |  |  |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Tray Design + Efficiency + Pressure Drop)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** for the 2024 Scheme ESE and internal assessments.

### Group A — Column Diameter & Tray Hydraulics

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| A1 | Vapor volumetric flow | $Q_V = V \cdot MW_{avg} / (\rho_V \cdot 3600)$ | m³/s |
| A2 | Liquid volumetric flow | $Q_L = L \cdot MW_{L,avg} / (\rho_L \cdot 3600)$ | m³/s |
| A3 | Souders-Brown flooding velocity | $u_{v,flood} = C_{SB}\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$ | m/s |
| A4 | Operating vapor velocity | $u_v = 0.75 \text{ to } 0.85\,u_{v,flood}$ | m/s |
| A5 | Column cross-section net area | $A_{net} = Q_V / u_v$ | m² |
| A6 | Column cross-section total area | $A_c = A_{net} / (1 - A_d/A_c)$ | m²; iterate |
| A7 | Column diameter | $D_c = \sqrt{4 A_c / \pi}$ | m |

### Group B — Tray Pressure Drop (Standard KTU Correlation)

The **total tray pressure drop** $h_t$ in **mm of liquid** is the sum of three heads:

$$h_t = h_d + h_l + h_\sigma$$

| # | Term | Formula | Description |
|---|------|---------|-------------|
| B1 | Dry tray pressure drop | $h_d = 51 \cdot \dfrac{\rho_V}{\rho_L} \cdot \dfrac{u_0^2}{C_o^2}$ | mm liquid; orifice coefficient $C_o$ ≈ 0.65–0.85 |
| B2 | Effective liquid head | $h_l = \beta \cdot (h_w + h_{ow})$ | mm; $\beta \approx 0.5$–$0.6$ for sieve |
| B3 | Residual / surface tension head | $h_\sigma = \dfrac{4 \sigma}{\rho_L \cdot g \cdot d_h}$ | mm; small, often neglected |
| B4 | Weir height | $h_w = 40 \text{ to } 90$ mm | typical sieve |
| B5 | Height over weir (Francis) | $h_{ow} = 750 \cdot \left[\dfrac{Q_L}{l_w}\right]^{2/3}$ | mm; $l_w$ in m, $Q_L$ in m³/s |
| B6 | Total column ΔP | $\Delta P_{col} = h_t \cdot N_{actual} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_L \cdot g}{1000}$ | kPa |

### Group C — Tray Efficiency Correlations

| # | Efficiency | Formula | Notes |
|---|------------|---------|-------|
| C1 | Murphree Vapor Efficiency (single tray) | $E_{MV} = \dfrac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}}$ | where $y_n^*$ is vapor in equilibrium with $x_n$ leaving the tray |
| C2 | Murphree Point Efficiency (single point) | $E_{OG} = \dfrac{y - y_{n+1}}{y^* - y_{n+1}}$ | A.I.Ch.E. correlation |
| C3 | Relationship (assuming plug flow) | $E_{MV} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\left(e^{\lambda E_{OG}} - 1\right)$, $\lambda = mV/L$ | for $mV/L \ne 1$ |
| C4 | Overall Column Efficiency (O'Connell) | $E_o = 0.492\,(\mu_L \alpha)^{-0.245}$ | $\mu_L$ in cP |
| C5 | Actual number of trays | $N_{actual} = N_{theoretical} / E_o$ | rounds up |
| C6 | Actual feed tray | Step from top, locate on diagram | use integer + fractional |

### Group D — Operating Limits (KTU Standard)

| Limit | Condition | Typical Value |
|-------|-----------|---------------|
| Weeping | $u_0 \ge u_{0,min}$ | $u_{0,min}$ from Fair correlation |
| Flooding | $u_v \le u_{v,flood}$ | Factor 0.75–0.85 |
| Entrainment | $\psi \le 0.1$ | fraction |
| Turndown ratio | $u_{v,max}/u_{v,min}$ | 2 to 4 |
| Recommended hole size $d_h$ | 3 to 12 mm | sieve |
| % Open area | 8% to 15% |  |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas are the daily bread of process engineers working in **petrochemical refineries** (crude distillation, naphtha splitters), **alcohol production** (ethanol dehydration columns), **air separation units** (LOX/LIN columns), and **pharmaceutical solvent recovery** units.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rectifying-Section Operating Line

Consider the **rectifying section** (top section, above the feed plate) of a continuous distillation column operating at **steady state**. Let the tray be numbered from the top.

**Mass Balance on the Rectifying Section** (envelope drawn from the top tray down to tray $n$):

**Total material balance:**
$$V_{n+1} = L_n + D$$

**Component (light key) balance:**
$$V_{n+1}\,y_{n+1} = L_n\,x_n + D\,x_D$$

**Applying the CMO assumption:** $L_n = L$ and $V_{n+1} = V$ are constant throughout the section. Also $V = L + D$, hence $L = V - D$. Substituting:

$$V\,y_{n+1} = (V - D)\,x_n + D\,x_D$$

Divide by $V$ and rearrange:

$$y_{n+1} = \left(1 - \dfrac{D}{V}\right) x_n + \dfrac{D}{V}\,x_D$$

Since $R = L/D$ and $V = L + D = D(R+1)$, we have $D/V = 1/(R+1)$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{\,y_{n+1} = \dfrac{R}{R+1}\,x_n + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}\,}$$

This is the **rectifying operating line**: slope $= R/(R+1)$ and $y$-intercept $= x_D/(R+1)$.

> It passes through the point $(x_D, x_D)$ on the 45° diagonal.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Stripping-Section Operating Line

Apply an **overall mass balance on the entire column**:
$$F = D + B$$

**Component balance:**
$$F\,z_F = D\,x_D + B\,x_B$$

**Mass balance on the stripping envelope** (from bottom up to tray $m$):
$$L'_m = V'_{m+1} + B$$

**Component balance on the stripping envelope:**
$$L'_m\,x_m = V'_{m+1}\,y_{m+1} + B\,x_B$$

By CMO, $L'_m = L'$ and $V'_{m+1} = V'$:

$$L'\,x_m = V'\,y_{m+1} + B\,x_B$$

$$y_{m+1} = \dfrac{L'}{V'}\,x_m - \dfrac{B\,x_B}{V'}$$

And from overall balances: $V' = V - (1-q)F = L + D - (1-q)F$ and $L' = L + qF$. This simplifies to:

$$\boxed{\,y_{m+1} = \dfrac{L+qF}{L + D - (1-q)F}\,x_m - \dfrac{B x_B}{L + D - (1-q)F}\,}$$

This **stripping line** passes through the point $(x_B, x_B)$ on the 45° diagonal.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the q-line (Feed Line)

Consider a feed stream entering the column. Let $q$ be the **molar fraction of the feed that is liquid** (thermal condition parameter). The feed contributes:
- $qF$ moles of liquid (joining the liquid downflow $L$)
- $(1-q)F$ moles of vapor (joining the vapor upflow $V$)

**Material balance across the feed plate:**
$$L' = L + qF \qquad V' = V - (1-q)F$$

**Component balance across the feed plate:**
$$L'\,x_{feed} = L\,x_{feed,below} + V\,y_{feed,above} - (1-q)F\,y_F + qF\,x_F$$

Simplifying using $x_F = z_F$ for binary feed and equating liquid/vapor compositions at the feed point $(x_q, y_q)$:

$$y_q = \dfrac{q}{q-1}\,x_q - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$$

$$\boxed{\,y = \dfrac{q}{q-1}\,x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}\,}$$

**Slope = $q/(q-1)$**, passes through the point $(z_F, z_F)$ on the 45° line.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Fenske Equation (Minimum Stages at Total Reflux)

At total reflux ($D = 0$, $B = 0$), $L = V$ for all stages. The operating line collapses to $y_{n+1} = x_n$. For a binary system with relative volatility $\alpha$:

$$y_n = \dfrac{\alpha\,x_n}{1+(\alpha-1)\,x_n} \quad \text{and} \quad y_{n+1} = x_n$$

So $x_{n+1} = y_n = \dfrac{\alpha\,x_n}{1+(\alpha-1)\,x_n}$. Define the **relative volatility ratio**:

$$\beta_n = \dfrac{y_n/(1-y_n)}{x_n/(1-x_n)} = \alpha$$

For two consecutive stages, the ratio of $y/(1-y)$ to $x/(1-x)$ is $\alpha$. Recursively:

$$\dfrac{y_1/(1-y_1)}{z_F/(1-z_F)} = \alpha^{N_{min}} \cdot \dfrac{x_{N+1}/(1-x_{N+1})}{z_F/(1-z_F)}$$

At the top $y_1 = x_D$ and at the bottom $x_{N+1} = x_B$. Hence:

$$\dfrac{x_D/(1-x_D)}{x_B/(1-x_B)} = \alpha^{N_{min}}$$

Taking logarithms:

$$\boxed{\,N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\cdot\dfrac{1-x_B}{x_B}\right]}{\ln \alpha}\,}$$

For multicomponent systems, the $\alpha$ used is between the **light key (LK)** and **heavy key (HK)**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of $R_{min}$ (Minimum Reflux Ratio)

At minimum reflux, an **infinite number of stages** is required. Geometrically, this occurs at the **pinch point** where the operating line intersects the equilibrium curve.

**Rectifying line equation:**
$$y = \dfrac{R}{R+1}\,x + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$$

**Solving for R at the pinch point $(x_q, y_q)$:**
$$R_{min} + 1 = \dfrac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1} \implies R_{min} = \dfrac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q}$$

In the case where the **q-line is tangent** to the equilibrium curve (azeotropic systems), the pinch point is at the tangent contact.

---

## 3.6 Derivation of Murphree Vapor Tray Efficiency

Consider a single tray where vapor of composition $y_{n+1}$ enters from below and leaves with composition $y_n$. The liquid on the tray is well-mixed with composition $x_n$.

**Definition** (vapor basis):
$$E_{MV} = \dfrac{\text{actual vapor composition change}}{\text{equilibrium vapor composition change}} = \dfrac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}}$$

where $y_n^*$ is the vapor composition that would be **in equilibrium** with the liquid leaving the tray ($x_n$).

**Derivation from a point balance:** For a single point on the tray with point efficiency $E_{OG}$:

$$\dfrac{dy}{y^* - y} = -E_{OG}\,dA / A$$

Integration (assuming $y^* \approx m x + b$ linear) gives:

$$E_{MV} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\left(e^{\lambda E_{OG}} - 1\right), \quad \text{where } \lambda = \dfrac{mV}{L}$$

When $\lambda \to 0$ (large $L$): $E_{MV} \to E_{OG}$. When $\lambda = 1$: $E_{MV} = E_{OG}$ (special case).

---

## 3.7 Worked Example — Full McCabe Thiele + Efficiency + Tray Sizing

**Problem Statement (KTU Standard Pattern):**
A continuous distillation column separates a benzene–toluene mixture. Given:
- $x_F = 0.40$, $x_D = 0.95$, $x_B = 0.05$
- Feed: saturated liquid ($q = 1$)
- $\alpha_{avg} = 2.47$ (benzene/toluene)
- Reflux ratio $R = 1.5 R_{min}$
- Pressure = 1 atm

**Step 1 — Compute $N_{min}$ (Fenske):**
$$N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{0.95}{0.05}\cdot\dfrac{0.95}{0.05}\right]}{\ln 2.47} = \dfrac{\ln(361)}{\ln 2.47} = \dfrac{5.886}{0.9046} = 6.51 \approx 7 \text{ stages}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $R_{min}$:**

For $q = 1$, the q-line is vertical at $x = 0.40$. Intersection with equilibrium curve:

$$y^* = \dfrac{2.47(0.40)}{1 + (1.47)(0.40)} = \dfrac{0.988}{1.588} = 0.6221$$

$$R_{min} = \dfrac{0.95 - 0.6221}{0.6221 - 0.40} = \dfrac{0.3279}{0.2221} = 1.476$$

**Step 3 — Operating R:**
$$R = 1.5 \times 1.476 = 2.214$$

**Step 4 — Construct McCabe Thiele and step off:**

- Rectifying line: $y = \dfrac{2.214}{3.214}\,x + \dfrac{0.95}{3.214} = 0.689\,x + 0.296$
- Stripping line: passes through $(0.05, 0.05)$ and the intersection of the rectifying line with the q-line at $x = 0.40$:
$$y_{q-intersect} = 0.689(0.40) + 0.296 = 0.5716$$
- Stripping slope: $\dfrac{0.5716 - 0.05}{0.40 - 0.05} = \dfrac{0.5216}{0.35} = 1.490$ — wait, this is > 1, which is fine for $q=1$ (saturated liquid).

**Step 5 — Step off stages (graphical):**
$N_{theoretical} \approx 14$ (rectifying: 6 stages; stripping: 8 stages). The feed plate is the 7th from the top.

**Step 6 — Apply O'Connell efficiency:**
$\mu_L \approx 0.34$ cP (toluene-rich), $\alpha = 2.47$. So:
$$E_o = 0.492\,(0.34 \times 2.47)^{-0.245} = 0.492\,(0.8398)^{-0.245}$$
$$E_o = 0.492 \times e^{-0.245 \times \ln(0.8398)} = 0.492 \times e^{-0.245 \times (-0.1744)} = 0.492 \times e^{0.0427} = 0.492 \times 1.0436 = 0.514$$

So $E_o \approx 51\%$.

**Step 7 — Actual trays:**
$$N_{actual} = 14 / 0.514 = 27.2 \to 28 \text{ actual trays}$$
Feed tray (actual) = $8 / 0.514 \approx 15.6 \to$ 16th from top.

**Step 8 — Column sizing (assume $V = 80$ kmol/h, $MW_{avg} = 80$ kg/kmol, $\rho_V = 2.8$ kg/m³, $\rho_L = 800$ kg/m³):**

Volumetric vapor flow:
$$Q_V = \dfrac{80 \times 80}{2.8 \times 3600} = 0.635 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Souders-Brown with $C_{SB} = 0.08$ m/s (typical for 0.61 m tray spacing):
$$u_{v,flood} = 0.08\sqrt{\dfrac{800 - 2.8}{2.8}} = 0.08 \times 16.86 = 1.349 \text{ m/s}$$

Operating at 80% flooding:
$$u_v = 0.80 \times 1.349 = 1.079 \text{ m/s}$$

Net area:
$$A_{net} = 0.635 / 1.079 = 0.589 \text{ m}^2$$

Assuming downcomers occupy 12% of total area:
$$A_c = 0.589 / 0.88 = 0.669 \text{ m}^2$$

$$D_c = \sqrt{4 \times 0.669 / \pi} = 0.923 \text{ m} \approx 1.0 \text{ m (round to standard size)}$$

**Step 9 — Pressure drop per tray:**

Hole velocity: assume 9% open area, $A_h = 0.09 \times 0.589 = 0.053$ m².
$$u_0 = 0.635 / 0.053 = 11.98 \text{ m/s}$$

Dry tray pressure drop ($C_o = 0.75$):
$$h_d = 51 \cdot \dfrac{2.8}{800} \cdot \dfrac{11.98^2}{0.75^2} = 51 \times 0.0035 \times 255.0 = 45.5 \text{ mm liquid}$$

Weir height $h_w = 50$ mm; weir length $l_w = 0.7 D_c = 0.7$ m; $Q_L = 0.001$ m³/s.
$$h_{ow} = 750 \cdot \left[\dfrac{0.001}{0.7}\right]^{2/3} = 750 \times 0.00143^{2/3} = 750 \times 0.00277 = 2.08 \text{ mm}$$

Effective liquid head ($\beta = 0.55$):
$$h_l = 0.55 \times (50 + 2.08) = 28.6 \text{ mm}$$

Residual head (small, neglect):
$$h_t = 45.5 + 28.6 + 0 = 74.1 \text{ mm liquid per tray}$$

**Total column pressure drop:**
$$\Delta P_{col} = 74.1 \text{ mm} \times 28 \times \dfrac{800 \times 9.81}{1000} = 16.28 \text{ kPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A typical KTU design spec is $\Delta P_{col} \le 25$–$50$ kPa per column, so this design is **acceptable**.

---

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — KTU Exam-Critical Summary

| Step | Concept | What KTU Tests |
|------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Fenske | $N_{min}$ from $\alpha, x_D, x_B$ |
| 2 | Underwood | $R_{min}$ from q-line/equilibrium intersection |
| 3 | Gilliland | Locate $R$ vs $N$ on correlation chart |
| 4 | McCabe Thiele | Step off $N$ for chosen $R$ |
| 5 | Feed plate | Locate switch from rectifying to stripping |
| 6 | Efficiency | Convert $N_{theor}$ to $N_{actual}$ |
| 7 | Column diameter | Souders-Brown + flooding |
| 8 | Pressure drop | Sum of three heads |
| 9 | Check limits | Weeping, entrainment, flooding |
| 10 | Reflux pump | Total condenser duty $Q_C = V \cdot \lambda$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 McCabe Thiele Construction Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start with given data: alpha, xD, xB, xF, q, R] --> B1[Compute Nmin via Fenske equation]
    B1 --> B2[Compute Rmin by q-line / equilibrium intersection]
    B2 --> B3[Choose operating R = 1.2 to 1.5 times Rmin]
    B3 --> C1[Draw equilibrium curve y-star vs x]
    C1 --> C2[Draw 45 degree diagonal y = x]
    C2 --> C3[Plot q-line through zF on diagonal with slope q over q minus 1]
    C3 --> C4[Plot rectifying line through xD on diagonal with slope R over R plus 1]
    C4 --> C5[Plot stripping line through xB on diagonal through q-line intersection]
    C5 --> D1[Start at xD on diagonal]
    D1 --> D2[Move horizontally to equilibrium curve at point P1]
    D2 --> D3[Move vertically to rectifying line at point P2]
    D3 --> D4{Is x less than xB?}
    D4 -- No --> D5[Step count N = N + 1]
    D5 --> D6{Switched past q-line?}
    D6 -- No --> D2
    D6 -- Yes --> E1[Use stripping line for further steps]
    E1 --> D2
    D4 -- Yes --> F1[Stop: N theoretical stages obtained]
    F1 --> G1[Apply efficiency: Nactual = Ntheor / Eo]
    G1 --> H1[Done]
```

## 4.2 Tray Internal — Sieve Tray Anatomy (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TrayDeck[Sieve Tray Cross Section]
        IN1[Inlet Downcomer from tray above] --> DS[Distribution Zone]
        DS --> AM[Active Mass Transfer Area with Vapor Risers]
        AM --> OW[Outlet Weir]
        OW --> OD[Outlet Downcomer to tray below]
        VP1[Vapor Riser Inlet at Tray Floor] --> VR[Perforated Sieve Holes 5 to 12 mm Diameter]
        VR --> VC[Vapor Caps Liquid Layer 40 to 90 mm]
        VC --> AM
    end
```

## 4.3 Tray Design Procedure (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Inputs]
        I1[F, xF, xD, xB, alpha, R, P]
        I2[Physical properties: rhoV, rhoL, muL, sigma]
    end
    subgraph B[Step 1: Equilibrium and Operating Lines]
        S1[Compute Nmin, Rmin, choose R]
    end
    subgraph C[Step 2: Theoretical Stages]
        S2[McCabe Thiele stepping]
    end
    subgraph D[Step 3: Real Stages]
        S3[Eo correlation, Nactual]
    end
    subgraph E[Step 4: Column Sizing]
        S4[Souders-Brown, Dc]
    end
    subgraph F[Step 5: Tray Hydraulics]
        S5[hd, hl, hsigma, total ht]
    end
    subgraph G[Step 6: Verification]
        S6[Weeping, flooding, entrainment check]
    end
    subgraph H[Output]
        O1[Dc, Nactual, feed tray, total Delta P]
    end
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H
```

## 4.4 Tray Efficiency Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PE[Point Efficiency Eog] -->|Lewis case 1 plug flow| MV[Murphree Vapor Efficiency EmV]
    MV -->|Lewis case 3 complete mixing both phases| COL[Overall Column Efficiency Eo]
    MV -->|integration| MV2[Murphree EmV by Lewis case 1]
    COL --> ACT[Actual Trays Nact = Ntheor over Eo]
    EoC[OConnell correlation Eo = 0.492 times muL alpha to the power minus 0.245] -.-> COL
    EoD[Drickamer Bradford] -.-> COL
    EoE[AIChE method] -.-> PE
```

## 4.5 Pressure Drop Components (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Total Tray Pressure Drop ht] --> P2[hd Dry Tray 50 to 100 mm]
    P1 --> P3[hl Effective Liquid Head]
    P3 --> P3a[beta times hw plus how]
    P1 --> P4[hsigma Surface Tension 4 sigma over rhoL g dh]
    P2 --> P5[51 times rhoV over rhoL times u0 squared over Co squared]
    P3a --> P5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: McCabe Thiele Assumption

**Q1a.** State the **two principal assumptions** of the McCabe Thiele method. How is the **constant molar overflow** assumption violated in practice? `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Constant molar overflow (CMO):** Molar latent heats of both components are equal; sensible heat changes are negligible; heat of mixing is zero. This makes operating lines straight (3 marks).

2. **Violation in practice:** When the difference in latent heats exceeds ~10% (e.g., ethanol-water, ammonia-water systems), or when sensible heat effects are significant (large temperature differences between top and bottom), the operating lines **curve**, requiring a more rigorous method such as **Ponchon-Savarit** or the **Lewis-Matheson** stage-by-stage calculation. The McCabe Thiele method then gives an *approximate* but acceptable result.

---

### Question 2: Tray Efficiency Types

**Q1b.** Differentiate between **Murphree point efficiency**, **Murphree tray efficiency**, and **overall column efficiency**. Give the formula relating the first two. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Point efficiency $E_{OG}$:** Efficiency measured at a *single point* on the tray. Depends on local mass transfer coefficients.
- **Murphree tray efficiency $E_{MV}$:** Efficiency of the *entire tray* (assumes uniform liquid composition; vapor may be plug flow or mixed).
- **Overall column efficiency $E_o$:** Ratio of *theoretical* stages to *actual* trays for the whole column.
- **Relation:** $E_{MV} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\left(e^{\lambda E_{OG}} - 1\right)$ where $\lambda = mV/L$ (3 marks).

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, students answer **ONE full question of 14 marks** from a Module. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each (or 7+7, sometimes 10+4 patterns). We provide TWO alternate full questions (A and B) per KTU choice pattern.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**A continuous distillation column is to be designed to separate a binary mixture of methanol–water at atmospheric pressure. The following data are given:**

- Mole fraction methanol in feed: $x_F = 0.30$
- Distillate purity: $x_D = 0.92$
- Bottoms purity: $x_B = 0.02$
- Feed condition: Saturated liquid ($q = 1$)
- Average relative volatility $\alpha_{avg} = 3.5$
- Reflux ratio $R = 1.4\,R_{min}$
- Tray efficiency to be estimated by O'Connell correlation; tray type: sieve.
- Viscosity of liquid at average column temperature $\mu_L = 0.45$ cP.

#### Part (a) — Determine Number of Theoretical Stages using McCabe Thiele. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute $N_{min}$ using Fenske equation:**

$$N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D} \cdot \dfrac{1-x_B}{x_B}\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}} = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{0.92}{0.08} \cdot \dfrac{0.98}{0.02}\right]}{\ln 3.5}$$

$$N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln(563.5)}{\ln 3.5} = \dfrac{6.333}{1.2528} = 5.06 \approx 5.1 \text{ stages}$$

**Step 2: Compute $R_{min}$:**

For $q = 1$, the q-line is vertical at $x = 0.30$. The intersection with the equilibrium curve:

$$y^* = \dfrac{3.5 \times 0.30}{1 + 2.5 \times 0.30} = \dfrac{1.05}{1.75} = 0.60$$

$$R_{min} = \dfrac{x_D - y_q^*}{y_q^* - x_q} = \dfrac{0.92 - 0.60}{0.60 - 0.30} = \dfrac{0.32}{0.30} = 1.067$$

**Step 3: Operating $R$:**

$$R = 1.4 \times 1.067 = 1.494 \approx 1.5$$

**Step 4: Operating line equations:**

Rectifying line:
$$y = \dfrac{1.5}{2.5}\,x + \dfrac{0.92}{2.5} = 0.600\,x + 0.368$$

Stripping line: passes through $(0.02, 0.02)$ and the intersection of the rectifying line with the q-line at $x = 0.30$:
$$y_q = 0.600(0.30) + 0.368 = 0.548$$
Slope of stripping line: $\dfrac{0.548 - 0.02}{0.30 - 0.02} = \dfrac{0.528}{0.28} = 1.886$

Stripping line: $y = 1.886\,x - 0.0177$

**Step 5: Stepping off stages (graphical or stepwise):**

Using the Kremser-type stage calculation:

Stage 1: $y_1 = x_D = 0.92$; $x_1 = y_1/\alpha/(1 + (\alpha-1)y_1/\alpha) = 0.92/3.5/(1 + 2.5 \times 0.92/3.5) = 0.2629/(1.657) = 0.1587$

(For rigorous stepping, use $x_1 = y_1/(\alpha - (\alpha-1) y_1) = 0.92/(3.5 - 2.5 \times 0.92) = 0.92/1.20 = 0.7667$... let me recompute)

Correct formula: $x = y/(\alpha - (\alpha-1)y)$:

$x_1 = 0.92/(3.5 - 2.5 \times 0.92) = 0.92/(3.5 - 2.3) = 0.92/1.20 = 0.7667$

Then $y_2 = 0.600(0.7667) + 0.368 = 0.460 + 0.368 = 0.828$

Continue similarly through $\approx 11$ theoretical stages (rectifying: 4, stripping: 7).

**Final answer:** $N_{theoretical} \approx 11$ stages, with feed at the 5th stage from the top. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Part (b) — Estimate Actual Trays, Column Diameter, and Pressure Drop. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Overall column efficiency (O'Connell):**

$$E_o = 0.492\,(\mu_L \alpha)^{-0.245} = 0.492\,(0.45 \times 3.5)^{-0.245} = 0.492\,(1.575)^{-0.245}$$

$$\ln(1.575) = 0.4543; \quad -0.245 \times 0.4543 = -0.1113; \quad e^{-0.1113} = 0.8947$$

$$E_o = 0.492 \times 0.8947 = 0.440 \text{ or } 44\%$$

**Step 2: Actual trays:**

$$N_{actual} = 11 / 0.440 = 25 \to 26 \text{ trays (round up)}$$

Actual feed plate: $5 / 0.440 \approx 11.4 \to 12$th from top.

**Step 3: Column diameter (Souders-Brown):**

Assume $V = 60$ kmol/h, $MW_{avg} = 28$ kg/kmol, $\rho_V = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\rho_L = 950$ kg/m³, $C_{SB} = 0.085$ m/s (TS = 0.61 m):

$$Q_V = \dfrac{60 \times 28}{1.2 \times 3600} = 0.389 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$u_{v,flood} = 0.085 \sqrt{\dfrac{950 - 1.2}{1.2}} = 0.085 \times 28.10 = 2.388 \text{ m/s}$$

Operating velocity (80% of flood):
$$u_v = 0.80 \times 2.388 = 1.910 \text{ m/s}$$

$$A_{net} = 0.389 / 1.910 = 0.2036 \text{ m}^2$$

Downcomers 12% of total: $A_c = 0.2036 / 0.88 = 0.2314 \text{ m}^2$

$$D_c = \sqrt{4 \times 0.2314/\pi} = 0.543 \text{ m} \to \text{round to 0.6 m (standard)}$$

**Step 4: Pressure drop per tray:**

Hole velocity: 9% open area, $A_h = 0.09 \times 0.2036 = 0.01832$ m², $u_0 = 0.389/0.01832 = 21.23$ m/s

Dry tray ($C_o = 0.75$):
$$h_d = 51 \times \dfrac{1.2}{950} \times \dfrac{21.23^2}{0.75^2} = 51 \times 0.001263 \times 800.4 = 51.6 \text{ mm liquid}$$

Weir height $h_w = 50$ mm, weir length $l_w = 0.4$ m, $Q_L = 0.0008$ m³/s:
$$h_{ow} = 750 \times \left[\dfrac{0.0008}{0.4}\right]^{2/3} = 750 \times 0.002^{2/3} = 750 \times 0.001587 = 1.19 \text{ mm}$$

$$h_l = 0.55 \times (50 + 1.19) = 28.2 \text{ mm}$$

Neglect $h_\sigma$ (small): $h_t = 51.6 + 28.2 = 79.8$ mm liquid per tray.

**Total column pressure drop:**
$$\Delta P_{col} = 79.8 \text{ mm} \times 26 \times \dfrac{950 \times 9.81}{1000} = 19.34 \text{ kPa} \quad ✓ \text{(acceptable)}$$

**[7 marks]**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative

**A binary distillation column with sieve trays is to be designed for the acetone–benzene system. Data:**

- $x_F = 0.50$, $x_D = 0.98$, $x_B = 0.01$
- Feed: 60 mol% liquid + 40 mol% vapor (two-phase feed)
- $\alpha_{avg} = 2.18$
- $R = 1.3\,R_{min}$

#### Part (a) — Construct the McCabe Thiele and Step Off Theoretical Stages. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: $N_{min}$ (Fenske):**
$$N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{0.98}{0.02} \cdot \dfrac{0.99}{0.01}\right]}{\ln 2.18} = \dfrac{\ln(4851)}{\ln 2.18} = \dfrac{8.487}{0.7793} = 10.89 \approx 11 \text{ stages}$$

**Step 2: Feed condition:** $q = 0.6$ (60% liquid).

**Step 3: $R_{min}$:**

q-line slope: $q/(q-1) = 0.6/(-0.4) = -1.5$
q-line intercept: $-z_F/(q-1) = -0.50/(-0.4) = 1.25$
q-line equation: $y = -1.5\,x + 1.25$

Intersection with equilibrium curve (numerical):
$1.25 - 1.5 x = 2.18 x / (1 + 1.18 x)$

Multiply out: $(1.25 - 1.5x)(1 + 1.18x) = 2.18x$
$1.25 + 1.475x - 1.5x - 1.77x^2 = 2.18x$
$1.25 - 0.025x - 1.77x^2 = 2.18x$
$1.77x^2 + 2.205x - 1.25 = 0$
$x = \dfrac{-2.205 + \sqrt{4.862 + 8.85}}{3.54} = \dfrac{-2.205 + 3.704}{3.54} = 0.4235$

$y_q = -1.5(0.4235) + 1.25 = 0.6148$

$$R_{min} = \dfrac{0.98 - 0.6148}{0.6148 - 0.4235} = \dfrac{0.3652}{0.1913} = 1.909$$

**Step 4: $R = 1.3 \times 1.909 = 2.482$**

**Step 5: Rectifying line:** $y = \dfrac{2.482}{3.482}x + \dfrac{0.98}{3.482} = 0.7128\,x + 0.2815$

**Step 6: Stripping line:** passes through $(0.01, 0.01)$ and $(0.4235, 0.6148)$:
slope $= 0.6048 / 0.4135 = 1.4627$
stripping line: $y = 1.4627\,x - 0.00463$

**Step 7: Stepping off:** Theoretical stages $\approx 17$ (rectifying 7, stripping 10). Feed at the 8th stage from the top. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Part (b) — Tray Design: Sieve Tray Layout and Pressure Drop Verification. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Given: $N_{actual} = 17/0.55 \approx 31$ trays (assuming $E_o \approx 55\%$).

**Step 1: Tray layout:**
- Tray spacing $TS = 0.61$ m
- Column $D_c = 1.2$ m (assume), $A_c = 1.131$ m²
- Net area (downcomers 12%): $A_{net} = 0.88 \times 1.131 = 0.995$ m²
- Weir length $l_w = 0.7 \times 1.2 = 0.84$ m
- Hole diameter $d_h = 5$ mm
- % open area = 10%, so $A_h = 0.0995$ m²

**Step 2: Vapor load (assume $V = 100$ kmol/h, $MW = 60$, $\rho_V = 3.0$ kg/m³):**

$$Q_V = \dfrac{100 \times 60}{3.0 \times 3600} = 0.556 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$u_0 = 0.556 / 0.0995 = 5.587 \text{ m/s (superficial hole velocity)}$$

**Step 3: Dry tray drop ($C_o = 0.73$):**
$$h_d = 51 \times \dfrac{3.0}{850} \times \dfrac{5.587^2}{0.73^2} = 51 \times 0.00353 \times 58.49 = 10.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Liquid head:**

$Q_L = 0.001$ m³/s; $h_w = 50$ mm
$$h_{ow} = 750 \times \left[\dfrac{0.001}{0.84}\right]^{2/3} = 750 \times 0.00119^{2/3} = 750 \times 0.00112 = 0.84 \text{ mm}$$
$$h_l = 0.55 \times (50 + 0.84) = 27.96 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Total tray drop:** $h_t = 10.5 + 27.96 + 0 = 38.5$ mm liquid/tray

**Step 6: Column ΔP:**
$$\Delta P_{col} = 38.5 \times 31 \times \dfrac{850 \times 9.81}{1000} = 9.96 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 7: Check limits:**
- Weeping velocity (Fair): $u_{0,min} \approx 3$ m/s. We have $u_0 = 5.587 > 3$ ✓
- Flooding: $u_v = 0.556/0.995 = 0.559$ m/s, well below flood. ✓
- Entrainment: low $\rho_V$ and $u_v$ → low entrainment ✓
- $h_t = 38.5$ mm is **lower** than the typical 50–100 mm; tray may weep at low turndown. Recommend increasing $h_w$ to 70 mm for stability.

**Recommendation:** Increase $h_w$ to 70 mm → $h_l = 0.55 \times 70.84 = 38.96$ mm, $h_t = 49.5$ mm ✓

**Final design:** $D_c = 1.2$ m, $N_{actual} = 31$, $h_t = 49.5$ mm liquid/tray, $\Delta P_{col} = 12.8$ kPa. **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $N_{actual} = N_{theor} / E_o$ step** — many students directly use the theoretical stages as actual. This costs 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Sign errors in $q$-line equation** — remember slope is $q/(q-1)$, NOT $(q-1)/q$. For $q = 1$, slope is infinite (vertical line).
> 3. **Forgetting the divisor on liquid density** in $h_d = 51 (\rho_V/\rho_L)(u_0^2/C_o^2)$. The 51 is dimensioned for **mm of liquid**, but you must still divide by $\rho_L$.
> 4. **Using operating $R$ in place of $R_{min}$** in Gilliland correlation. The Gilliland chart is a function of $(R - R_{min})/(R+1)$ vs $(N - N_{min})/(N+1)$.
> 5. **Ignoring the reboiler as a theoretical stage** in some KTU problems where the problem statement implies a partial reboiler. The reboiler acts as an additional equilibrium stage.
> 6. **Mixing up Murphree E_MV (vapor) and E_mL (liquid) versions** — they are equal **only** when molar latent heats are equal (CMO assumption).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **McCabe Thiele** = graphical stepping on $x$–$y$ diagram between **operating lines** and **equilibrium curve**.
- [ ] Five key elements: **45° diagonal**, **equilibrium curve**, **rectifying line**, **q-line**, **stripping line**.
- [ ] **Fenske equation** → $N_{min}$ at total reflux; uses $\alpha$ and product purities.
- [ ] **$R_{min}$** = intersection of q-line and equilibrium curve (or tangent for pinch).
- [ ] **Operating $R$** = typically 1.2 to 1.5 times $R_{min}$.
- [ ] **Feed stage** = the stage where the stepping crosses from rectifying line to stripping line.
- [ ] **Constant molar overflow** is the central assumption — verify molar latent heats are within ~10%.
- [ ] **Murphree point efficiency** $E_{OG}$ → at one tray location.
- [ ] **Murphree tray efficiency** $E_{MV} = (e^{\lambda E_{OG}} - 1)/\lambda$ with $\lambda = mV/L$.
- [ ] **Overall column efficiency** $E_o$ from O'Connell: $0.492 (\mu_L \alpha)^{-0.245}$.
- [ ] **Actual trays** $N_{actual} = N_{theor} / E_o$ (round up).
- [ ] **Souders-Brown** correlation: $u_{v,flood} = C_{SB} \sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_V)/\rho_V}$ → gives $D_c$.
- [ ] **Tray pressure drop** = $h_d + h_l + h_\sigma$ (all in mm liquid).
- [ ] **Dry tray drop** $h_d = 51 (\rho_V/\rho_L)(u_0/C_o)^2$.
- [ ] **Hydraulic head** $h_l = \beta(h_w + h_{ow})$.
- [ ] **Height over weir** $h_{ow} = 750 (Q_L / l_w)^{2/3}$ (Francis formula).
- [ ] **Column ΔP** = $h_t \times N_{actual} \times \rho_L g / 1000$ (in kPa).
- [ ] **Weeping check** — operating $u_0 \ge u_{0,min}$ (Fair).
- [ ] **Flooding check** — $u_v \le 0.85 u_{v,flood}$.
- [ ] **Entrainment** — typically acceptable below flooding.
- [ ] **Standard tray spacing** = 0.45 to 0.91 m; **typical weir height** = 40–90 mm.
- [ ] **Hole diameter** sieve trays = 3 to 12 mm; **% open area** = 8 to 15%.
- [ ] **Reboiler** = one theoretical stage (partial) — include in stage count unless stated otherwise.
- [ ] **Total condenser** = no equilibrium stage (vapor leaving is in equilibrium with liquid leaving reflux drum).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Entrainment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT601 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN II > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Entrainment ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Chemical Engineering Design II (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, **entrainment** is formally defined as the phenomenon in which liquid droplets, either from the bulk liquid phase on a tray or from the liquid film/dispersed phase, are physically carried upward by the rising vapor stream in a mass transfer or separation device (most notably, a **distillation/absorption column**, a **knock-out drum**, or a **cyclone separator**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize This):** > *Entrainment is the undesirable carryover of liquid as fine droplets by the vapor phase from one tray to the tray above, caused when the vapor velocity exceeds the droplet's terminal settling velocity. It directly reduces the Murphree tray efficiency, causes product contamination, and can lead to operational upsets such as priming, flooding, and foaming.* Mathematically, the **entrainment ratio (ψ)** is expressed as: $$\psi = \frac{L_e}{L_L + L_e}$$ Where: - $L_e$ = moles of liquid entrained per unit time (kmol/h or kg/h) - $L_L$ = moles of liquid flowing down the tray (downcomer liquid) **Standard Design Threshold (KTU 2024):** A common engineering rule of thumb is to limit the entrainment ratio $\psi$ to a maximum of **0.10** (i.e., 10%), although for high-pressure and high-purity columns the limit is often tightened to **0.05**. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bubbling Tea Kettle" Imagine you are boiling water in a kettle on a stove. As the water boils, you can see fine droplets of water being carried upward and out of the spout along with the steam. This is *entrainment* in everyday life. Now translate this to a chemical column: - The **kettle water** is the **liquid on the tray**. - The **steam escaping** is the **vapor rising to the next tray**. - The **droplets of water carried out** are the **entrained liquid**. If the "fire under the kettle" (vapor velocity) gets too strong, more droplets escape, and you "lose" liquid from the kettle, making your separation messy and inefficient. The same happens in a column — excessive entrainment washes liquid back up the column, destroying the carefully established concentration gradient. > [!NOTE] > **Why should a KTU student care?** > In the university exam, entrainment is a *favorite* Module 1 question because it directly links three major design sub-topics: (1) tray hydraulics, (2) column diameter, and (3) Murphree efficiency. Understanding entrainment unlocks 30–40% of Module 1 marks. ### 1.3 Types of Entrainment | Type | Description | When It Occurs | |---|---|---| | **Spray Entrainment** | Vapor is continuous phase, liquid is dispersed as fine droplets in a frothy regime. | High vapor rates, low liquid rates (spray regime operation) | | **Bubble/Froth Entrainment** | Vapor bubbles burst at the liquid surface ejecting fine droplets. | Normal operating regime (most tray columns) | | **Jet Entrainment** | Vapor jets through liquid holes at very high velocity, shearing liquid into droplets. | Near flooding, weeping recovery, or with damaged trays | ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** $9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - **Density of water (reference):** $1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - **Surface tension of water (reference):** $0.072 \text{ N/m}$ - **Standard air molar volume:** $22.414 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol}$ at STP - **Design entrainment limit:** $\psi_{max} = 0.10$ (general); $\psi_{max} = 0.05$ (high-purity) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mechanism of Entrainment — Step-by-Step Logic The formation of entrained liquid in a sieve-tray or bubble-cap column proceeds through four sequential steps: 1. **Vapor Generation:** Vapor rises through the perforations or risers of the tray below, creating a high-velocity jet. 2. **Liquid Shearing:** The high-velocity vapor jet impinges on the liquid pool, shearing the liquid into a fine dispersion of droplets (atomization). 3. **Droplet Ejection:** Droplets are thrown upward by the impulse of the jet and by the bursting of vapor bubbles at the froth interface. 4. **Counter-Current Balance:** A droplet is entrained *only if* the upward drag force of the vapor exceeds the downward gravitational settling force of the droplet. This is the **Stokes' Law regime** at low Reynolds numbers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Critical Question" of Entrainment:** > Will a droplet of diameter $d_p$ be carried upward, or will it fall back to its parent tray? > **Answer:** It is entrained if its terminal settling velocity $u_t$ is **less than** the superficial vapor velocity $u_v$. ### 2.2 Derivation of the Terminal Settling Velocity The terminal settling velocity of a spherical liquid droplet falling under gravity in a vapor medium is derived from a force balance: $$\text{Drag Force (downward resistance)} = \text{Gravitational Force (downward)} - \text{Buoyancy Force (upward)}$$ In **Stokes' Law regime** (laminar flow, $Re_p < 0.1$): $$u_t = \frac{g \cdot d_p^2 \cdot (\rho_L - \rho_V)}{18 \mu_V}$$ Where: - $u_t$ = terminal settling velocity (m/s) - $d_p$ = droplet diameter (m) - $\rho_L$ = liquid density (kg/m³) - $\rho_V$ = vapor density (kg/m³) - $\mu_V$ = vapor viscosity (Pa·s) - $g$ = $9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ > [!NOTE] > Notice the **quadratic dependence on $d_p$**: doubling the droplet size increases the settling velocity by a factor of 4. This is why the design of tray geometry (which controls the maximum stable droplet size) is so critical. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application / Boundary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Entrainment Ratio** | $\psi = L_e / (L_L + L_e)$ | Correlates to Murphree efficiency loss | | 2 | **Terminal Velocity (Stokes)** | $u_t = g d_p^2 (\rho_L - \rho_V) / 18\mu_V$ | $Re_p < 0.1$, laminar | | 3 | **Terminal Velocity (Newton)** | $u_t = \sqrt{3 g d_p (\rho_L - \rho_V) / \rho_V}$ | $Re_p > 1000$, turbulent | | 4 | **Souders-Brown Max Vapor Velocity** | $u_{V,max} = C_{SB} \sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_V)/\rho_V}$ | Used for column diameter design | | 5 | **Souders-Brown Coefficient** | $C_{SB} = (-0.171\, T^2 + 0.27\, T - 0.047)$ (for SI in m/s) | $T$ = tray spacing in meters (0.5 to 1.0 m) | | 6 | **Operating Vapor Velocity** | $u_V = (0.6 \text{ to } 0.85) \cdot u_{V,max}$ | 80% of flooding is the safe operating point | | 7 | **Flooding Velocity (Fair's Correlation)** | $u_{flood} = C_{F} \sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_V)/\rho_V}$ | $C_F$ read from Fair's chart | | 8 | **Murphree Efficiency Correction (Colburn)** | $E_{MV,actual} = E_{MV,dry} \cdot (1 - Z)$ | $Z = \psi / (L/V + \psi \cdot (1 - \psi)^{-1})$ | | 9 | **Overall Tray Efficiency (Lewis)** | $E_o = \sum (E_{MV,i} \cdot V_i \cdot y_i) / \sum (V_i \cdot y_i)$ | Required for number of real trays | | 10 | **Maximum Entrainment Flooding** | $\psi \to \infty$ | This defines the *flooding point* | > [!WARNING] > **KTU 2024 Mark-Saver:** > In formulas with **vertical bars** (e.g., the absolute value of a number), **never write `|x|` inside a table cell** — it breaks Markdown table syntax. Use the LaTeX command `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead. This is a common silent error in student answer sheets when they copy from PDFs. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The entrainment correlation is not just a textbook exercise — it is used in the **design of every distillation column in the hydrocarbon, petrochemical, and pharmaceutical industries**. For example: - **LNG Fractionation Trains:** Entrainment limits dictate the tray spacing; for cryogenic columns, $T = 0.5$ m is common due to low $\rho_L - \rho_V$ differences. - **Vacuum Distillation (Crude Oil):** High vapor volumes at low pressures make entrainment the **governing design constraint**; multi-pass trays and 0.6 m spacing are typical. - **Air Separation Units (ASU):** Foaming systems require modified Souders-Brown coefficients (0.6 × standard) to prevent argon-rich liquid carryover into the oxygen product. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Souders-Brown Equation The **Souders-Brown equation** is the single most important equation in KTU Module 1, and the university loves to ask students to derive it. Below is a step-by-step, fully explicit derivation. --- #### **Given / Assumed:** - A liquid droplet of diameter $d_p$ (m) is suspended in an upward-flowing vapor stream. - The vapor is assumed to behave as an ideal gas, and the droplet is spherical. - The droplet is at its **terminal settling velocity** $u_t$ (i.e., the drag force balances gravity minus buoyancy). #### **Step 1 — Force Balance on the Droplet** For a droplet in steady-state suspension, the **net force = 0**: $$F_{gravity} = F_{buoyancy} + F_{drag}$$ #### **Step 2 — Express Each Force** The mass of the droplet is: $$m = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \, \rho_L$$ Therefore: $$F_{gravity} = m \cdot g = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_L g$$ The buoyancy force equals the weight of the displaced vapor: $$F_{buoyancy} = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_V g$$ The drag force on a sphere in the Stokes regime ($Re_p < 0.1$): $$F_{drag} = 3 \pi \mu_V d_p u_t$$ > This is **Stokes' Drag Law**, valid for laminar flow around the sphere. The dimensionless drag coefficient $C_D$ is $24/Re_p$, and $F_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} C_D \rho_V u_t^2 A$, with $A = \frac{\pi d_p^2}{4}$. Substituting $C_D = 24/Re_p = 24 \mu_V / (\rho_V u_t d_p)$ recovers $F_{drag} = 3 \pi \mu_V d_p u_t$. #### **Step 3 — Substitute into the Force Balance** $$\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_L g = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_V g + 3 \pi \mu_V d_p u_t$$ #### **Step 4 — Simplify** Divide through by $\frac{\pi}{6} d_p$: $$\frac{1}{6} d_p^2 \rho_L g = \frac{1}{6} d_p^2 \rho_V g + \frac{18 \mu_V u_t}{1}$$ Wait — careful with arithmetic. Let us redo with $\frac{\pi}{6} d_p$ as the common factor on the left and $3\pi \mu_V d_p$ on the right: $$\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^2 \rho_L g - \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^2 \rho_V g = 3 \pi \mu_V u_t$$ $$\frac{\pi d_p^2}{6} (\rho_L - \rho_V) g = 3 \pi \mu_V u_t$$ Cancel $\pi$ from both sides: $$\frac{d_p^2}{6} (\rho_L - \rho_V) g = 3 \mu_V u_t$$ #### **Step 5 — Solve for $u_t$** $$u_t = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_L - \rho_V) g}{18 \mu_V}$$ This is the **Stokes terminal velocity** formula quoted in Section 2.2. #### **Step 6 — Connect to the Souders-Brown Concept** The vapor velocity that can suspend (just barely carry) the maximum-size stable droplet is **equal to the terminal velocity of that droplet**. Setting $u_V = u_t$ at the onset of significant entrainment: $$u_{V,max} = u_t = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_L - \rho_V) g}{18 \mu_V}$$ #### **Step 7 — Eliminate $d_p$ Using an Empirical Grouping** Souders and Brown observed that the term $\frac{d_p^2 g \rho_L}{18 \mu_V}$ is approximately constant for a given system, and they lumped it into an empirical coefficient $C_{SB}$ with units of m/s. They also observed that $u_{V,max}$ scales as $\sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_V)/\rho_V}$. Combining the two: $$u_{V,max} = C_{SB} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$$ This is the **Souders-Brown Equation** — *the most tested equation in KTU Module 1*. #### **Step 8 — Empirical Correlation for $C_{SB}$** For **sieve trays** with tray spacing $T$ in meters (valid for $0.5 \le T \le 1.0$ m): $$C_{SB} = -0.171\, T^2 + 0.27\, T - 0.047 \quad \text{(units: m/s)}$$ > **Numerical check (KTU typical problem):** $T = 0.6$ m > $C_{SB} = -0.171(0.36) + 0.27(0.6) - 0.047$ > $C_{SB} = -0.0616 + 0.162 - 0.047 = 0.0534 \text{ m/s}$ --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Variables | Operating Variable | Effect on Entrainment | Engineering Lever | |---|---|---| | **↑ Vapor velocity $u_V$** | ↑ Entrainment (quadratic) | Reduce column diameter or reflux | | **↑ Tray spacing $T$** | ↓ Entrainment | Increase column height (capital cost) | | **↑ Liquid density $\rho_L$** | ↓ Entrainment (denser droplets settle faster) | Choose colder operating temperature | | **↑ Vapor density $\rho_V$** | ↑ Entrainment (denser vapor carries more momentum) | Higher column pressure helps | | **↑ Surface tension** | ↓ Entrainment (droplets are larger) | Add antifoam in fouling systems | | **↑ Viscosity $\mu_V$** | ↑ Entrainment (Stokes regime) | Vacuum columns are critical | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Conceptual Shortcut (Board Examiner's Tip):** > For the same column, doubling the vapor rate increases entrainment by **~4×** (since $u_{V,max} \propto \sqrt{\rho_L - \rho_V}$ but $L_e \propto u_V^{3.5}$ to $u_V^{5.0}$). This is why the operating point is set at 80–85% of flooding velocity, not 95%. ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A distillation column has a tray spacing of 0.6 m. The liquid density is 870 kg/m³, and the vapor density is 4.2 kg/m³. Calculate the maximum allowable vapor velocity using the Souders-Brown correlation. **Solution:** Step 1 — Calculate $C_{SB}$ for $T = 0.6$ m: $$C_{SB} = -0.171(0.6)^2 + 0.27(0.6) - 0.047$$ $$C_{SB} = -0.0616 + 0.162 - 0.047 = 0.0534 \text{ m/s}$$ Step 2 — Apply Souders-Brown: $$u_{V,max} = 0.0534 \sqrt{\frac{870 - 4.2}{4.2}}$$ $$u_{V,max} = 0.0534 \sqrt{\frac{865.8}{4.2}} = 0.0534 \sqrt{206.14}$$ $$u_{V,max} = 0.0534 \times 14.358 = 0.767 \text{ m/s}$$ Step 3 — Design vapor velocity (typically 80% of max): $$u_{V,design} = 0.80 \times 0.767 = 0.614 \text{ m/s}$$ **Answer:** $u_{V,design} \approx 0.61 \text{ m/s}$ ✅ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Entrainment Mechanism in a Sieve Tray The following Mermaid flowchart maps the physical mechanism by which entrainment is generated on a single tray, showing the causal chain from vapor entry to droplet ejection to tray contamination. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step A: Vapor Inlet
from Tray Below] --> B[Step B: Vapor Jets
through Perforations] B --> C[Step C: Vapor Impinges
on Liquid Pool] C --> D[Step D: Atomization and
Froth Formation] D --> E[Step E: Bubble Burst at
Froth Interface] E --> F{Step F: Force Comparison} F -- Drag greater than Gravity --> G[Step G: Droplet is
ENTRAINED upward] F -- Gravity greater than Drag --> H[Step H: Droplet Falls
Back to Tray] G --> I[Step I: Liquid Carryover
to Tray Above] I --> J[Step J: Contamination and
Efficiency Loss] I --> K[Step K: Possible Flooding
if Severe] H --> L[Step L: Normal Operation
No Entrainment Loss] J --> M[Step M: Design Mitigation
Tray Spacing or Diameter] K --> M L --> N[Step N: System in
Steady State] M --> N ``` ### 4.2 System-Level Block Diagram: Entrainment Control Loop The following diagram illustrates how engineers design feedback into a column to keep entrainment within safe limits. It maps the closed-loop relationship between the design parameters (inputs), the entrainment response (process), and the control actions (outputs). ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input A
Tray Spacing T] --> B[Block B
Souders Brown Calc] C[Input C
Fluid Densities] --> B D[Input D
Vapor Rate] --> B B --> E{Block E
Is uV less than uVmax} E -- YES --> F[Output F
SAFE Operation
Entrainment less than 10%] E -- NO --> G[Output G
FLOODING RISK
High Entrainment] G --> H[Block H
Design Correction] H --> A H --> D F --> I[Block I
Steady Murphree Efficiency] ``` ### 4.3 Reference Schematic: Tray Cross-Section Showing Entrainment ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Tray_N_Plus_1 [Upper Tray N plus 1] N1[Liquid Pool on Upper Tray] end subgraph Tray_N [Operating Tray N] N2[Liquid Pool on Tray N] N3[Vapor Jets from Tray N minus 1] end subgraph Tray_N_Minus_1 [Lower Tray N minus 1] N4[Liquid Pool on Lower Tray] end N3 -->|High Velocity Vapor| N2 N2 -->|Entrained Droplets| N1 N2 -->|Settled Droplets| N4 N1 -->|Entrained Further| O[Top of Column] N4 -->|Recirculation| O ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] *Define entrainment. State two consequences of excessive entrainment in a distillation column.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (2 marks):** Entrainment is the carryover of liquid droplets from a tray by the rising vapor stream, occurring when the vapor velocity exceeds the terminal settling velocity of the droplets. - **Consequences (1 mark, any two):** 1. Reduction in Murphree tray efficiency (mixing of lighter and heavier liquid fractions). 2. Product contamination in distillate and bottoms. 3. Premature flooding of the column. 4. Increased downcomer loading. #### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] *What is the Souders-Brown equation? Write the empirical correlation for $C_{SB}$ in terms of tray spacing.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Equation (2 marks):** The Souders-Brown equation gives the maximum allowable superficial vapor velocity in a tray column: $$u_{V,max} = C_{SB} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$$ - **Empirical Correlation (1 mark):** $C_{SB} = -0.171\, T^2 + 0.27\, T - 0.047$, where $T$ is the tray spacing in meters (valid for 0.5 ≤ T ≤ 1.0 m). --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks).** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Derive the Souders-Brown equation for the maximum allowable vapor velocity in a tray column, starting from a force balance on a spherical liquid droplet suspended in a rising vapor stream. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A distillation column operating at 1.0 atm has the following data: - Liquid density: 950 kg/m³ - Vapor density: 1.20 kg/m³ - Tray spacing: 0.5 m - Vapor flow rate: 0.30 m³/s Calculate: (i) the maximum allowable vapor velocity, (ii) the column cross-sectional area, and (iii) the column diameter. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 Marks):** > The derivation is exactly as in Section 3.1 of these notes. The student must show: > - Force balance: $F_g = F_b + F_d$ (1 mark) > - Express each force in terms of $d_p$, $\rho_L$, $\rho_V$, $\mu_V$ (2 marks) > - Use Stokes' Law $F_d = 3\pi \mu_V d_p u_t$ (1 mark) > - Arrive at $u_t = d_p^2 (\rho_L - \rho_V) g / 18\mu_V$ (1 mark) > - Define $u_{V,max} = u_t$ and introduce $C_{SB}$ empirically (1 mark) > - Final equation $u_{V,max} = C_{SB} \sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_V)/\rho_V}$ (1 mark) **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **(i) Maximum allowable vapor velocity (3 marks):** $$C_{SB} = -0.171(0.5)^2 + 0.27(0.5) - 0.047$$ $$C_{SB} = -0.04275 + 0.135 - 0.047 = 0.04525 \text{ m/s}$$ $$u_{V,max} = 0.04525 \sqrt{\frac{950 - 1.20}{1.20}}$$ $$u_{V,max} = 0.04525 \sqrt{790.67} = 0.04525 \times 28.119 = 1.272 \text{ m/s}$$ **(ii) Column cross-sectional area (2 marks):** Using a design velocity of 80% of flooding (standard practice): $$u_{V,design} = 0.80 \times 1.272 = 1.018 \text{ m/s}$$ $$A = \frac{Q_V}{u_{V,design}} = \frac{0.30}{1.018} = 0.2947 \text{ m}^2$$ **(iii) Column diameter (2 marks):** $$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.2947}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{0.3752} = 0.6126 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{D \approx 0.61 \text{ m} \quad \text{(round up to 0.65 m as standard size)}}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks).** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Explain the mechanism of entrainment formation in a sieve-tray column with the help of a labeled sketch. Discuss how the terminal settling velocity of a droplet determines whether entrainment occurs. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A packed column is used to absorb ammonia from an air–ammonia mixture. The column uses 25-mm ceramic Raschig rings. Calculate the maximum allowable vapor velocity and the column diameter for the following operating data: - Gas (vapor) flow rate: 0.50 m³/s - Liquid density: 1000 kg/m³ - Gas density: 1.15 kg/m³ - Use the generalized pressure drop correlation with $\Delta P/Z = 500$ Pa/m. - $C_{SB}$ for this packing at 0.5 m tray spacing: 0.043 m/s. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Mechanism (7 Marks):** - The student must describe in writing the **4-step mechanism** (vapor generation, liquid shearing, droplet ejection, force balance) and explain the role of **terminal velocity**. - The deciding criterion: **droplet is entrained if** $u_V > u_t$. - Reference to **Stokes' regime** for small droplets is expected. - 4 marks for explanation + 2 marks for the decision criterion + 1 mark for a labeled sketch description. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **(i) Maximum allowable vapor velocity (3 marks):** $$u_{V,max} = C_{SB} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$$ $$u_{V,max} = 0.043 \sqrt{\frac{1000 - 1.15}{1.15}} = 0.043 \sqrt{868.5}$$ $$u_{V,max} = 0.043 \times 29.47 = 1.267 \text{ m/s}$$ **(ii) Design velocity (2 marks):** $$u_{V,design} = 0.80 \times 1.267 = 1.014 \text{ m/s}$$ **(iii) Cross-sectional area and diameter (2 marks):** $$A = \frac{Q_V}{u_{V,design}} = \frac{0.50}{1.014} = 0.493 \text{ m}^2$$ $$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.493}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.6277} = 0.792 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{D \approx 0.79 \text{ m} \quad \text{(standard size: 0.80 m)}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Writing $C_{SB}$ in m/s but plugging tray spacing in cm. Always verify units. > 2. **Forgetting the 80% rule (–1 to –2 marks):** Using $u_{V,max}$ directly as the design velocity. The operating point is 80% of flooding; the column is *never* designed at 100%. > 3. **Skipping assumptions (–1 to –3 marks):** Stokes' derivation is **only** valid for $Re_p < 0.1$ (laminar). For larger droplets, use Newton's regime. Always state the assumption. > 4. **No Murphree efficiency discussion (–1 mark):** Linking entrainment to tray efficiency using the **Colburn equation** $E_{MV,actual} = E_{MV,dry}(1 - Z)$ scores full conceptual credit. > 5. **Stating only one consequence:** Examiners expect at least 2–3 consequences of entrainment — flooding, efficiency loss, contamination, and increased reflux duty. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Print this list and revise 5 minutes before the exam.** - [ ] **Definition:** Entrainment = liquid droplets carried by vapor to the tray above. - [ ] **Decision criterion:** Entrainment occurs when $u_V > u_t$. - [ ] **Stokes terminal velocity:** $u_t = d_p^2 (\rho_L - \rho_V) g / (18 \mu_V)$ — quadratic in $d_p$. - [ ] **Newton terminal velocity:** $u_t = \sqrt{3 g d_p (\rho_L - \rho_V) / \rho_V}$ — for turbulent regime. - [ ] **Souders-Brown:** $u_{V,max} = C_{SB} \sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_V) / \rho_V}$ — the *master equation* of column sizing. - [ ] **$C_{SB}$ for sieve trays:** $-0.171 T^2 + 0.27 T - 0.047$ (with $T$ in meters). - [ ] **Design operating point:** $u_{V,design} = 0.80 \cdot u_{V,max}$ (or 85% for tight designs). - [ ] **Entrainment ratio $\psi$:** Typically limited to 0.10 (general) or 0.05 (high-purity). - [ ] **Effect on Murphree efficiency:** Colburn correction $E_{MV,actual} = E_{MV,dry}(1 - Z)$, where $Z$ is the entrainment function. - [ ] **Mitigation strategies:** Increase tray spacing, reduce vapor rate, use demister pads, lower column pressure, use larger packing. - [ ] **Flooding definition:** $\psi \to \infty$ — the point of operational failure. - [ ] **Vacuum columns are critical:** Low $\rho_V$ amplifies $u_{V,max}$ but also amplifies entrainment; tray spacing must be increased. - [ ] **High-pressure columns are forgiving:** High $\rho_V$ reduces $u_{V,max}$ required, so smaller columns are feasible. - [ ] **Unit safety:** Always check that $C_{SB}$ is in **m/s** and tray spacing in **m** for SI consistency. - [ ] **Most-tested sub-topics:** (1) Souders-Brown derivation, (2) numerical column diameter calculation, (3) consequence listing, (4) Murphree efficiency correction.

topics.title: Downflow Flooding and Weeping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT601 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Downflow Flooding and Weeping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition (KTU-Syllabus Standard Terminology)

In tray hydraulics of distillation columns, two critical abnormal operating limits are **Downflow Flooding** and **Weeping**. Both are operational boundaries that an engineer must calculate during the mechanical design of a sieve/valve tray column.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Downflow Flooding (Downcomer Flooding / Hydraulic Flooding):**
> It is the operating condition at which the **liquid downflow backup in the downcomer becomes excessive**, such that the clear liquid height in the downcomer approaches the tray spacing. Beyond this limit, liquid cannot exit the downcomer, the tray above floods, and the column can no longer maintain steady-state operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weeping:**
> It is the condition in which the **vapor velocity through the tray perforations (holes or valves) is insufficient to support the liquid on the tray deck**, causing liquid to leak downward through the perforations onto the tray below, instead of flowing cleanly into the downcomer.

## 1.2 Real-World Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a multi-storey building where each floor has a shower (the **tray**) that drains into a vertical pipe (the **downcomer**) carrying water down to the next floor.

- **Weeping** is like the shower drain leaking water *back through the showerhead holes* because the water pressure above is too low — the upward spray of water from below isn't strong enough to hold the water up. So water just dribbles down through every hole in the shower plate.
- **Downflow Flooding** is like the vertical drainpipe becoming so clogged with sudsy, foamy water that water backs up onto the floor above. The next shower cannot drain fast enough, so the bathroom floods.

In a real distillation column:
- **Vapor** rises up through the holes → pushes liquid up.
- **Liquid** falls down through the downcomer → carries product down.
- If vapor is too weak → liquid *weeps* through the holes (loss of mass transfer).
- If vapor is too strong → liquid cannot drain → *downcomer floods* (catastrophic shutdown).

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics Used

The following standard metrics are used in KTU board numericals:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Clear liquid height in downcomer | $h_{dc}$ | 50–70% of $T_S$ |
| Tray spacing | $T_S$ | **0.45 m, 0.6 m, 0.9 m** (standard) |
| Minimum vapor hole velocity to avoid weeping | $V_{om}$ | 0.3–1.5 m/s |
| Downcomer residence time | $\theta_d$ | **≥ 3 s** (Kister rule) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus (Module 1: Tray Hydraulics) specifically demands derivation/calculation of downflow flooding velocity using **Fair's correlation** and weeping velocity using **Eduljee's correlation**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Downflow Flooding

Downflow flooding is governed by the **liquid residence time in the downcomer**. When vapor throughput rises, the following sequence occurs:

1. Vapor velocity through holes increases.
2. The **aerated liquid density** $\rho_{a}$ inside the downcomer rises because more vapor is entrained.
3. The clear liquid backup height $h_{dc}$ increases (because $h_{dc} \propto \rho_a$).
4. When $h_{dc} \to T_S + h_w$ (tray spacing plus weir height), the downcomer can no longer drain.
5. Liquid accumulates on the tray above → flooding.

The **aerated liquid density** is related to vapor void fraction $\phi$ by:
$$\rho_a = \rho_L (1 - \phi)$$

The **Clear Liquid Height in Downcomer** (the KTU master equation) is:
$$h_{dc} = h_t + h_{ow} + h_{da} + h_{db}$$

Where each term has a specific hydraulic meaning (explained in §3).

## 2.2 Mechanism of Weeping

Weeping is a **low-vapor-velocity** failure mode.

1. Vapor enters through perforations at velocity $V_o$.
2. The drag force of vapor on the liquid above each hole *balances* the liquid head.
3. If $V_o < V_{om}$ (minimum vapor velocity), drag force < liquid weight, and liquid *drains* through the hole.
4. The result: liquid bypasses proper contact on the tray, **Murphree efficiency collapses**, and vapor–liquid equilibrium is not approached.

> [!WARNING]
> **Why this matters:** At weep point, tray efficiency can drop from ~70–80% to as low as **20–30%**, even though the column still "operates." Many students wrongly treat weeping as a minor issue.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table must be memorized for KTU ESE — these are the highest-yield equations in Module 1.**

| # | Quantity | Equation | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flooding velocity (Fair) | $V_f = K_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$ | $V_f$ in ft/s; $K_1$ from Souders-Brown correlation chart |
| 2 | Operating velocity (Kister) | $V_{\text{op}} = \alpha \cdot V_f$ | $\alpha = 0.75$–$0.85$ typical (design factor) |
| 3 | Min. vapor hole velocity (Eduljee, weep) | $V_{om} = K_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L}{\rho_V} \cdot h_{ct}}$ | $h_{ct}$ = clear liquid height on tray (m) |
| 4 | Clear liq. backup in downcomer | $h_{dc} = h_t + h_{ow} + h_{da} + h_{db}$ | All in **mm of liquid** |
| 5 | Dry-tray pressure drop | $h_t = 0.4 F_o^2 \, \dfrac{\rho_V}{\rho_L}$ | $F_o = V_o \sqrt{\rho_V}$ is the F-factor |
| 6 | Aerated liquid density | $\rho_a = \rho_L (1 - \phi)$ | $\phi$ from Solari–Bell correlation |
| 7 | Hydraulic gradient | $\Delta h_c = \dfrac{1.6 \cdot F_o^{1.2} \cdot L^{0.5}}{C_d^{1.6} \cdot \rho_L}$ | Used for large-diameter sieve trays |
| 8 | Downcomer residence time | $\theta_d = \dfrac{A_d \cdot H_{dc}}{Q_L}$ | $\theta_d \geq 3$ s mandatory |

> [!NOTE]
> **Always convert ft/s to m/s** in KTU problems: $1 \text{ ft/s} = 0.3048 \text{ m/s}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery crude distillation units** — operators must operate at 75–85% of flood to allow for surges during feed composition swings.
- **Air separation columns** (cryogenic) — operate very close to weeping limit during turndown; weep-induced efficiency loss is a major issue.
- **Design safety margin**: Industrial columns are designed at **$\approx 80\%$ of flood velocity**, ensuring turndown flexibility and avoiding downcomer flood.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Fair's Flooding Correlation (Downflow)

### Starting Principle (Force Balance on Liquid in Downcomer)

Consider a control volume of aerated liquid of height $h_a$ inside the downcomer. The pressure at the bottom of the downcomer (entering the tray deck) must equal the tray pressure (assuming negligible gas-phase ΔP for short downcomer).

The **clear liquid equivalent height** is:
$$h_{dc} = \rho_a \cdot h_a / \rho_L = (1 - \phi) h_a$$

By mechanical energy balance across the downcomer:
$$h_{dc} = h_t + h_{ow} + h_{da} + h_{db}$$

Now substitute each component:

**Dry Tray Pressure Drop ($h_t$):**
From orifice equation for vapor through holes:
$$\Delta P_t = \dfrac{\rho_V V_o^2}{2 C_o^2}$$

Converting to clear-liquid height:
$$h_t = \dfrac{\Delta P_t}{\rho_L \cdot g} = \dfrac{\rho_V V_o^2}{2 C_o^2 \rho_L \cdot g} = \dfrac{0.4 F_o^2 \rho_V}{\rho_L}$$

(in standard KTU notation with $g$ absorbed into the constant 0.4)

**Weir Crest ($h_{ow}$):**
For a straight segmental weir, Francis weir formula (SI units):
$$h_{ow} = 0.00284 \left(\dfrac{Q_L}{l_w}\right)^{2/3}$$

**Froth Height Loss ($h_{da}$):**
$$h_{da} = 0.5 \cdot h_a = \dfrac{0.5 \cdot h_{dc}}{1 - \phi}$$

**Back-Up into Downcomer ($h_{db}$):**
$$h_{db} = \rho_a \cdot h_a / \rho_L$$

Substituting iteratively gives the **iterative Fair flooding solution**.

### Final Form of Fair's Flooding Correlation (Operating Form)

Souders-Brown / Fair flooding parameter:
$$C_{SB} = V_f \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_V}{\rho_L - \rho_V}}$$

$$\boxed{C_{SB} = K_1 \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{20}\right)^{0.2} \left(\dfrac{A_h}{A_a}\right)^{0.5} \left(\dfrac{T_S}{0.305}\right)^{0.5}}$$

Where $K_1$ is read from Fair's flooding chart (typically 0.10–0.12 ft/s for sieve trays).

## 3.2 Derivation of Eduljee's Weep Correlation

### Force Balance on Liquid Above a Single Hole

A liquid droplet of effective thickness $h_{ct}$ sits above a hole. Vapor exerts drag; gravity acts downward.

**Vapor drag force per hole:**
$$F_{drag} = C_d \cdot A_{hole} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_V V_o^2}{2}$$

**Gravity force holding liquid up:**
$$F_{gravity} = A_{hole} \cdot h_{ct} \cdot \rho_L \cdot g$$

At **incipient weeping**, drag = gravity:
$$C_d \cdot A_{hole} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_V V_o^2}{2} = A_{hole} \cdot h_{ct} \cdot \rho_L \cdot g$$

Canceling $A_{hole}$ and rearranging:
$$\dfrac{V_{om}^2 \cdot \rho_V}{2 C_d} = h_{ct} \cdot \rho_L \cdot g$$

$$V_{om} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 C_d \cdot g \cdot \rho_L \cdot h_{ct}}{\rho_V}}$$

In KTU-syllabus form, absorbing constants ($2 C_d g$ → $K_2^2$):
$$\boxed{V_{om} = K_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L \cdot h_{ct}}{\rho_V}}}$$

Where:
- $K_2 = 0.85$ (sieve tray, Eduljee empirical constant, SI units)
- $h_{ct}$ = clear liquid height on tray (m)
- $\rho_L, \rho_V$ in **kg/m³**

## 3.3 Worked KTU-Style Numerical (Complete Step-by-Step)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 2024 board style):**
A sieve tray column has tray spacing $T_S = 0.6$ m, hole area $A_h = 0.10$ m², active area $A_a = 0.80$ m², weir length $l_w = 1.2$ m, weir height $h_w = 50$ mm, vapor density $\rho_V = 2.5$ kg/m³, liquid density $\rho_L = 950$ kg/m³, surface tension $\sigma = 25$ dyn/cm, vapor flow rate $Q_V = 0.50$ m³/s, liquid flow rate $Q_L = 0.005$ m³/s. Determine:
(a) The flooding velocity $V_f$ using Fair's correlation.
(b) The minimum vapor hole velocity $V_{om}$ to prevent weeping, using Eduljee's correlation.

### Solution

**Part (a): Flooding Velocity (Fair)**

Step 1 — Compute flow parameter (dimensionless):
$$F_{LV} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_V} \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_V}{\rho_L}}$$

$$F_{LV} = \dfrac{0.005}{0.50} \sqrt{\dfrac{2.5}{950}} = 0.01 \times 0.0513 = 5.13 \times 10^{-4}$$

Step 2 — Apply surface tension correction factor:
$$\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{20}\right)^{0.2} = \left(\dfrac{25}{20}\right)^{0.2} = (1.25)^{0.2} \approx 1.0456$$

Step 3 — Apply area ratio factor:
$$\left(\dfrac{A_h}{A_a}\right)^{0.5} = \left(\dfrac{0.10}{0.80}\right)^{0.5} = \sqrt{0.125} = 0.3536$$

Step 4 — Apply tray spacing factor:
$$\left(\dfrac{T_S}{0.305}\right)^{0.5} = \left(\dfrac{0.6}{0.305}\right)^{0.5} = \sqrt{1.967} = 1.4025$$

Step 5 — Read $K_1$ from Fair's flooding chart at $F_{LV} = 5.13 \times 10^{-4}$:
$$K_1 \approx 0.107 \text{ ft/s} = 0.0326 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 6 — Compute Souders-Brown flooding parameter:
$$C_{SB} = 0.0326 \times 1.0456 \times 0.3536 \times 1.4025 = 0.01691 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 7 — Solve for flooding velocity:
$$V_f = C_{SB} \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$$

$$V_f = 0.01691 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{950 - 2.5}{2.5}} = 0.01691 \times \sqrt{379} = 0.01691 \times 19.47$$

$$\boxed{V_f = 0.329 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Part (b): Minimum Vapor Velocity to Avoid Weeping (Eduljee)**

Step 1 — Compute clear liquid height on tray $h_{ct}$:
Weir crest:
$$h_{ow} = 0.00284 \left(\dfrac{Q_L}{l_w}\right)^{2/3} = 0.00284 \left(\dfrac{0.005}{1.2}\right)^{2/3}$$

$$= 0.00284 \times (4.167 \times 10^{-3})^{2/3} = 0.00284 \times 0.0259 = 7.36 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$

Clear liquid height on tray (aerated factor $\beta = 0.6$ typical):
$$h_{ct} = \beta(h_w + h_{ow}) = 0.6 \times (0.05 + 0.0000736)$$

$$h_{ct} = 0.6 \times 0.05007 = 0.03004 \text{ m}$$

Step 2 — Apply Eduljee's correlation ($K_2 = 0.85$):
$$V_{om} = 0.85 \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L \cdot h_{ct}}{\rho_V}}$$

$$V_{om} = 0.85 \sqrt{\dfrac{950 \times 0.03004}{2.5}} = 0.85 \sqrt{11.415}$$

$$V_{om} = 0.85 \times 3.378 = \boxed{2.871 \text{ m/s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Check:** Operating velocity should be **between** $V_{om}$ and $V_f$. The actual operating $V_o = Q_V / A_h = 0.50 / 0.10 = 5.0$ m/s — which exceeds $V_f$ → flooding. Design column diameter must be increased.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Tray Hydraulics Failure Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operating Vapor Velocity Vo] --> B{Vo versus V_om}
    A --> C{Vo versus V_f}

    B -->|Vo less than V_om| D[LiLEAKAGE]
    D --> D1[Liquid leaks through holes]
    D1 --> D2[Efficiency collapse]
    D2 --> D3[Liquid bypasses active area]
    D3 --> D4[Murphree efficiency drops 70 percent to 25 percent]

    B -->|Vo greater than V_om| E[STABLE OPERATION BAND]

    E --> C
    C -->|Vo greater than V_f| F[DOWNFLOW FLOODING]
    F --> F1[Aerated liquid backup in downcomer]
    F1 --> F2[Clear liquid height approaches tray spacing]
    F2 --> F3[Tray above floods]
    F3 --> F4[Column shutdown required]

    C -->|Vo less than V_f| G[DESIGN SAFE ZONE]
    G --> G1[Operating velocity between 0.75 Vf and 0.85 Vf]
    G1 --> G2[Downcomer residence time greater than 3 s]
    G2 --> G3[Normal tray efficiency 70 to 85 percent]

    style D fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#fff4e5,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Architecture Flow — Downcomer Pressure Balance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[Vapor Inlet] --> DT[Dry Tray Pressure Drop ht]
    DT --> WC[Weir Crest how]
    WC --> FL[Froth Loss in Downcomer hda]
    FL --> BU[Backup Head hdb]

    DT -.->|Calculated from Fo| HT[ht eq 0.4 Fo squared rhoV over rhoL]
    WC -.->|Francis formula| HOW[how eq 0.00284 times QL over lw to power 2 over 3]
    FL -.->|Aerated density correlation| HDA[hda eq 0.5 times ha]
    BU -.->|RHOA equals RHOL 1 minus phi| HDB[hdb eq rhoa ha over rhoL]

    HT --> SUM[Clear Liquid Height hdc]
    HOW --> SUM
    HDA --> SUM
    HDB --> SUM

    SUM --> CHECK{hdc less than TS plus hw}
    CHECK -->|TRUE| SAFE[DESIGN ACCEPTABLE]
    CHECK -->|FALSE| FLOOD[DOWNFLOW FLOODING CONDITION]

    style FLOOD fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style SAFE fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`
**Differentiate between downflow flooding and jet flooding in a tray column.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Downflow Flooding | Jet Flooding |
|---|---|---|
| **Cause** | Excessive liquid backup in downcomer | Excessive vapor momentum carrying liquid upward |
| **Location** | Inside downcomer | Above tray (spray regime) |
| **Criterion** | $h_{dc} \to T_S + h_w$ | Vapor velocity > entrainment flooding velocity |
| **Appearance** | Liquid level rises in downcomer | Entrainment of liquid to tray above |

*(1 mark for cause, 1 mark for location, 1 mark for criterion — KTU board pattern)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Define weeping. What is the consequence of weeping on tray efficiency?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Weeping is the leakage of liquid through the perforations of a tray at low vapor velocities when the vapor drag force is insufficient to support the liquid head on the tray.
- **Consequence (1.5 marks):** It causes liquid bypass through tray perforations, leading to inadequate vapor–liquid contact. **Murphree efficiency** $E_{MV}$ drops dramatically (often below 30%), and the column fails to achieve design separation despite operating without mechanical shutdown.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Downflow Flooding Focus

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of downflow flooding in a sieve tray column. Derive the expression for clear liquid height in the downcomer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tray column has the following specifications: $\rho_V = 3.0$ kg/m³, $\rho_L = 900$ kg/m³, $T_S = 0.6$ m, $A_h / A_a = 0.10$, $\sigma = 20$ dyn/cm. Using Fair's correlation with $K_1 = 0.108$ ft/s, compute the flooding velocity $V_f$. If operating velocity is **0.80 $V_f$**, determine whether the design is safe. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Mechanism + Derivation (7 marks):**

**Mechanism (3 marks):**
1. Vapor rising through tray perforations entrains into the downcomer liquid.
2. The downcomer contents become aerated (vapor-liquid mixture, density $\rho_a < \rho_L$).
3. The clear liquid equivalent height $h_{dc}$ increases with vapor rate.
4. When $h_{dc} \to T_S + h_w$, the downcomer can no longer discharge liquid onto the tray deck below.
