5. Liquid accumulates on the tray above → flooding.

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Pressure balance on downcomer contents:

$$h_{dc} = h_t + h_{ow} + h_{da} + h_{db}$$

Substituting each:

$$h_{dc} = \underbrace{\dfrac{0.4 F_o^2 \rho_V}{\rho_L}}_{h_t} + \underbrace{0.00284\left(\dfrac{Q_L}{l_w}\right)^{2/3}}_{h_{ow}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{0.5 \, h_a \rho_a}{\rho_L}}_{h_{da}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{h_a \rho_a}{\rho_L}}_{h_{db}}$$

With $h_{dc} = h_a \rho_a / \rho_L$ → solving for $h_a$ and $h_{dc}$ iteratively gives the **complete Fair's solution**.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Convert $K_1$:
$$K_1 = 0.108 \text{ ft/s} \times 0.3048 = 0.03292 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 2 — Surface tension factor:
$$\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{20}\right)^{0.2} = \left(\dfrac{20}{20}\right)^{0.2} = 1.0$$

Step 3 — Area ratio factor:
$$\left(\dfrac{0.10}{1}\right)^{0.5} = 0.3162$$

Step 4 — Tray spacing factor:
$$\left(\dfrac{0.6}{0.305}\right)^{0.5} = \sqrt{1.967} = 1.4025$$

Step 5 — Souders-Brown flooding parameter:
$$C_{SB} = 0.03292 \times 1.0 \times 0.3162 \times 1.4025 = 0.01459 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 6 — Flooding velocity:
$$V_f = C_{SB} \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}} = 0.01459 \sqrt{\dfrac{897}{3.0}} = 0.01459 \times 17.29$$

$$\boxed{V_f = 0.2523 \text{ m/s}}$$

Step 7 — Operating velocity:
$$V_{\text{op}} = 0.80 \times 0.2523 = 0.2018 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 8 — Safety check: $V_{\text{op}} < V_f$ → **SAFE** (operates within 80% of flood). ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Weeping Focus

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the mechanism of weeping and the effect on tray efficiency. Derive Eduljee's correlation for minimum vapor velocity to prevent weeping. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sieve tray has $h_{ct} = 30$ mm, $\rho_L = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_V = 2.0$ kg/m³. Using Eduljee's correlation ($K_2 = 0.85$), calculate the minimum vapor velocity through the holes to avoid weeping. If the actual operating velocity is 4 m/s, what is the design margin? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Mechanism + Derivation (7 marks):**

**Mechanism (3 marks):**
Weeping occurs at low vapor throughput. At the incipient weep point, the **vapor drag force through the hole equals the gravity force of the liquid column above the hole**.

**Consequences (to be discussed briefly):** Liquid bypasses the tray; tray efficiency drops below 30%; column cannot achieve product specs.

**Derivation (4 marks):**
At incipient weep:
$$F_{\text{drag}} = F_{\text{gravity}}$$
$$\dfrac{C_d \rho_V V_{om}^2}{2} A_{hole} = h_{ct} \rho_L g A_{hole}$$
$$V_{om}^2 = \dfrac{2 C_d g \rho_L h_{ct}}{\rho_V}$$

Letting $K_2^2 = 2 C_d g$:
$$\boxed{V_{om} = K_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L h_{ct}}{\rho_V}}}$$

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Substitute values:
$$V_{om} = 0.85 \sqrt{\dfrac{1000 \times 0.030}{2.0}} = 0.85 \sqrt{15}$$

Step 2 — Evaluate:
$$V_{om} = 0.85 \times 3.873 = \boxed{3.292 \text{ m/s}}$$

Step 3 — Design margin:
$$\text{Margin} = \dfrac{V_{\text{op}} - V_{om}}{V_{om}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{4.0 - 3.292}{3.292} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Margin} = 21.5\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design verdict:** Margin > 15% → **safe from weeping** during normal operation, with adequate turndown flexibility.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Downflow Flooding & Weeping Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch on $K_1$:** Fair's correlation chart gives $K_1$ in **ft/s** — students often forget to convert to m/s, leading to a 5–7 mark loss in 14-mark problems.
>
> 2. **Wrong tray spacing factor formula:** Some students use $(T_S/0.305)^{0.5}$ without the 0.305 factor — always include the standard 0.305 m (1 ft) reference.
>
> 3. **Confusing $V_f$ with $V_{om}$:** $V_f$ is the **maximum** allowable velocity (downflow flooding limit); $V_{om}$ is the **minimum** allowable velocity (weeping limit). Operating velocity must satisfy: $V_{om} < V_{\text{op}} < V_f$.
>
> 4. **Eduljee's $K_2$:** The constant $K_2 = 0.85$ is empirical — students using $K_2 = 1.0$ (incorrect shortcut) lose 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting $\beta$ (aerated fraction):** When computing $h_{ct}$, always apply $\beta \approx 0.6$ to $(h_w + h_{ow})$ — otherwise the result is non-conservative.
>
> 6. **Mermaid issue:** When drawing tray diagrams in the answer script, label each part (weir, downcomer, perforations) — examiners specifically look for **neat labeled sketches** and award 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Downflow flooding** is caused by **liquid backup in the downcomer**, governed by Fair's correlation.
- [ ] **Weeping** is caused by **low vapor velocity through holes**, governed by Eduljee's correlation.
- [ ] Operating velocity $V_{\text{op}}$ **must satisfy**: $V_{om} < V_{\text{op}} < V_f$.
- [ ] Typical design operates at **80% of $V_f$** for safety margin.
- [ ] Fair's $K_1$ chart value is in **ft/s** — convert before substitution.
- [ ] Eduljee's $K_2 = 0.85$ for sieve trays in SI units.
- [ ] Clear liquid downcomer height $h_{dc} = h_t + h_{ow} + h_{da} + h_{db}$.
- [ ] Downcomer residence time $\theta_d \geq 3$ s is mandatory.
- [ ] Weeping reduces Murphree efficiency from ~75% to ~25%.
- [ ] Surface tension correction term is $\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{20}\right)^{0.2}$ in Fair's correlation.
- [ ] Tray spacing factor is $\left(\dfrac{T_S}{0.305}\right)^{0.5}$ (reference = 1 ft = 0.305 m).
- [ ] Aerated liquid density $\rho_a = \rho_L(1-\phi)$; $\phi$ increases with vapor rate.
- [ ] Always check **both** flooding **and** weeping limits before finalizing design.
- [ ] KTU 2024 board frequently asks for a **comparison table** between the two failure modes — memorize the differentiation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Design of Steady State Isothermal Packed Bed Absorption and Stripping Column for Dilute Systems Without Chemical Reaction: Number of Transfer Units, Height of Transfer Units, Column Diameter, Packing Height, Liquid Distribution, Pressure Drop
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT601 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Design of Steady State Isothermal Packed Bed Absorption and Stripping Column for Dilute Systems Without Chemical Reaction: Number of Transfer Units, Height of Transfer Units, Column Diameter, Packing Height, Liquid Distribution, Pressure Drop

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

A **Steady-State Isothermal Packed Bed Absorption Column** is a vertical, cylindrical pressure vessel filled with random or structured packing (e.g., Raschig rings, Pall rings, Berl saddles, or structured gauze) used to transfer a soluble component (the **solute**) from a **gas/vapour phase** into a non-volatile **liquid solvent** through physical mass transfer — **without any chemical reaction** between the phases. The system is considered *dilute* when the mole fraction of the solute in both phases is low (typically $y_A \le 0.05$ in gas and $x_A \le 0.05$ in liquid), permitting simplifications such as constant molar flow rates, constant slip velocity, and linearised equilibrium relationships.

A **Steady-State Isothermal Packed Bed Stripping Column** is the mathematical twin: it transfers the volatile solute from the **liquid phase** into a stripping gas/vapour by reversing the direction of mass transfer. The governing differential equations, HTU-NTU framework, and design correlations are identical in form, with only the boundary conditions and the *direction* of flux reversed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 1, PCCHT601):** Design must be carried out using the **HTU-NTU Concept** (also called the **Kremser equations / Two-Film driven approach**), where the **Height of a Transfer Unit (HTU)** is the *kinetic* part and the **Number of Transfer Units (NTU)** is the *thermodynamic* part. The product gives the **Packing Height (Z)**. **The Column Diameter (D)** is determined independently from flooding correlations (Sherwood, Eckert/GPDC, or Kister). **Liquid Distribution** ensures uniform wetting, and **Pressure Drop** confirms hydraulic operability.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wet Sponge Tower"

Imagine a tall vertical glass tube filled with marbles (the packing). Now blow a polluted airstream containing ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$) from the **bottom** upwards, and trickle a stream of clean water from the **top** downwards. As the dirty air curls around the wet marbles, the ammonia — a gas that loves water — *dissolves* into the thin water film clinging to each marble. By the time the air exits at the top, it is sweet and clean. The water that drips out at the bottom is now "ammonia-loaded" and can be sent to a stripping tower where hot air blows it out again.

In this analogy:
- The **marbles** = packing (provides huge interfacial area).
- The **thin water film on each marble** = the *liquid phase* mass transfer resistance.
- The **air boundary layer around the marble** = the *gas phase* mass transfer resistance.
- The **total height of marbles** = the *Packing Height $Z$* required to reach the desired purity.
- The **diameter of the tube** = the *Column Diameter $D$* required to handle the gas flow without flooding (i.e., without the water "choking" the gas and flooding the column).
- The **rain-shower head at the top** = the *Liquid Distributor* (ensures every square centimetre of cross-section gets equal water).
- The **pressure that builds at the bottom** = the *Pressure Drop* $\Delta P$ that the blower must overcome.

### 1.3 Fundamental Governing Assumptions (For Dilute Systems)

For the KTU design problem, the following simplifying assumptions are standard:

1. **Isothermal operation** ($T = \text{constant}$ along the height).
2. **Dilute solute** ($y_A$, $x_A \le 0.05$): molar flow rates $G$ and $L$ are essentially constant.
3. **No chemical reaction** between the solute and the solvent.
4. **Constant pressure** along the column (small pressure drop relative to total).
5. **Ideal gas behaviour** for the gas phase.
6. **Henry's Law equilibrium** (or linear portion of the equilibrium curve): $y_A = m x_A$, where $m$ = Henry's constant (dimensionless).
7. **Steady-state**: no accumulation, $\partial/\partial t = 0$.
8. **Plug flow** of both phases (no axial mixing / dispersion).
9. **Uniform liquid distribution** (idealised distributor).

> [!NOTE]
> The **Henry's Law constant $m$** for a dilute system is defined on a mole-fraction basis as $y_A = m x_A$, where $m$ is dimensionless. If $m < 1$, the gas is more soluble (favours absorption); if $m > 1$, the gas is less soluble. The ratio $L/(mG)$ is called the **absorption factor $A$**, and its inverse $mG/L$ is the **stripping factor $S$**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Always in Bold)

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Standard molar volume (at STP):** $22.414 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol}$
- **Henry's constant $m$** (dimensionless, mole-fraction basis)
- **Mass transfer coefficients:** $k_G$ (kmol/m²·s·Pa), $k_L$ (m/s), or $K_G$, $K_L$ (overall)
- **Interfacial area per unit volume:** $a$ (m²/m³)
- **Volumetric flow rates:** $G$ (kmol/s) gas, $L$ (kmol/s) liquid

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two-Film Theory — Foundation of Mass Transfer in Packed Beds

The **Whitman Two-Film Theory (1923)** postulates that a thin, stagnant film exists on each side of the gas–liquid interface, and the entire resistance to mass transfer is concentrated in these two films. At the interface itself, the two phases are in equilibrium.

**Local Fluxes (per unit interfacial area):**

$$N_A = k_G \left( y_A - y_{A,i} \right) = k_L \left( x_{A,i} - x_A \right)$$

For a **dilute system** with linear equilibrium $y^* = m x$, the **overall coefficients** are related to the individual film coefficients by:

$$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L} \qquad ; \qquad \frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{m \, k_G} + \frac{1}{k_L}$$

**Volumetric Mass Transfer Coefficients (the working form for packed columns):**

$$K_G a, \; k_G a, \; k_L a, \; K_L a \quad \left[ \frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{s}} \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> In packed columns, we never measure $k_G$ or $k_L$ alone — we use the **volumetric** form $k_G a$ and $k_L a$ because the true interfacial area $a$ is hard to know. The product is measured experimentally and correlated against operating conditions (e.g., Onda's correlations).

## 2.2 Operating Line and Equilibrium Line — The $y$–$x$ Diagram

For a counter-current packed column (gas enters at the **bottom**, liquid enters at the **top**), the material balance on the solute over the entire column gives:

$$G \, y_{A,1} + L \, x_{A,2} = G \, y_{A,2} + L \, x_{A,1}$$

**Operating Line (mass balance over the differential section from top to any point):**

$$y = \left( \frac{L}{G} \right) x + \left( y_{A,1} - \frac{L}{G} \, x_{A,1} \right)$$

**Equilibrium Line (Henry's law, dilute):**

$$y^* = m x$$

The **Operating Line Slope** is $L/G$, and the **Equilibrium Line Slope** is $m$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absorption Rule:** For the gas to be absorbed (transferred from gas to liquid), the operating line must lie **above** the equilibrium line. This requires $L/G > m$, i.e., **Absorption Factor $A = L/(mG) > 1$**.
>
> **Stripping Rule:** For the solute to be stripped (transferred from liquid to gas), the operating line must lie **below** the equilibrium line. This requires $G/L > 1/m$, i.e., **Stripping Factor $S = mG/L > 1$**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Operating line slope | $L / G$ | dimensionless |
| Equilibrium line slope | $m$ | dimensionless, Henry's law |
| Absorption factor | $A = L / (mG)$ | $A > 1$ for absorption |
| Stripping factor | $S = mG / L$ | $S > 1$ for stripping |
| **NTU (gas phase basis)** | $NTU_G = \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \dfrac{dy}{y - y^*}$ | dimensionless |
| **NTU (liquid phase basis)** | $NTU_L = \int_{x_2}^{x_1} \dfrac{dx}{x^* - x}$ | dimensionless |
| **HTU (gas phase)** | $HTU_G = \dfrac{G}{K_G a \, (1 - y)_M}$ | metres |
| **HTU (liquid phase)** | $HTU_L = \dfrac{L}{K_L a \, (1 - x)_M}$ | metres |
| **Packing Height** | $Z = HTU_G \cdot NTU_G = HTU_L \cdot NTU_L$ | metres |
| **Slip correction factor** | $(1 - y)_M$ or $(1 - x)_M$ | accounts for dilution |
| **Kremser absorption** | $\dfrac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2} = A^{N+1} - \dfrac{A - 1}{A - 1/A^N}$ | when $A = 1$ (use limit) |
| **Kremser stripping** | $\dfrac{x_1 - y_2/m}{x_2 - y_2/m} = S^{N+1} - \dfrac{S - 1}{S - 1/S^N}$ | $N$ = number of stages equivalent |
| **Flooding velocity (Eckert/GPDC abscissa)** | $\dfrac{L}{G} \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_G}{\rho_L - \rho_G}}$ | dimensionless $X$-axis |
| **Flooding velocity (Eckert ordinate)** | $\dfrac{G^2 \, C_f \, \mu_L^{0.2}}{\rho_G (\rho_L - \rho_G) g}$ | dimensionless $Y$-axis |
| **Operating pressure drop** | $\Delta P / Z$ | mm H₂O/m or Pa/m |
| **Column cross-section** | $A_c = \dfrac{G_{vol}}{u_{op}}$ | m² |
| **Column diameter** | $D = \sqrt{4 A_c / \pi}$ | metres |
| **Liquid load** | $\ell = L_{vol} / A_c$ | m³/(m²·h) |
| **Gas load (F-factor)** | $F = G_{vol} \sqrt{\rho_G}$ | Pa$^{0.5}$ or m/s·(kg/m³)$^{0.5}$ |
| **Onda $k_L a$ correlation** | $k_L \left( \dfrac{\rho_L}{\mu_L g} \right)^{1/3} = 0.0051 \left( \dfrac{L_{mass}}{a_w \mu_L} \right)^{2/3} \left( \dfrac{\mu_L}{\rho_L D_L} \right)^{-0.5} (a_p d_p)^{0.4}$ | empirical |
| **Onda $k_G a$ correlation** | $\dfrac{k_G}{a_p D_G} = C \left( \dfrac{G_{mass}}{a_p \mu_G} \right)^{0.7} \left( \dfrac{\mu_G}{\rho_G D_G} \right)^{1/3} (a_p d_p)^{-2.0}$ | $C = 5.23$ for $\text{d} > 0.012$ m, $C = 2.00$ otherwise |
| **Wetted area $a_w$ (Onda)** | $a_w = a_p \left[ 1 - \exp \left\{ -1.45 \left( \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\sigma} \right)^{0.75} \left( \dfrac{L_{mass}}{a_p \mu_L} \right)^{0.1} \left( \dfrac{L_{mass}^2 a_p}{\rho_L^2 g \right)^{0.2} \right\}$ | 0.05 \right] \right]$ | m²/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subscript convention used in KTU textbooks:**
> - **Subscript 1** = *rich* end of gas = **bottom** of absorber, **top** of stripper.
> - **Subscript 2** = *lean* end of gas = **top** of absorber, **bottom** of stripper.
> - For **liquid**: subscript 1 is at the **top** of the absorber (rich liquid leaving) and subscript 2 is at the **bottom** (lean liquid entering).

## 2.4 Why HTU–NTU? (The Engineering Rationale)

The **Number of Transfer Units (NTU)** captures *how difficult the separation is* (a thermodynamic, equilibrium-driven quantity). It depends on the inlet/outlet compositions and the equilibrium line — *not* on the packing type.

The **Height of a Transfer Unit (HTU)** captures *how efficiently the packing performs* (a kinetic, transport-driven quantity). It depends on the mass transfer coefficients, the packing geometry, the flow rates, and the physical properties — *not* on the desired compositions.

Multiplying them gives the **Packing Height $Z$**, which is the total height of tower required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two forms (HTU_G and HTU_L)?** Either basis gives the same $Z$ mathematically, but in practice one basis may be easier to evaluate. If the **gas-phase resistance is dominant** (e.g., absorption of a poorly soluble gas in water), the **gas-phase** HTU-NTU is more accurate. Conversely, if the **liquid-phase resistance** is dominant (e.g., absorption of $\text{CO}_2$ in water), use the **liquid-phase** form.

## 2.5 The Flooding, Loading, and Operating Regimes

In a packed column, three hydraulic regimes are observed as gas velocity increases at fixed liquid rate:

1. **Below loading:** Liquid trickles down, gas flows up. Low pressure drop.
2. **Loading:** Liquid begins to accumulate on the packing; pressure drop rises sharply.
3. **Flooding:** Liquid cannot flow down — the column is "flooded" and cannot operate.

The column is always designed to operate at **60–80% of the flooding velocity** to ensure a safety margin and stable operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** The **Generalised Pressure Drop Correlation (GPDC) of Eckert** (also called the **Sherwood–Eckert–Levy chart**) is the most commonly used flooding correlation. The abscissa is the **Flow Parameter** $FP = (L/G)\sqrt{\rho_G/(\rho_L - \rho_G)}$ and the ordinate is a modified capacity parameter. The chart gives the **flooding gas velocity $G_f$** for a chosen packing. The design velocity is taken as $G_{\text{op}} = f_f \cdot G_f$, where $f_f$ is the **flooding fraction** (typically $0.6$–$0.8$).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Operating Line

Consider a counter-current packed absorption column. The gas flows **upward** and the liquid flows **downward**. Let us define an arbitrary horizontal section at height $z$ from the top. Above this section, the gas has composition $y$ and the liquid has composition $x_{A,2}$ (entering at the top).

**Component mass balance on the solute A (steady state, no reaction):**

$$G \, y + L \, x_{A,2} = G \, y_{A,2} + L \, x_{A,1}$$

Rearranging for $y$ as a function of $x$:

$$y = \left( \frac{L}{G} \right) x + \underbrace{\left( y_{A,2} + \frac{L}{G} x_{A,1} - \frac{L}{G} x_{A,2} \right)}_{\text{constant}}$$

For a packed column, since $G$ and $L$ are essentially constant (dilute system), the operating line is a **straight line** of slope $L/G$ on the $y$–$x$ diagram, passing through the points $(x_{A,1}, y_{A,1})$ at the bottom and $(x_{A,2}, y_{A,2})$ at the top.

## 3.2 Derivation of the NTU–HTU Equation (Gas-Phase Basis)

Take a differential height $dz$ of packing. Within this slice, the gas composition changes by $dy$ and the liquid composition changes by $dx$.

**Mass balance over $dz$ (solute A):**

The rate of solute transferred from gas to liquid across the differential packing volume $A_c \, dz$ (where $A_c$ is the cross-sectional area) is given by the **overall flux times the interfacial area per volume times the volume**:

$$G \, dy = K_G \, a \, (y - y^*) \, A_c \, dz \quad \text{[for } y - y^* \text{ driving force]}$$

Wait — careful with signs. Gas is losing A as it goes up, so if $z$ is measured **upward from bottom**, $dy/dz < 0$. The correct form is:

$$-G \, dy = K_G \, a \, (y - y^*) \, A_c \, dz$$

But the **height** $Z$ is measured between two ends, and we conventionally express the integral such that the limits are properly ordered. Taking $z$ measured from the **bottom** of the column, the gas composition *decreases* upward, so:

$$-G \, dy = K_G \, a \, (y - y^*) \, A_c \, dz$$

Separating variables:

$$dz = -\frac{G}{K_G \, a \, A_c} \cdot \frac{dy}{y - y^*}$$

Integrating from $z = 0$ (bottom, $y = y_1$) to $z = Z$ (top, $y = y_2$):

$$Z = \int_{0}^{Z} dz = -\frac{G}{K_G \, a \, A_c} \int_{y_1}^{y_2} \frac{dy}{y - y^*}$$

By the **mean value theorem** for molar flow rates in a dilute system, the molar flow $G$ is essentially constant, and we can write $G / A_c = G_S$ (the **superficial molar flux** of gas, kmol/m²·s).

**Defining the HTU and NTU:**

$$\boxed{HTU_G \;\equiv\; \frac{G_S}{K_G \, a} \quad \text{(metres)}}$$

$$\boxed{NTU_G \;\equiv\; \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y - y^*} \quad \text{(dimensionless)}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{Z = HTU_G \times NTU_G}$$

The same logic on the liquid phase gives:

$$\boxed{Z = HTU_L \times NTU_L, \quad HTU_L = \frac{L_S}{K_L \, a}, \quad NTU_L = \int_{x_2}^{x_1} \frac{dx}{x^* - x}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **driving force in the gas-phase integral** uses the difference $y - y^*$, where $y^*$ is the gas composition in equilibrium with the *liquid* at that point. The **driving force in the liquid-phase integral** uses $x^* - x$, where $x^*$ is the liquid composition in equilibrium with the *gas* at that point. The two integrals are mathematically equivalent.

## 3.3 Closed-Form NTU Solution for a Linear Equilibrium Line

For a **dilute system** with **linear equilibrium** $y^* = m x$ and **linear operating line** of slope $L/G$ (constant $G$ and $L$), the integration can be done analytically. Let us define:

$$\lambda = \frac{m G}{L} = \frac{1}{A} = S \quad \text{(stripping factor)}$$

For an **absorber**, integrating the gas-phase form:

$$NTU_G = \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y - m \left( \frac{y - y_{A,2}}{L/G} + x_{A,2} \right)}$$

After substituting the operating-line relation $x = (y - y_2 - (L/G) x_2) / (L/G) + x_2 = (G/L) y + (x_1 - (G/L) y_1)$ and simplifying, one obtains:

$$NTU_G = \frac{A}{A - 1} \ln \left[ \left( \frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2} \right) \left( \frac{1 - 1/A}{1 - y_2/(A x_2)} \right) \right]$$

For a **dilute system** (where $1 - y$ and $1 - x$ corrections are negligible), this reduces to the cleaner form:

$$\boxed{NTU_G = \frac{A}{A - 1} \ln \left[ \left( \frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2} \right) \left( 1 - \frac{1}{A} \right) \right] \quad (A \ne 1)}$$

For the special case $A = 1$ (i.e., $L = mG$, operating line parallel to equilibrium), the limit yields:

$$NTU_G = \frac{y_1 - y_2}{y_2 - m x_2} \quad (A = 1)$$

**Equivalent form for a stripper** (using $S = 1/A$):

$$\boxed{NTU_L = \frac{S}{S - 1} \ln \left[ \left( \frac{x_1 - y_2 / m}{x_2 - y_2 / m} \right) \left( 1 - \frac{1}{S} \right) \right] \quad (S \ne 1)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When the equilibrium is non-linear (concentrated system or curved equilibrium), the integral **cannot be done in closed form**. Use either **graphical integration** (plot $1/(y-y^*)$ vs $y$ and find the area) or **numerical integration** (Simpson's rule, trapezoidal rule). For dilute systems with Henry's law, always use the **closed-form expression above**.

## 3.4 Relation Between HTU Values

A useful identity relating the gas- and liquid-phase HTU values:

$$HTU_G \cdot (1 - y)_M = HTU_L \cdot (1 - x)_M \cdot m \cdot \frac{L}{G \cdot m} = HTU_L \cdot (1 - x)_M \cdot A$$

For a dilute system, $(1 - y)_M \approx (1 - x)_M \approx 1$, and so:

$$HTU_G \approx HTU_L \cdot A \quad \text{(dilute approximation)}$$

## 3.5 Column Diameter — Flooding-Based Sizing

The column diameter is determined by hydraulic considerations, not mass transfer. We proceed in these steps:

**Step 1: Calculate the Flow Parameter (Eckert abscissa).**

$$X = \frac{L'}{G'} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_G}{\rho_L - \rho_G}}$$

where $L'$ and $G'$ are the **mass flow rates** (kg/s) of liquid and gas, and $\rho_L$, $\rho_G$ are the densities (kg/m³).

**Step 2: Read the flooding ordinate $Y_f$ from the GPDC chart** (or compute it using the Strigle equation — see below).

**Step 3: Solve for the flooding gas mass velocity $G'_f$:**

$$Y_f = \frac{(G'_f)^2 \, C_f \, \mu_L^{0.2}}{\rho_G (\rho_L - \rho_G) g}$$

$$G'_f = \sqrt{ \frac{Y_f \cdot \rho_G (\rho_L - \rho_G) g}{C_f \, \mu_L^{0.2}} }$$

where $C_f$ is the **packing factor** (m⁻¹), $\mu_L$ is the liquid viscosity in **cP**, and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Step 4: Choose a design gas mass velocity.**

$$G'_{\text{op}} = f_f \cdot G'_f$$

Typically $f_f = 0.6$ to $0.8$ (flooding fraction). A common KTU value is $0.7$.

**Step 5: Calculate the cross-sectional area and diameter.**

$$A_c = \frac{G'_{\text{op,total}}}{G'_{\text{op}}} \quad \text{[m}^2\text{]} \qquad ; \qquad D = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_c}{\pi}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **packing factor $C_f$** is an empirical property of the packing. Typical values (in m⁻¹):
> - 1-inch (25 mm) Raschig rings: $C_f = 525$
> - 1-inch Pall rings: $C_f = 180$
> - 1-inch Berl saddles: $C_f = 200$
> - 2-inch (50 mm) Pall rings: $C_f = 92$
> - 2-inch Raschig rings: $C_f = 245$
> - 50 mm Intalox saddles: $C_f = 130$
> - 25 mm Intalox saddles: $C_f = 320$

## 3.6 Pressure Drop — Empirical Correlations

The most widely used **Generalised Pressure Drop Correlation (GPDC)** of Eckert is read as a chart. For computational use, the **Strigle modification (1987)** or the **Kister (1992) equation** is used:

**Kister / Robbins Equation (Strigle modification):**

$$\Delta P = \frac{0.192 \, (G'_S)^{1.9} \, (L'_S)^{0.1} \, \mu_L^{0.15} \, (1 - \epsilon)^{2.2}}{\epsilon^{4.2} \, d_{eq}^{1.1} \, \rho_G^{0.9}} \quad \text{[per metre of packing]}$$

where:
- $G'_S$ = superficial gas mass velocity, kg/(m²·s)
- $L'_S$ = superficial liquid mass velocity, kg/(m²·s)
- $\mu_L$ = liquid viscosity, cP
- $\epsilon$ = void fraction of packing (e.g., 0.74 for 1-inch Pall rings)
- $d_{eq}$ = equivalent packing diameter, m

The total pressure drop is then $\Delta P_{\text{total}} = (\Delta P / Z) \times Z$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** A typical operating pressure drop for a packed column is in the range **15–50 mm H₂O per metre of packing** (~150–500 Pa/m). The total column pressure drop should not exceed **10% of the column operating pressure**.

## 3.7 Packing Height — Final Design Equation

Combining the HTU and NTU:

$$\boxed{Z = HTU_G \cdot NTU_G \quad \text{or} \quad Z = HTU_L \cdot NTU_L}$$

**Complete Design Sequence (Absorber):**

1. **Define specifications:** $y_1, y_2, x_1, x_2, T, P, G, L$, physical properties.
2. **Select a packing type and size** based on corrosion, capacity, and efficiency considerations.
3. **Check the equilibrium and operating lines** ($L/G > m$ for absorption).
4. **Compute NTU** using the closed-form expression or graphical integration.
5. **Compute HTU** using the Onda, Bravo-Fair, or Cornell correlations, or vendor data.
6. **Compute the Packing Height:** $Z = HTU \cdot NTU$. Round up to the next standard shell height (typically 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0 m sections).
7. **Compute the flooding gas velocity** using the GPDC / Eckert chart.
8. **Choose a flooding fraction** (60–80%) and compute the **Column Diameter**.
9. **Check the Pressure Drop** using the Kister equation.
10. **Specify the Liquid Distributor** (≥30 drip points per m² for Pall rings, ≥40 for Raschig rings).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define "Height of a Transfer Unit" (HTU) and "Number of Transfer Units" (NTU). State the relationship between them and the packing height. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **NTU (Number of Transfer Units):** A dimensionless quantity that measures the *difficulty of separation* (thermodynamic component). It is the integral of the driving-force term along the column:
$$NTU_G = \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y - y^*}$$

- **HTU (Height of a Transfer Unit):** A length that measures the *efficiency of packing* (kinetic component). It depends on mass transfer coefficients, flow rates, and physical properties:
$$HTU_G = \frac{G_S}{K_G \, a}$$

- **Relation to Packing Height:** $Z = HTU \times NTU$ (1 mark for this final statement).

### Question 2. What is the flooding velocity in a packed column? Why is the operating velocity typically chosen as 60–80% of the flooding velocity? `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Flooding velocity** is the maximum superficial gas velocity at which the column can operate before the liquid is held up by the gas and cannot flow downward. It is determined from the **Generalised Pressure Drop Correlation (GPDC) of Eckert** using the Flow Parameter and packing factor.
- The operating velocity is chosen at **60–80%** of flooding to:
  1. Provide a safety margin against operational upsets.
  2. Ensure stable liquid distribution.
  3. Keep pressure drop low and predictable.
  4. Avoid the onset of loading (incipient flooding).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Q1]`

A gas mixture containing **5 mol% SO₂** in air is to be scrubbed with **pure water** in a counter-current packed absorption column. The desired exit gas contains **0.1 mol% SO₂**. The **liquid-to-gas molar flow ratio** is $L/G = 60$, the **Henry's constant** is $m = 30$ (at the operating temperature, expressed on a mole-fraction basis), and the **gas molar flow** is $G = 0.1$ kmol/s. The packing used is **1-inch Pall rings**, with $K_G a = 0.005$ kmol/(m³·s·Δy). Assume dilute system, isothermal operation, and pure liquid inlet.

**(a)** Calculate the **Number of Transfer Units ($NTU_G$)** and the **Packing Height ($Z$)** using the gas-phase HTU–NTU approach. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **Column Diameter** and check the **Pressure Drop** using the Eckert GPDC chart, given that the gas density is $\rho_G = 1.2$ kg/m³, the liquid density is $\rho_L = 1000$ kg/m³, the liquid viscosity is $\mu_L = 1.0$ cP, the packing factor is $C_f = 180$ m⁻¹, and the flooding fraction is $f_f = 0.7$. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Step 1: Identify the inlet and outlet compositions.**

- $y_1 = 0.05$ (rich gas, bottom)
- $y_2 = 0.001$ (lean gas, top)
- $x_2 = 0$ (pure water, top)
- $x_1$ = to be determined (or use operating line)

**Step 2: Compute the Absorption Factor $A$.**

$$A = \frac{L}{mG} = \frac{60}{30} = 2.0$$

Since $A = 2.0 > 1$, the operating line lies above the equilibrium line, confirming valid absorption operation.

**Step 3: Apply the closed-form NTU equation.**

For $A \ne 1$ and a dilute system with $x_2 = 0$:

$$NTU_G = \frac{A}{A - 1} \ln \left[ \left( \frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2} \right) \left( 1 - \frac{1}{A} \right) \right]$$

Substituting $y_1 = 0.05$, $y_2 = 0.001$, $x_2 = 0$, $A = 2$:

$$\frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2} = \frac{0.05 - 0}{0.001 - 0} = 50$$

$$1 - \frac{1}{A} = 1 - \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

$$NTU_G = \frac{2}{2 - 1} \ln \left[ 50 \times 0.5 \right] = 2 \cdot \ln(25)$$

$$\ln(25) = \ln(5^2) = 2 \ln(5) = 2 \times 1.6094 = 3.2189$$

$$\boxed{NTU_G = 2 \times 3.2189 = 6.438}$$

**Step 4: Compute the HTU.**

$$HTU_G = \frac{G_S}{K_G a} = \frac{G / A_c}{K_G a}$$

We need the cross-sectional area first, but for the **HTU per unit flux** form, we use the molar flux. Assume an operating gas velocity of 1.0 m/s (will be verified in part b).

$$G_S = \frac{G}{A_c} = \rho_{G,\text{mol}} \cdot u_S = \frac{1.2}{29} \times 1.0 = 0.0414 \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$

$$HTU_G = \frac{0.0414}{0.005} = 8.28 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Compute the Packing Height.**

$$\boxed{Z = HTU_G \times NTU_G = 8.28 \times 6.438 = 53.3 \text{ m}}$$

This is a very tall column. In practice, the dilute assumption may need a small correction $(1-y)_M$, and the HTU would be re-evaluated with the correct gas velocity from part (b).

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1: Calculate the Flow Parameter (Eckert abscissa).**

Convert molar flows to mass flows.

Gas mass flow: $G' = G \times M_{air,avg} = 0.1 \times 29 = 2.9$ kg/s (since SO₂ is dilute, $M \approx 29$).

Liquid mass flow: $L' = G \times (L/G) \times M_{water} = 0.1 \times 60 \times 18 = 108$ kg/s.

$$X = \frac{L'}{G'} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_G}{\rho_L - \rho_G}} = \frac{108}{2.9} \sqrt{\frac{1.2}{1000 - 1.2}}$$

$$X = 37.24 \times \sqrt{0.001201} = 37.24 \times 0.03465 = 1.290$$

**Step 2: Read the flooding ordinate $Y_f$ from the GPDC chart.**

For 1-inch Pall rings and $X = 1.29$, the flooding ordinate is approximately $Y_f \approx 0.022$ (this value is read from the chart; a typical reading for this range is $0.020$–$0.025$).

**Step 3: Solve for the flooding gas mass velocity $G'_f$.**

$$Y_f = \frac{(G'_f)^2 \, C_f \, \mu_L^{0.2}}{\rho_G (\rho_L - \rho_G) g}$$

$$(G'_f)^2 = \frac{Y_f \cdot \rho_G (\rho_L - \rho_G) g}{C_f \, \mu_L^{0.2}}$$

$$(G'_f)^2 = \frac{0.022 \times 1.2 \times 998.8 \times 9.81}{180 \times 1.0^{0.2}} = \frac{258.7}{180} = 1.437$$

$$G'_f = \sqrt{1.437} = 1.199 \text{ kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$

**Step 4: Apply the flooding fraction.**

$$G'_{\text{op}} = 0.7 \times 1.199 = 0.839 \text{ kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$

**Step 5: Compute the cross-sectional area and diameter.**

$$A_c = \frac{G'_{\text{total}}}{G'_{\text{op}}} = \frac{2.9}{0.839} = 3.457 \text{ m}^2$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 3.457}{\pi}} = \sqrt{4.402} = 2.10 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Verify the operating gas velocity.**

$$u_S = \frac{G'_{\text{op}}}{\rho_G} = \frac{0.839}{1.2} = 0.699 \text{ m/s}$$

(Compare to our assumed 1.0 m/s — re-run HTU calculation if precision is needed. With $u_S = 0.699$ m/s, $G_S = 0.0289$ kmol/(m²·s), $HTU_G = 5.78$ m, $Z = 5.78 \times 6.44 = 37.2$ m.)

**Step 7: Check the pressure drop.**

Use the Kister equation. For 1-inch Pall rings, void fraction $\epsilon \approx 0.74$, equivalent diameter $d_{eq} \approx 0.025$ m.

$$G'_S = 0.839 \text{ kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}, \quad L'_S = \frac{L'}{A_c} = \frac{108}{3.457} = 31.24 \text{ kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$

$$\Delta P / Z = \frac{0.192 \times (0.839)^{1.9} \times (31.24)^{0.1} \times (1.0)^{0.15} \times (0.26)^{2.2}}{(0.74)^{4.2} \times (0.025)^{1.1} \times (1.2)^{0.9}}$$

Computing term by term:
- $(0.839)^{1.9} = 0.713$
- $(31.24)^{0.1} = 1.413$
- $(0.26)^{2.2} = 0.0538$
- $(0.74)^{4.2} = 0.273$
- $(0.025)^{1.1} = 0.0189$
- $(1.2)^{0.9} = 1.181$

Numerator: $0.192 \times 0.713 \times 1.413 \times 1.0 \times 0.0538 = 0.01036$

Denominator: $0.273 \times 0.0189 \times 1.181 = 0.00609$

$$\Delta P / Z = \frac{0.01036}{0.00609} = 1.70 \text{ Pa/m}$$

For 37.2 m height: $\Delta P_{\text{total}} = 1.70 \times 37.2 = 63.2$ Pa ≈ **6.4 mm H₂O**.

This is **very low** (below the typical 15–50 mm H₂O/m range), which means the column is operating far below flooding — we could safely increase the gas velocity to reduce $D$. The designer should iterate.

---

### Question B. (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2, Q2]`

A **packed stripping column** is to be designed to remove **acetone** from wastewater. The feed liquid contains **0.05 mole fraction acetone** and must be stripped to **0.005 mole fraction**. Steam (inert to acetone) is used as the stripping gas. The **gas-to-liquid molar flow ratio** is $G/L = 1.5$, and the **Henry's constant** is $m = 0.5$ at the operating temperature. Assume dilute system, linear equilibrium, and acetone-free steam.

**(a)** Calculate the **Number of Transfer Units ($NTU_L$)** and the **Packing Height ($Z$)** given that $K_L a = 0.02$ kmol/(m³·s·Δx) and the operating liquid molar flux is $L_S = 0.05$ kmol/(m²·s). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the **Liquid Distributor** for a column diameter of 1.5 m and recommend a suitable **Pressure Drop** specification. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Step 1: Identify compositions and compute the Stripping Factor $S$.**

- $x_1 = 0.05$ (rich liquid, top of stripper)
- $x_2 = 0.005$ (lean liquid, bottom)
- $y_2 = 0$ (acetone-free steam, bottom)
- $S = \frac{mG}{L} = \frac{0.5 \times 1.5}{1} = 0.75$

Wait — $S = 0.75 < 1$. This means the operating line is **above** the equilibrium line, which is a violation of the stripping condition. The designer must **increase $G/L$** so that $S > 1$. We re-specify: $G/L = 2.5$ gives $S = 0.5 \times 2.5 = 1.25 > 1$. ✓

**Step 2: Apply the closed-form NTU equation for a stripper.**

With $y_2 = 0$ (pure stripping gas), $S = 1.25$:

$$NTU_L = \frac{S}{S - 1} \ln \left[ \left( \frac{x_1 - y_2/m}{x_2 - y_2/m} \right) \left( 1 - \frac{1}{S} \right) \right]$$

$$\frac{x_1 - y_2/m}{x_2 - y_2/m} = \frac{0.05}{0.005} = 10$$

$$1 - \frac{1}{S} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.25} = 0.2$$

$$NTU_L = \frac{1.25}{0.25} \ln [ 10 \times 0.2 ] = 5 \times \ln(2) = 5 \times 0.6931 = 3.466$$

$$\boxed{NTU_L = 3.466}$$

**Step 3: Compute the HTU.**

$$HTU_L = \frac{L_S}{K_L a} = \frac{0.05}{0.02} = 2.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Packing Height.**

$$\boxed{Z = HTU_L \times NTU_L = 2.5 \times 3.466 = 8.67 \text{ m}}$$

Round up to **9.0 m** of packing (typically 4–5 packed sections of 1.5–2.0 m each).

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1: Liquid Distributor Selection.**

For a 1.5 m diameter column:
- Cross-sectional area: $A_c = \pi (0.75)^2 = 1.767$ m².
- For 1-inch Pall rings, the KTU / Kister recommendation is **30–40 drip points per m²** of tower cross-section.
- Total drip points: $N_{\text{drip}} = 1.767 \times 35 = 62$ (using a mid-range 35 points/m²).
- Distributor type: **Notched-trough gravity distributor** with 6 radial troughs, each with ~10 V-notches; or a **spray nozzle distributor** (less preferred for fouling services).
- Liquid irrigation density: $\ell = L_S / (\rho_L / M_L) = 0.05 \times 18 / 1000 = 9.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/(m²·s) × check.

Re-derive more carefully: $L_S = 0.05$ kmol/(m²·s) × 18 kg/kmol = 0.9 kg/(m²·s) of water. Volumetric = $0.9 / 1000 = 9 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/(m²·s) = $3.24$ m³/(m²·h). This is in the recommended range of 2–10 m³/(m²·h) ✓.

**Step 2: Pressure Drop Specification.**

Target pressure drop = **25–40 mm H₂O per metre of packing** (~250–400 Pa/m), a typical safe operating value.

For a 9.0 m packing height: $\Delta P_{\text{total}} = 30 \times 9 = 270$ mm H₂O = **2.65 kPa**.

If the column operates near atmospheric pressure (~101 kPa), this represents ~2.6% — well within the **10% rule** of thumb.

The pressure drop specification is verified by the GPDC chart at the design point. The Kister equation (from Question A) would be applied here similarly to confirm.

**Recommended Distributor Type:** **Notched-Trough** (preferred for fouling/wastewater service) with 6 troughs, 62 notches, each notch 12–15 mm wide.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Subscript confusion (–3 marks):** Always remember — **subscript 1** = *rich* end of gas = **bottom** of absorber. **Subscript 2** = *lean* end of gas = **top** of absorber. In a **stripper**, it is reversed.
> 2. **Wrong form of NTU equation (–4 marks):** For **absorption**, use the gas-phase integral $NTU_G$. For **stripping**, use the liquid-phase integral $NTU_L$. They give the same $Z$ but the equations look different.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor $(1 - 1/A)$ in the Kremser form (–2 marks):** Students often write $\ln[(y_1-mx_2)/(y_2-mx_2)]$ only and miss the multiplicative $(1 - 1/A)$. This gives a wrong NTU.
> 4. **Using molar flow $G$ instead of molar flux $G_S$ in HTU (–2 marks):** $HTU$ requires the **superficial flux** ($G/A_c$), not the total molar flow. Mixing units is a common error.
> 5. **Not verifying the flooding condition first (–2 marks):** If $A < 1$ in an absorber or $S < 1$ in a stripper, the operating line crosses the equilibrium and the design is infeasible. Always check first.
> 6. **Failing to round $Z$ to standard shell sections (–1 mark):** Real columns come in 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0 m sections. Always round up.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **HTU–NTU Framework** is the *kinetic* (HTU) × *thermodynamic* (NTU) = *Packing Height $Z$*.
- **Gas-phase form:** $Z = (G_S / K_G a) \times \int_{y_2}^{y_1} dy/(y - y^*)$.
- **Liquid-phase form:** $Z = (L_S / K_L a) \times \int_{x_2}^{x_1} dx/(x^* - x)$.
- **Absorption Factor $A = L/(mG) > 1$** for valid absorption.
- **Stripping Factor $S = mG/L > 1$** for valid stripping.
- **Closed-form NTU (linear equilibrium, dilute):**
  - Absorber: $NTU_G = \dfrac{A}{A-1} \ln\left[\dfrac{y_1 - mx_2}{y_2 - mx_2}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{A}\right)\right]$
  - Stripper: $NTU_L = \dfrac{S}{S-1} \ln\left[\dfrac{x_1 - y_2/m}{x_2 - y_2/m}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{S}\right)\right]$
  - For $A = 1$ or $S = 1$: use the limit form (linear driving force).
- **Column Diameter** is determined from the **Generalised Pressure Drop Correlation (GPDC / Eckert chart)**:
  - Compute Flow Parameter $X = (L/G)\sqrt{\rho_G / (\rho_L - \rho_G)}$.
  - Read $Y_f$ at flooding from the chart.
  - Solve for $G'_f$ using the packing factor $C_f$ and liquid viscosity $\mu_L$.
  - Apply flooding fraction $f_f = 0.6$–$0.8$ to find operating velocity.
  - Compute $D = \sqrt{4 G'_{\text{total}} / (\pi G'_{\text{op}})}$.
- **Onda Correlations** give $k_L$ and $k_G$ separately, useful for non-vendor-specific design.
- **Pressure Drop** is typically 15–50 mm H₂O per metre of packing; total < 10% of column pressure.
- **Liquid Distributor:** 30–40 drip points/m² for Pall rings; 40–60 for Raschig rings.
- **Packing Selection:** Pall rings (high capacity), Raschig rings (low cost, low efficiency), Berl saddles (good for acids), structured packing (very low pressure drop, very high efficiency).
- **Subscript convention:** **1** = rich (gas-in for absorber); **2** = lean (gas-out for absorber).
- **Dilute system simplifications:** Constant $G$, $L$, $(1-y)_M \approx 1$, $(1-x)_M \approx 1$, Henry's law linear $y^* = mx$.
- **Two-film theory** gives $1/K_G = 1/k_G + m/k_L$ (resistances add in series).
- **The packing height $Z$ is independent of the chosen HTU basis** — both HTU_G·NTU_G and HTU_L·NTU_L yield the same $Z$.

---

**End of Module 1 — Topic Notes | PCCHT601 — Chemical Engineering Design II | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Design of Single Solvent Sieve Tray Extraction Columns for Immiscible System: Number of Trays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT601 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Design of Single Solvent Sieve Tray Extraction Columns for Immiscible System: Number of Trays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liquid–Liquid Extraction** is a separation operation in which a solute is transferred from one liquid phase (the **feed/carrier**) into another immiscible liquid phase (the **solvent**). When a **single solvent** is used and the two bulk liquids are **immiscible**, the design of a multistage contactor reduces to a stage-by-stage mass-transfer calculation governed by the **equilibrium distribution coefficient (K_D)**.

A **Sieve Tray Extraction Column** is a vertical column containing a stack of horizontal perforated plates (sieve trays) on which the two immiscible liquids are contacted countercurrently. The light phase rises as dispersed droplets through the perforations of each tray, while the heavy phase flows across each tray as a continuous layer and drops through the downcomers to the tray below (or vice versa, depending on which phase is dispersed).

The **Number of Trays (N_actual)** required is determined from:

1. The theoretical (ideal) number of equilibrium stages, **N_eq**, computed from the **Kremser equation** for immiscible systems.
2. The **Murphree stage efficiency, E_M**, which corrects for the fact that real trays do not achieve thermodynamic equilibrium.

$$N_{actual} = \frac{N_{eq}}{E_M}$$

The classical **Kremser equation** for an immiscible system with constant distribution coefficient $K_D$ is:

$$\boxed{\,N_{eq} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{(x_F - x^{\star})}{(x_N - x^{\star})}\right]}{\ln(A)}\,}$$

where $A = K_D \, V/L$ is the **extraction factor**, $x^{\star} = y_S / K_D$ is the raffinate composition in equilibrium with the entering solvent, and the subscripts $F$ (feed), $N$ (raffinate), and $S$ (solvent) denote stream compositions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Tea-Bag in a Cascade of Cups" Analogy**

Imagine you have a cup of strong, bitter tea (the **carrier + solute**, e.g., water + acetic acid) and you want to remove the bitterness using a sequence of fresh cups of cream (the **solvent**, e.g., toluene — immiscible with water). You pour the tea into cup 1, stir, the bitterness partially migrates into the cream, and you pour the tea into cup 2 with fresh cream, and so on.

- Each "cup" is a **theoretical tray/equilibrium stage**.
- The ratio "how much bitterness moves per cup, per cup of cream" is the **distribution coefficient $K_D$**.
- The number of cups you need to bring the tea from very bitter to barely bitter is exactly the **Number of Trays**.
- The extraction factor $A = K_D V/L$ tells you whether one cup of cream is "stronger" or "weaker" than the tea it must purify — much like the size of each successive cream cup relative to the tea volume.

In an industrial sieve-tray column, this "cascade of cups" is replaced by a vertical stack of perforated plates, with **continuous countercurrent contact** instead of batch-wise transfers — making the calculation slightly different (Kremser rather than simple geometric ratios) but conceptually identical.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Distribution coefficient $K_D = y^{\star}/x$** (mass fraction basis): typically **0.1 – 10** for industrial systems; dimensionless.
- **Extraction factor $A = K_D \, V/L$**: dimensionless; design target **A > 1** (favourable extraction).
- **Murphree tray efficiency $E_M$**: typically **0.30 – 0.80** for liquid–liquid systems (lower than gas–liquid because of higher viscosity and slower mass transfer).
- **Interfacial tension**: typically **8 – 50 dyne/cm**; controls droplet formation through sieve holes.
- **Standard sieve hole diameter**: **3 – 8 mm**; free area **5 % – 25 %** of tray area.
- **Tray spacing**: typically **150 – 600 mm**, much larger than distillation because of dispersed-phase coalescence/redispersion requirements.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why the Kremser Equation Works

For an **immiscible** solvent pair, the bulk flow rates of each phase are assumed **constant** along the column (only the solute redistributes). This single simplification converts a coupled, nonlinear two-phase problem into a linear, analytically solvable recurrence.

### 2.1.1 Governing Equations (Step-by-Step Logic)

| Step | Statement | Reason |
|------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | $L, V = \text{const}$ along the column | Carrier and solvent are mutually immiscible; only solute C transfers. |
| 2 | $y_n = K_D \, x_n$ at equilibrium on tray $n$ | Constant $K_D$ (linear equilibrium) — true for dilute systems. |
| 3 | Operating line: $V \, y_{n+1} = L \, x_n + V \, y_S - L \, x_N$ | Overall solute balance around the bottom $n$ trays. |
| 4 | Define $A = K_D V/L$ and $x^{\star} = y_S / K_D$ | Non-dimensionalise so the recurrence collapses. |
| 5 | $x_n - x^{\star} = A\,(x_{n+1} - x^{\star})$ | Substitution gives a first-order linear recurrence. |
| 6 | Telescope from tray 0 (feed) to tray $N$ | $x_F - x^{\star} = A^N (x_N - x^{\star})$. |
| 7 | Solve for $N$ | Take logarithms → **Kremser equation**. |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is **exam-ready**: every entry is a tested formula for PCCHT601 Module 1.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Boundary Conditions | Typical Units |
|---|--------------------|------------------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $K_D = y^{\star} / x$ | Equilibrium distribution coefficient (linear range) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $A = K_D \, V / L$ | Extraction factor (must be $> 1$ for finite $N$) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $x^{\star} = y_S / K_D$ | Pseudo-equilibrium raffinate composition w.r.t. entering solvent | mass fraction |
| 4 | $N_{eq} = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_F - x^{\star}}{x_N - x^{\star}}\right]}{\ln A}$ | **Kremser equation** (immscible, constant $K_D$, $A \neq 1$) | stages |
| 5 | $N_{eq} = \dfrac{x_F - x_N}{x_F - y_S/K_D}$ | Kremser limit as $A \rightarrow 1$ | stages |
| 6 | $N_{eq} = \dfrac{\ln(x_F/x_N)}{\ln A}$ | Special case: pure entering solvent ($y_S = 0 \Rightarrow x^{\star}=0$) | stages |
| 7 | $N_{actual} = N_{eq} / E_M$ | Correction for non-ideal tray | trays |
| 8 | $E_M = \dfrac{x_{n-1} - x_n}{x_{n-1} - x_n^{\star}}$ | Murphree efficiency (raffinate phase basis) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $V_{min} = L / K_D$ | Minimum solvent flow rate (when $A=1$) | kg/h |
| 10 | $y_{n+1} = (L/V) x_n + y_S - (L/V) x_N$ | Operating line (linear for constant $L, V$) | mass fraction |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceuticals**: Recovery of penicillin / antibiotics from fermentation broth using amyl acetate or butyl acetate in a sieve-tray column. $K_D$ values are pH-dependent; the design is iterated until pH, $K_D$, and $N$ all converge.
- **Petroleum refining**: **Propane deasphalting** uses sieve-tray extractors to separate heavy oils from asphalt — here $A \gg 1$ is required because the solute is non-volatile and difficult to extract.
- **Nuclear reprocessing**: **Purex process** uses sieve-tray pulsed columns with TBP/kerosene to extract uranium and plutonium from nitric acid solutions.
- **Vegetable-oil processing**: Liquid–liquid extraction of free fatty acids using a sieve-tray contactor with hexane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all the above, the **Kremser equation** is the *first-cut* analytical tool; non-constant $K_D$ requires the **Hunter–Nash graphical method** (covered later in the course) — but for KTU Module 1, the immiscible-system Kremser result is the central deliverable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Kremser Equation (Immiscible, Constant $K_D$)

### 3.1.1 Stage Numbering Convention (Treybal Notation)

We adopt the following notation, which is standard in KTU board examinations:

- Stage $0$ → **feed stage** (fresh feed enters as $L$ with composition $x_F$).
- Stage $n$ → raffinate composition $x_n$, extract composition $y_n$ (in equilibrium).
- Stage $1$ → extract product leaves as $V$ with composition $y_1$.
- Stage $N$ → raffinate product leaves as $L$ with composition $x_N$.
- Solvent enters at the **bottom** as $V$ with composition $y_S$ (i.e., $y_{N+1} = y_S$).

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Material Balance Around the Bottom $n$ Trays

A solute balance around stages $n, n+1, \ldots, N$ gives:

$$V \, y_{n+1} + L \, x_N \;=\; V \, y_S + L \, x_n$$

Rearranging for $y_{n+1}$:

$$y_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{L}{V}\,x_n \;+\; y_S \;-\; \frac{L}{V}\,x_N \quad \text{...(Operating Line)}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Substitute the Linear Equilibrium Relation

Since $K_D$ is constant:

$$y_n \;=\; K_D \, x_n \quad \Longrightarrow \quad y_{n+1} \;=\; K_D \, x_{n+1}$$

Insert this into the operating line:

$$K_D \, x_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{L}{V}\,x_n \;+\; y_S \;-\; \frac{L}{V}\,x_N$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Introduce Non-Dimensional Parameters

Define:

$$A \;\equiv\; \frac{K_D \, V}{L} \qquad \text{(extraction factor, dimensionless)}$$

$$x^{\star} \;\equiv\; \frac{y_S}{K_D} \qquad \text{(equilibrium raffinate composition w.r.t. solvent)}$$

Divide the operating-line equation by $K_D$:

$$x_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{x_n}{A} \;+\; \frac{y_S}{K_D} \;-\; \frac{x_N}{A} \;=\; \frac{x_n}{A} \;+\; x^{\star} \;-\; \frac{x_N}{A}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Form the Recurrence in $(x_n - x^{\star})$

Rearrange:

$$x_n \;-\; x^{\star} \;=\; A\,(x_{n+1} - x^{\star})$$

This is a **geometric progression** with common ratio $A$.

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Telescope the Recurrence from $n = 0$ to $n = N$

Applying the recurrence repeatedly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_0 - x^{\star} &= A\,(x_1 - x^{\star}) \\
                &= A^2\,(x_2 - x^{\star}) \\
                &= \;\;\vdots \\
                &= A^N\,(x_N - x^{\star})
\end{aligned}
$$

By definition, $x_0 = x_F$ (composition of the entering feed), so:

$$x_F - x^{\star} \;=\; A^{N}\,(x_N - x^{\star})$$

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Solve for $N$

Taking natural logarithms (valid for $A > 0, \;A \neq 1$):

$$\ln(x_F - x^{\star}) \;-\; \ln(x_N - x^{\star}) \;=\; N\,\ln A$$

$$\boxed{\,N_{eq} \;=\; \frac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_F - x^{\star}}{x_N - x^{\star}}\right]}{\ln A}\,}$$

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Limiting Case $A \to 1$ (Use L'Hôpital's Rule)

When $A \to 1$, both numerator and denominator vanish. Differentiating with respect to $A$:

$$\lim_{A \to 1}\frac{\ln(x_F - x^{\star}) - \ln(x_N - x^{\star})}{\ln A} \;=\; \frac{x_F - x_N}{x_F - x^{\star}}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\,N_{eq}(A=1) \;=\; \frac{x_F - x_N}{x_F - y_S/K_D}\,}$$

This represents an **infinite** number of stages in the strict mathematical sense — a useful "infinity-check" for KTU problems.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard 14-Mark Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — Acetic Acid Extraction with Isopropyl Ether**

A dilute aqueous solution containing **$x_F = 0.30$ (mass fraction) acetic acid** is to be extracted countercurrently with **isopropyl ether** in a sieve-tray column. The equilibrium distribution coefficient $K_D = y/x = 0.85$ is constant over the composition range. The mass flow rates are $L = 1000$ kg/h (aqueous) and $V = 800$ kg/h (ether). The entering solvent is **pure**, so $y_S = 0$. The desired raffinate composition is $x_N = 0.04$.

**Required:** (a) Calculate the **number of ideal (equilibrium) stages** using the Kremser equation.
(b) If the **Murphree efficiency** of each sieve tray is $E_M = 0.55$, calculate the **actual number of trays**.
(c) Comment on whether the chosen solvent rate is acceptable.

### Solution

#### Part (a) — Number of Ideal Stages

**Step 1:** Compute the extraction factor.

$$A = \frac{K_D \, V}{L} = \frac{0.85 \times 800}{1000} = 0.68$$

**Step 2:** Compute the pseudo-equilibrium composition (solvent is pure, $y_S = 0$).

$$x^{\star} = \frac{y_S}{K_D} = \frac{0}{0.85} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Kremser equation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{eq} &= \frac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_F - x^{\star}}{x_N - x^{\star}}\right]}{\ln A} \\
       &= \frac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{0.30 - 0}{0.04 - 0}\right]}{\ln(0.68)} \\
       &= \frac{\ln(7.5)}{\ln(0.68)} \\
       &= \frac{2.0149}{-0.3857} \\
       &= -5.224
\end{aligned}
$$

The **negative sign** indicates that the magnitude is the answer (because $A < 1$, extraction is unfavourable). So:

$$N_{eq} = 5.22 \text{ ideal stages} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; N_{eq} \approx 6 \text{ stages (round up)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Dropping the negative sign on $\ln A$ when $A < 1$ gives a *positive* value of $N$ automatically — always take the magnitude of the final answer. The direction of the extraction is set by **whether $A > 1$ or $A < 1$**, not by the sign of $N$.

#### Part (b) — Actual Number of Trays

$$N_{actual} = \frac{N_{eq}}{E_M} = \frac{5.22}{0.55} = 9.49 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 10 \text{ actual sieve trays}$$

#### Part (c) — Solvent Rate Discussion

Since $A = 0.68 < 1$, the system is in the **unfavourable extraction regime**, and an infinite number of stages would be required as $A \to 1$. **Recommendation:** Increase $V$ such that $A \geq 1.3$ for a robust design. With $A_{target} = 1.3$:

$$V_{new} = \frac{A_{target} \cdot L}{K_D} = \frac{1.3 \times 1000}{0.85} = 1529 \text{ kg/h}$$

The increased solvent rate would lower the number of ideal stages to:

$$N_{eq}^{new} = \frac{\ln(7.5)}{\ln(1.3)} = \frac{2.0149}{0.2624} = 7.68 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 8 \text{ ideal stages}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for $A$ Value

| Value of $A$ | Physical Meaning | Kremser Result | Design Action |
|--------------|------------------|----------------|----------------|
| $A > 1$ | Favourable extraction | Finite positive $N$ | Acceptable — proceed to size column |
| $A = 1$ | Pinch point | Infinite $N$ (limit form) | Increase $V$ or change solvent |
| $A < 1$ | Unfavourable extraction | Finite but large $N$ (magnitude) | Increase $V$ or pick better solvent (higher $K_D$) |
| $A \to 0$ | Stripping-like regime | $N$ very large | Not feasible; switch to cross-flow or different solvent |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sieve Tray Extraction Column — Block-Level Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below renders a **Block-Level Functional Flow** of the internal mass-transfer operations of a single sieve-tray extractor. This is preferred over a physical drawing because Mermaid cannot faithfully render 3-D perforated plates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Fresh Feed: L, xF]:::input --> STAGE0
    A1[Recycle Solvent: V, yS]:::input --> STAGEN
    STAGE0[Stage 0: Feed Entry]:::stage
    STAGE0 -- "L, x0 = xF" --> STAGE1
    STAGE1[Stage 1: Tray 1]:::stage
    STAGE1 -- "y1 = KD·x1" --> EXT
    STAGE1 -- "L, x1" --> STAGE2
    STAGE2[Stage 2: Tray 2]:::stage
    STAGE2 -- "y2 = KD·x2" --> EXT
    STAGE2 -- "L, x2" --> STAGE3
    STAGE3["..."]:::stage
    STAGE3 -- "L, xN-1" --> STAGEN
    STAGEN[Stage N: Bottom Tray]:::stage
    STAGEN -- "L, xN" --> RAFF[RAFFINATE PRODUCT]:::output
    STAGEN -- "yN+1 = yS" --> RECY[SOLVENT RECYCLE]:::output
    EXT[EXTRACT PRODUCT y1]:::output
    RECY -->|distill / strip| A1

    classDef input fill:#FFF4D6,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef stage fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#1F618D,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#196F3D,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Mass-Transfer Logic Within a Single Sieve Tray (Functional Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRAY[Single Sieve Tray]
        A[Heavy Phase In: L, xn-1]:::heavy --> JCT[Dispersing Junction]
        B[Light Phase In: V, yn+1]:::light --> JCT
        JCT -- "droplets rise" --> CONT[Contact Zone: Interfacial Mass Transfer]
        CONT -- "disengagement" --> COAL[Coalescence Zone above tray]
        COAL --> CL[Clear Light Phase: yn, V]
        CONT --> CH[Clear Heavy Phase: xn, L]
    end
    CL[Clear Light Phase: yn, V]:::light
    CH[Clear Heavy Phase: xn, L]:::heavy
    CL -->|upward to next tray| NT[Next Tray]
    CH -->|downward via downcomer| DT[Downcomer]

    classDef heavy fill:#FADBD8,stroke:#922B21,color:#000
    classDef light fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#1F618D,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Algorithm for Determining Number of Trays (Logical Flow Chart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Given: xF, xN, yS, L, V, KD]:::start --> Q2{KD constant over range?}
    Q2 -- NO --> GN[Use Hunter-Nash Graphical Method]:::note
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3[Compute A = KD V / L]:::step
    Q3 --> Q4{A >= 1.3?}
    Q4 -- NO --> Q5[Increase V or pick better solvent]:::warn
    Q4 -- YES --> Q6[Compute xstar = yS / KD]:::step
    Q6 --> Q7{A == 1?}
    Q7 -- YES --> Q8[Use N = xF - xN divided by xF - xstar]:::step
    Q7 -- NO --> Q9[Use Kremser: N = ln xF-xstar over xN-xstar divided by ln A]:::step
    Q9 --> Q10[Compute Nactual = N ideal divided by EM]:::step
    Q8 --> Q10
    Q10 --> Q11[Select tray spacing, hole size, free area]:::step
    Q11 --> Q12[Final Column Design]:::end
```
