It is a *measure of the column's mass transfer efficiency*. A *small* HTU means the column is highly efficient (one transfer unit is accomplished in a small height); a *large* HTU means poor efficiency.
>
> **Formally (1.5 marks):** The gas-phase overall HTU is:
> $$H_{tG} = \frac{G_s}{K_Y \cdot a}$$
> and the liquid-phase overall HTU is:
> $$H_{tL} = \frac{L_s}{K_X \cdot a}$$
>
> **Combination of Resistances (3.5 marks):** Using the additivity of mass transfer resistances in the two-film theory:
> $$\frac{1}{K_Y} = \frac{1}{k_Y} + \frac{m}{k_X}$$
> where $k_Y$ and $k_X$ are the individual film coefficients. Converting to HTU form:
> $$\frac{H_{tG}}{G_s/a} = \frac{H_G}{G_s/a} + \frac{m \cdot H_L}{L_s/a}$$
> Simplifying:
> $$\boxed{\frac{1}{H_{tG}} = \frac{1}{H_G} + \frac{m \cdot G_s / L_s}{H_L}}$$
> Similarly for the liquid side:
> $$\boxed{\frac{1}{H_{tL}} = \frac{1}{H_L} + \frac{L_s / (m G_s)}{H_G}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

> **Step 1 — Verify operating line (0.5 marks):** Material balance:
> $G_s(Y_1 - Y_2) = L_s(X_1 - X_2)$
> Since $mG_s/L_s = 1.0$ is given, and $m = 1.5$, then $G_s/L_s = 1/1.5 = 0.667$.

> **Step 2 — Find the slope ratio $mG_s/L_s$ in the combination formula (1 mark):** Given as **1.0** in the problem.

> **Step 3 — Compute $H_{tG}$ from $H_G$ and $H_L$ (2 marks):**
> $$\frac{1}{H_{tG}} = \frac{1}{H_G} + \frac{m G_s / L_s}{H_L} = \frac{1}{0.40} + \frac{1.0}{0.25}$$
> $$\frac{1}{H_{tG}} = 2.50 + 4.00 = 6.50 \text{ m}^{-1}$$
> $$H_{tG} = \frac{1}{6.50} = 0.1538 \text{ m}$$

> **Step 4 — Compute driving forces (1.5 marks):**
> At rich end: $Y_1^* = 1.5 \times 0.010 = 0.0150$. Driving force: $Y_1 - Y_1^* = 0.04 - 0.0150 = 0.0250$.
> At lean end: $Y_2^* = 1.5 \times 0 = 0$. Driving force: $Y_2 - Y_2^* = 0.004 - 0 = 0.0040$.

> **Step 5 — Apply Colburn equation (1.5 marks):**
> Log-mean driving force:
> $$(Y - Y^*)_{LM} = \frac{0.0250 - 0.0040}{\ln(0.0250 / 0.0040)} = \frac{0.0210}{\ln(6.25)} = \frac{0.0210}{1.8326} = 0.01146$$
> $$NTU_G = \frac{0.04 - 0.004}{0.01146} = \frac{0.036}{0.01146} = 3.142$$

> **Step 6 — Packed height (0.5 marks):**
> $$Z = NTU_G \times H_{tG} = 3.142 \times 0.1538 = 0.4832 \text{ m}$$

> **Final Answer: $Z \approx 0.483 \text{ m}$**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **HTU Combination Direction:** Many students incorrectly write $H_{tG} = H_G + (mG_s/L_s) H_L$ (adding HTUs directly) instead of the correct **reciprocal addition** $\frac{1}{H_{tG}} = \frac{1}{H_G} + \frac{mG_s/L_s}{H_L}$. This is the most common error in HTU problems and costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Using the Right Basis:** When combining HTUs, *always* stay consistent with the phase basis. If you start with $H_{tG}$ (gas-based), the slope factor is $mG_s/L_s$. If you switch to $H_{tL}$ (liquid-based), the slope factor becomes $L_s/(mG_s)$. Mixing these is a 1-mark trap.

---

### 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read Before Every KTU Exam**

- **NTU Definition (Gas Basis):** $NTU_G = \int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} dY / (Y - Y^*)$ — the *integral of fractional change in driving force*.
- **NTU Definition (Liquid Basis):** $NTU_L = \int_{X_2}^{X_1} dX / (X^* - X)$.
- **Master Design Equation:** $Z = NTU \times HTU$ — the single most important equation in the topic.
- **Colburn Equation (straight lines):** $NTU_G = (Y_1 - Y_2) / (Y - Y^*)_{LM}$ — used for *dilute*, *linear-equilibrium* systems.
- **Log-Mean Driving Force:** Always check that both driving-force terms have the *same sign* before taking the logarithm. If signs differ, the equation is **inapplicable**.
- **HTU Combination Rules (memorise both):**
  - Gas side: $1/H_{tG} = 1/H_G + (mG_s/L_s)/H_L$
  - Liquid side: $1/H_{tL} = 1/H_L + (L_s/(mG_s))/H_G$
- **Always convert mol% to mole ratios** ($Y = y / (1-y)$) before using NTU equations.
- **Feasibility Check:** For a gas absorber, require $L_s / G_s \geq 1.5 \, m$ (rule of thumb). If the ratio is too low, increase the liquid rate.
- **HTU has units of length (metres)**, NOT dimensionless. NTU is the only *truly dimensionless* quantity.
- **NTU is preferred over McCabe-Thiele** when: (i) the system is dilute, (ii) the equilibrium data are extensive and accurate, (iii) the column is packed (no discrete stages), and (iv) a quick analytical estimate is required.
- **Comparison Metric:** For a given separation, $NTU_{rigorous} \geq NTU_{Colburn}$ (the Colburn result is the lower bound when the lines are slightly non-parallel).
- **Typical KTU marking pattern:** Part (a) = definition/derivation (7 marks), Part (b) = numerical (7 marks) with 1–2 marks allocated to *assumption statements* and *unit conversions*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diameter of Dryer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT601 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Diameter of Dryer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Chemical Engineering Design II (PCCHT601)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, the **Diameter of a Dryer** refers specifically to the **internal shell diameter of a Rotary (Drum) Dryer**. It is the principal geometric parameter determined during the mechanical design phase of the dryer based on **simultaneous heat-and-mass transfer constraints** and **gas-solid hydrodynamic flow conditions**.

The diameter is fixed such that the **volumetric gas flow rate**, the **mass velocity of the drying gas**, and the **required volumetric heat transfer coefficient** ($U_a$) are all satisfied simultaneously without exceeding the **entrainment velocity** of the wet solid particles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Drying):**
> The diameter of a rotary dryer is **not** chosen arbitrarily; it emerges as the *minimum feasible value* satisfying two independent design criteria:
> 1. **Heat Transfer Criterion** → driven by $U_a$ and $\Delta T$
> 2. **Fluidization/Entrainment Criterion** → driven by permissible gas mass velocity $G$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large rotating cement-mixer-like drum** lying horizontally on rollers. Hot air is blown in from one end (co-current) or sucked from the other (counter-current). Inside, wet sand tumbles forward as the drum slowly rotates. The drum must be:

- **Wide enough** so that hot air has enough residence time to dry the cascading curtain of falling particles.
- **Not too wide** so that the hot air velocity stays *below* the velocity that would simply blow the particles out of the drum like dust.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** Think of the diameter as a "compromise pipe size" — too narrow and drying is incomplete; too wide and you waste steel, floor space, and energy. The engineer calculates the *smallest* diameter that still allows sufficient heat transfer and acceptable gas velocity.

---

## 1.3 Standard Design Metrics & Physical Constants

The following tabulated values are universally accepted textbook ranges for **direct-heated rotary dryers** operating with air as the drying medium:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Volumetric Heat Transfer Coefficient | $U_a$ | **200 – 600** | $\text{W/m}^3\cdot\text{K}$ |
| Gas Mass Velocity | $G$ | **0.5 – 5.0** | $\text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$ |
| Entrainment Velocity (Hold-up Limit) | $V_{\text{ent}}$ | **0.5 – 1.5** | $\text{m/s}$ |
| Drum Inclination | $\alpha$ | **0 – 8°** | degrees |
| Drum Rotation Speed | $N$ | **2 – 8** | $\text{rpm}$ |
| Fill Fraction (Hold-up) | $\beta$ | **0.05 – 0.20** | dimensionless |
| Superficial Gas Velocity | $u_g$ | **0.5 – 2.0** | $\text{m/s}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Many students confuse the **volumetric** heat transfer coefficient $U_a$ (W/m³·K, applied over drum *volume*) with the **overall** heat transfer coefficient $U$ (W/m²·K, applied over drum *surface area*). This single confusion costs 4–5 marks in ESE numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of the Two Design Criteria

### Criterion 1 — Heat Transfer Criterion
The drum must supply enough heat to evaporate the required moisture. Since $U_a$ is defined per unit **drum volume**, the heat duty scales with $V_{\text{drum}} = (\pi D^2 / 4) L$. Hence a **smaller diameter forces a longer drum**, and vice-versa. The diameter is therefore the *primary* variable governing both capital cost and heat-transfer surface.

### Criterion 2 — Gas Velocity / Entrainment Criterion
If the superficial gas velocity $u_g$ exceeds the particle terminal (free-fall) velocity $u_t$, the wet solid is carried out as dust → severe product loss. Since $u_g = G/\rho_g$ and $G = \dot{m}_g / (\pi D^2 / 4)$, a **larger diameter *reduces*** $u_g$ for a fixed gas flow rate, thereby protecting against entrainment.

---

## 2.2 Derivation of the Two Governing Diameter Equations

### 2.2.1 Diameter from Heat-Transfer Requirement

Starting from the volumetric heat-transfer rate equation:

$$Q = U_a \, V_{\text{drum}} \, \Delta T$$

where $V_{\text{drum}} = (\pi D^2 / 4) L$ and $\Delta T = T_{\text{in,gas}} - T_{\text{out,gas}}$ (log-mean or arithmetic depending on co/counter-current mode).

Substituting:

$$Q = U_a \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$$

Solving for $D$:

$$\boxed{D_{\text{heat}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 Q}{U_a \, \pi \, L \, \Delta T}}}$$

### 2.2.2 Diameter from Gas Mass Velocity Requirement

The mass velocity $G$ is defined as mass flow rate of gas per unit cross-sectional area:

$$G = \frac{\dot{m}_g}{A_c} = \frac{\dot{m}_g}{(\pi D^2 / 4)}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{D_{\text{vel}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \dot{m}_g}{\pi G}}}$$

where $\dot{m}_g$ is the **mass flow rate of the drying gas (kg/s)** and $G$ is selected from the operating range to keep $u_g < u_t$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_{\text{heat}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 Q}{U_a \, \pi \, L \, \Delta T}}$ | $Q$ = total heat duty, $U_a$ = volumetric HTC, $L$ = drum length, $\Delta T$ = gas temperature difference | $D$ in **m**, $Q$ in **W**, $U_a$ in **W/m³·K** | Heat-transfer-limited design |
| 2 | $D_{\text{vel}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \dot{m}_g}{\pi G}}$ | $\dot{m}_g$ = gas mass flow, $G$ = permissible mass velocity | $D$ in **m**, $\dot{m}_g$ in **kg/s**, $G$ in **kg/m²·s** | Entrainment-limited design |
| 3 | $G = \rho_g \cdot u_g$ | $\rho_g$ = gas density, $u_g$ = superficial velocity | $\text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$ | Convert velocity ↔ mass velocity |
| 4 | $u_g = \dfrac{\dot{V}_g}{(\pi D^2 / 4)}$ | $\dot{V}_g$ = volumetric gas flow | m/s | Cross-check |
| 5 | $Q = \dot{m}_s \, (X_1 - X_2) \, \lambda_w$ | $\dot{m}_s$ = bone-dry solid feed, $X$ = moisture content (kg water/kg dry solid), $\lambda_w$ = latent heat of vaporization | W | Heat duty from mass balance |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_g = \dfrac{Q}{\Delta H_{\text{air}}}$ | $\Delta H_{\text{air}}$ = enthalpy drop of gas across dryer | kg/s | Gas requirement |
| 7 | $\Delta T = T_{\text{gas,in}} - T_{\text{gas,out}}$ | Arithmetic mean, or LMTD for precise counter-current | K | Temperature driving force |
| 8 | Hold-up: $\beta = \dfrac{0.5 \, \tau \, \dot{m}_s}{\rho_b \, (\pi D^2/4) L}$ | $\tau$ = residence time, $\rho_b$ = bulk density | dimensionless | Solids inventory |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** The **final design diameter** is the **larger** of $D_{\text{heat}}$ and $D_{\text{vel}}$ (the more conservative value). After selection, round **up** to the nearest standard ISO pipe/size.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Phosphates & Fertilizers:** Rotary dryers of diameter 2–4 m are used for drying NPK granules.
- **Cement Industry:** The largest rotary dryers (raw-material and clinker dryers) reach diameters of **5–6 m** and lengths of **40–80 m**.
- **Food Industry:** Sugar, starch, and grain dryers use diameters of **1–2.5 m** under gentle conditions.
- **Mineral Processing:** Sand, ore, and slag dryers typically operate in the **1.5–3.5 m** range.

The diameter choice directly impacts **shell thickness**, **rolling-ring design**, **drive horsepower**, and **foundation load** — all of which are downstream design decisions in PCCHT601.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of $D_{\text{heat}}$

### Step 1 — Statement of Heat-Transfer Equation
The total heat transferred from hot gas to wet solid in the dryer drum equals the product of the **volumetric heat transfer coefficient**, **drum volume**, and **driving-force temperature difference**.

$$Q = U_a \, V_{\text{drum}} \, \Delta T$$

**Logic:** $U_a$ is volumetric (W per m³ of drum volume per K of temperature difference). Multiplying by the total drum volume $V_{\text{drum}}$ converts it to a total heat-transfer rate in Watts.

### Step 2 — Express Drum Volume in Terms of Diameter
For a cylindrical shell of length $L$ and internal diameter $D$:

$$V_{\text{drum}} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot L$$

**Logic:** Cross-section of a cylinder is $\pi D^2/4$; multiplying by length gives volume.

### Step 3 — Substitute $V_{\text{drum}}$ into the Heat Equation

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= U_a \cdot \left(\frac{\pi D^2}{4}\right) \cdot L \cdot \Delta T \\
  &= \frac{U_a \, \pi \, L \, \Delta T \, D^2}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Isolate $D^2$
Multiply both sides by 4:

$$4Q = U_a \, \pi \, L \, \Delta T \, D^2$$

Divide both sides by $(U_a \, \pi \, L \, \Delta T)$:

$$D^2 = \frac{4Q}{U_a \, \pi \, L \, \Delta T}$$

### Step 5 — Take the Square Root

$$\boxed{D_{\text{heat}} = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{U_a \, \pi \, L \, \Delta T}}}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of $D_{\text{vel}}$

### Step 1 — Definition of Mass Velocity
Mass velocity $G$ is the **mass flow rate of gas per unit cross-sectional area** perpendicular to flow:

$$G = \frac{\dot{m}_g}{A_c}$$

**Logic:** $G$ has units of $\text{kg/(s·m}^2\text{)} = \text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$, which is equivalent to $\rho \cdot u$.

### Step 2 — Express Cross-Sectional Area

$$A_c = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into Mass-Velocity Definition

$$G = \frac{\dot{m}_g}{(\pi D^2 / 4)} = \frac{4 \dot{m}_g}{\pi D^2}$$

### Step 4 — Isolate $D^2$

$$\pi D^2 G = 4 \dot{m}_g$$

$$D^2 = \frac{4 \dot{m}_g}{\pi G}$$

### Step 5 — Take the Square Root

$$\boxed{D_{\text{vel}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \dot{m}_g}{\pi G}}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out KTU-Style Numerical Example (14-Mark Standard)

### Problem Statement
A **co-current rotary dryer** is to dry **5000 kg/h** of a wet granular solid from initial moisture $X_1 = 0.40$ to $X_2 = 0.04$ (kg water/kg dry solid). The following data are given:

- Dry solid specific heat: $C_{ps} = 0.88$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Water specific heat: $C_{pw} = 4.187$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Latent heat of vaporization at operating temperature: $\lambda_w = 2400$ kJ/kg
- Inlet air temperature: $T_1 = 400$ °C
- Outlet air temperature: $T_2 = 110$ °C
- Adopt: $U_a = 300$ W/(m³·K); $G = 1.2$ kg/(m²·s); $L = 10$ m
- Humidity ratio of inlet air $H_1 = 0.012$ kg water/kg dry air

**Required:** Calculate the diameter of the dryer using both criteria and select the design value.

---

### Solution — Step 1: Dry Solid Feed Rate

$$\dot{m}_s = \frac{\text{wet feed}}{1 + X_1} = \frac{5000}{1.40} = 3571.43 \;\text{kg dry solid/h} = 0.992 \;\text{kg/s}$$

**Logic:** $X_1$ is on a dry basis, so dry-solid rate = wet-rate ÷ $(1 + X_1)$.

### Solution — Step 2: Moisture to Be Evaporated

$$\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_s (X_1 - X_2) = 0.992 \times (0.40 - 0.04) = 0.357 \;\text{kg/s}$$

### Solution — Step 3: Heat Duty $Q$

Sensible + Latent terms:

$$Q = \dot{m}_s \, C_{ps} \, (T_{s,\text{out}} - T_{s,\text{in}}) + \dot{m}_w \, C_{pw} \, (T_{s,\text{out}} - T_{s,\text{in}}) + \dot{m}_w \, \lambda_w$$

Assuming solids enter at 25 °C and exit at 95 °C:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= [0.992 \times 0.88 \times (95 - 25)] + [0.357 \times 4.187 \times (95 - 25)] \\
  &\quad + [0.357 \times 2400] \;\text{ kJ/s} \\[4pt]
Q &= 61.05 + 104.66 + 856.80 \\
  &= 1022.51 \;\text{kW} = 1{,}022{,}510 \;\text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

### Solution — Step 4: Temperature Driving Force

For co-current dryer, use **arithmetic mean** approximation between air inlet and outlet:

$$\Delta T = T_1 - T_2 = 400 - 110 = 290 \;\text{K}$$

### Solution — Step 5: Diameter from Heat Criterion

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{heat}} &= \sqrt{\frac{4 Q}{U_a \, \pi \, L \, \Delta T}} \\[4pt]
               &= \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1{,}022{,}510}{300 \times \pi \times 10 \times 290}} \\[4pt]
               &= \sqrt{\frac{4{,}090{,}040}{2{,}733{,}054.6}} \\[4pt]
               &= \sqrt{1.4965} = 1.223 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Solution — Step 6: Gas Mass Flow Rate

Enthalpy drop of air: $\Delta H_{\text{air}} = C_{p,\text{air}}(T_1 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (400 - 110) = 291.45$ kJ/kg dry air.

$$\dot{m}_g = \frac{Q}{\Delta H_{\text{air}}} = \frac{1022.51}{291.45} = 3.508 \;\text{kg/s}$$

### Solution — Step 7: Diameter from Velocity Criterion

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{vel}} &= \sqrt{\frac{4 \dot{m}_g}{\pi G}} \\[4pt]
              &= \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 3.508}{\pi \times 1.2}} \\[4pt]
              &= \sqrt{\frac{14.032}{3.770}} \\[4pt]
              &= \sqrt{3.722} = 1.929 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Solution — Step 8: Final Design Selection

| Criterion | Computed $D$ (m) |
|---|---|
| Heat-transfer | **1.223** |
| Velocity / Entrainment | **1.929** |

Since $D_{\text{vel}} > D_{\text{heat}}$, the **velocity criterion governs**.

$$\boxed{D_{\text{design}} = 1.93 \;\text{m (round up to standard 2.0 m)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pattern:** In ~70 % of ESE numericals, the **velocity criterion dominates** when granular solids of medium particle size are used, because the permissible $G$ is intentionally conservative to prevent entrainment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Rotary Dryer Physical Layout (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wet Solid Feed Hopper] --> B[Inclined Rotary Drum]
    H[Air Blower] --> I[Air Heater Furnace]
    I --> J[Hot Gas Inlet Plenum]
    J --> B
    B --> K[Dust Cyclone Separator]
    B --> L[Discharge Chute for Dried Product]
    K --> M[Bag Filter / Scrubber]
    M --> N[ID Fan Induced Draft]
    N --> O[Stack to Atmosphere]
    P[Drive Motor with Gear Reducer] --> Q[Riding Rings]
    Q --> R[Support Tyres]
    R --> S[Trunnion Rollers]
    S --> T[Foundation Bed]
```

**Logic:** This is the **process flow direction** for a co-current rotary dryer. The diameter $D$ is fixed along the entire length of the **Drum** block, based on the dual criteria of heat duty and gas mass velocity.

---

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Which Criterion Governs?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Compute Q, m_dot_g, L, U_a, G] --> B[Compute D_heat]
    A --> C[Compute D_vel]
    B --> D{D_heat vs D_vel}
    C --> D
    D -->|D_vel greater| E[Velocity criterion governs]
    D -->|D_heat greater| F[Heat criterion governs]
    D -->|Both equal| G[Both criteria matched]
    E --> H[Select D = D_vel + 10 percent safety]
    F --> I[Select D = D_heat + 10 percent safety]
    G --> J[Select D as computed]
    H --> K[Cross-check u_g vs particle u_t]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Finalize Mechanical Shell Design]
```

**Logic:** In KTU board valuation, the examiner explicitly checks for *both* diameters being computed and the *governing criterion* being identified with justification.

---

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Gas-Solid Interaction Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hot Gas Entering from Inlet End] --> B[Cross Section pi D squared over 4]
    B --> C[Superficial Gas Velocity u_g]
    C --> D{Greater than Particle Terminal Velocity u_t ?}
    D -->|Yes| E[ENTRAINMENT - Particles blown out as dust - LOSS]
    D -->|No| F[SAFE OPERATION - Solids cascade through gas curtain]
    F --> G[Heat and Mass Transfer Occurs]
    G --> H[Dried Particles Discharge at Outlet]
```

**Logic:** This visually demonstrates the **entrainment criterion** — the diameter must be large enough that $u_g = \dot{m}_g/(\pi D^2/4) < u_t$.

---

## 4.4 Heat-Transfer Zone Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet: T_gas = 400 C, T_solid = 25 C] --> B[Zone 1: High Delta T, Rapid Evaporation]
    B --> C[Zone 2: Falling Rate Period, Internal Diffusion Control]
    C --> D[Zone 3: Equilibrium Moisture Reached]
    D --> E[Outlet: T_gas = 110 C, T_solid = 95 C]
```

**Logic:** This illustrates why an **average $\Delta T$** is acceptable for design — the **volumetric coefficient $U_a$** is already an integrated empirical average across all three zones.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define volumetric heat transfer coefficient $U_a$ for a rotary dryer. Why is it preferred over the surface-based coefficient $U$ in rotary-dryer design?**

**Model Answer:**
The volumetric heat transfer coefficient $U_a$ is defined as the rate of heat transfer per unit volume of dryer per unit temperature difference, with units of $\text{W/(m}^3\cdot\text{K)}$. It is preferred over the surface-based $U$ because the heat transfer in a rotary dryer occurs not only at the wall but predominantly through direct contact between cascading particles and the hot gas curtain across the entire drum volume. Using $U_a$ allows the designer to write the simple heat-balance equation $Q = U_a V \Delta T$, which directly yields the diameter. The value of $U_a$ is empirically correlated to drum diameter, fillage, and rotation speed.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the two independent criteria used for sizing the diameter of a rotary dryer. Which one usually governs for coarse granular solids?**

**Model Answer:**
The two criteria are:
1. **Heat-transfer criterion:** $D_{\text{heat}} = \sqrt{4Q/(U_a \pi L \Delta T)}$
2. **Gas-mass-velocity (entrainment) criterion:** $D_{\text{vel}} = \sqrt{4 \dot{m}_g / (\pi G)}$

For **coarse granular solids** (e.g., sand, ore), the permissible mass velocity $G$ is restricted to a low value to keep $u_g$ below the particle terminal velocity, so the **velocity criterion** usually governs.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE of the following]**

---

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

A rotary dryer is to be designed for drying **8000 kg/h** of a wet solid from **$X_1 = 0.35$** to **$X_2 = 0.02$** (dry basis). Additional data:

- Specific heat of dry solid: $C_{ps} = 0.75$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Specific heat of water: $C_{pw} = 4.187$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Latent heat of vaporization: $\lambda_w = 2350$ kJ/kg
- Inlet gas temperature: $T_1 = 380$ °C; outlet gas temperature: $T_2 = 115$ °C
- Solid enters at 30 °C; exits at 100 °C
- Permissible gas mass velocity: $G = 1.5$ kg/(m²·s)
- Volumetric heat-transfer coefficient: $U_a = 350$ W/(m³·K)
- Drum length: $L = 12$ m
- Specific heat of air: $C_{pa} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K)

**(a)** Compute the diameter using the **heat-transfer criterion**. (7 marks)
**(b)** Compute the diameter using the **gas-velocity criterion** and select the final design diameter. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Dry-solid feed rate

$$\dot{m}_s = \frac{8000}{1 + 0.35} = \frac{8000}{1.35} = 5925.93 \;\text{kg/h} = 1.646 \;\text{kg/s}$$

**Step 2:** Moisture to be evaporated

$$\dot{m}_w = 1.646 \times (0.35 - 0.02) = 1.646 \times 0.33 = 0.543 \;\text{kg/s}$$

**Step 3:** Heat duty $Q$

$$Q = \dot{m}_s C_{ps} \Delta T_s + \dot{m}_w C_{pw} \Delta T_s + \dot{m}_w \lambda_w$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= [1.646 \times 0.75 \times (100 - 30)] + [0.543 \times 4.187 \times (100 - 30)] \\
  &\quad + [0.543 \times 2350] \\[2pt]
Q &= 86.42 + 159.16 + 1276.05 \\
Q &= 1521.63 \;\text{kW} = 1{,}521{,}630 \;\text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** $\Delta T = 380 - 115 = 265$ K

**Step 5:** Apply formula

$$D_{\text{heat}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1{,}521{,}630}{350 \times \pi \times 12 \times 265}} = \sqrt{\frac{6{,}086{,}520}{3{,}494{,}933}} = \sqrt{1.7415}$$

$$\boxed{D_{\text{heat}} = 1.32 \;\text{m}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Gas enthalpy drop

$$\Delta H_{\text{air}} = 1.005 \times (380 - 115) = 266.33 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2:** Gas mass flow rate

$$\dot{m}_g = \frac{Q}{\Delta H_{\text{air}}} = \frac{1521.63}{266.33} = 5.713 \;\text{kg/s}$$

**Step 3:** Apply velocity formula

$$D_{\text{vel}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 5.713}{\pi \times 1.5}} = \sqrt{\frac{22.852}{4.712}} = \sqrt{4.849}$$

$$\boxed{D_{\text{vel}} = 2.20 \;\text{m}}$$

**Step 4:** Final selection — since $D_{\text{vel}} > D_{\text{heat}}$, the **velocity criterion governs**.

$$\boxed{D_{\text{design}} = 2.20 \;\text{m} \rightarrow \text{round up to standard 2.3 m}}$$

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

A counter-current rotary dryer handles **12,000 kg/h** of a wet granular fertilizer from $X_1 = 0.25$ to $X_2 = 0.03$ (dry basis). Data:

- $C_{ps} = 0.90$ kJ/(kg·K); $C_{pw} = 4.187$ kJ/(kg·K); $\lambda_w = 2400$ kJ/kg
- Gas in at $T_1 = 450$ °C, gas out at $T_2 = 120$ °C
- Solids in at 30 °C, solids out at 105 °C
- $U_a = 400$ W/(m³·K); $G = 1.8$ kg/(m²·s); $L = 14$ m
- $C_{pa} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K)

**(a)** Estimate the diameter from the **heat-transfer criterion** and the **mass-velocity criterion**. (10 marks)
**(b)** Explain why a **safety factor of 10–15 %** is normally added to the diameter, and comment on the **selection of $G$** if particle terminal velocity is reduced by 30 %. (4 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [10 Marks]

**Step 1:** $\dot{m}_s = 12000 / 1.25 = 9600$ kg/h $= 2.667$ kg/s

**Step 2:** $\dot{m}_w = 2.667 \times (0.25 - 0.03) = 2.667 \times 0.22 = 0.587$ kg/s

**Step 3:** Heat duty $Q$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= [2.667 \times 0.90 \times (105 - 30)] + [0.587 \times 4.187 \times (105 - 30)] \\
  &\quad + [0.587 \times 2400] \\[2pt]
Q &= 180.0 + 184.4 + 1408.8 = 1773.2 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** $D_{\text{heat}}$

$$D_{\text{heat}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1{,}773{,}200}{400 \times \pi \times 14 \times 330}} = \sqrt{\frac{7{,}092{,}800}{5{,}804{,}468}} = \sqrt{1.222}$$

$$D_{\text{heat}} = 1.105 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 5:** $\Delta H_{\text{air}} = 1.005 \times (450 - 120) = 331.65$ kJ/kg

**Step 6:** $\dot{m}_g = 1773.2 / 331.65 = 5.346$ kg/s

**Step 7:** $D_{\text{vel}}$

$$D_{\text{vel}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 5.346}{\pi \times 1.8}} = \sqrt{\frac{21.384}{5.655}} = \sqrt{3.782}$$

$$D_{\text{vel}} = 1.945 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 8:** Velocity governs → $D_{\text{design}} = 1.95$ m (rounded to standard 2.0 m).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [4 Marks]

A **10–15 % safety factor** is added to:

- Account for **scale-up uncertainties** in $U_a$ and $G$ (empirical correlations).
- Provide **operating flexibility** for feed-rate fluctuations.
- Allow **future capacity debottlenecking** without replacing the shell.

If particle terminal velocity is reduced by **30 %** (e.g., due to swelling, moisture, or attrition), then to keep $u_g < u_t$, the permissible $G$ must also be reduced by ~30 %. Recomputing: $G' = 1.8 \times 0.7 = 1.26$ kg/(m²·s), giving $D_{\text{vel}} = 2.36$ m. Hence the **diameter must be enlarged** when particle entrainment risk increases.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones (Examiner Comments from KTU Valuations):**
> 1. **Using $U$ instead of $U_a$** → wrong by a factor of $(L/D)$, typically 5–10 m. **Loss: 3 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting to convert kg/h to kg/s** → diameter comes out 60× too small. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Selecting the smaller of the two diameters** → design becomes unsafe; correct choice is the **larger**. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 4. **Ignoring the sensible-heat contribution to $Q$** → under-designs the diameter. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 5. **Not stating the governing criterion** → examiner cannot award "design justification" marks. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Diameter of Dryer"**
> - [x] Rotary dryer diameter = **larger** of $D_{\text{heat}}$ and $D_{\text{vel}}$
> - [x] $D_{\text{heat}} = \sqrt{4Q/(U_a \pi L \Delta T)}$ — uses **volumetric** HTC
> - [x] $D_{\text{vel}} = \sqrt{4\dot{m}_g/(\pi G)}$ — uses **mass velocity**
> - [x] $U_a$ range: **200–600 W/(m³·K)**; never confuse with $U$ (W/m²·K)
> - [x] $G$ range: **0.5–5.0 kg/(m²·s)**; select so that $u_g < u_t$
> - [x] Heat duty $Q$ includes **latent + sensible (solid + water)** terms
> - [x] Round the final $D$ **up** to the nearest standard size
> - [x] Add **10–15 % safety margin** on the chosen diameter
> - [x] Always justify which criterion governs
> - [x] Cross-verify $u_g$ against particle terminal velocity (entrainment check)
> - [x] Standard SI units **mandatory**: kg, m, s, K, W

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Length and Hold Up Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT601 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DESIGN II
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Length and Hold Up Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Length" in Chemical Engineering Equipment Design?

In the context of mass transfer equipment (absorption columns, stripping columns, extraction columns, and tray/packed towers), the term **Length** refers to the **axial dimension of the equipment along the direction of the principal fluid flow**. Depending on the type of column, this is more commonly called the **height (Z)** of the tower or the **active contact height**.

For the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the "Length" of a column is the dimension required to achieve a specified change in composition (or degree of separation) of the gas/liquid streams. It is fundamentally derived from either:

1. **The Number of Transfer Units (NTU)** multiplied by the **Height of a Transfer Unit (HTU)**, OR
2. **The Number of Theoretical Stages (N)** multiplied by the **Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate (HETP)**, OR
3. **Direct integration** of the mass-transfer differential equation (Kremser / Colburn approach).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:**
> *Length of a mass-transfer column is the total axial distance over which two immiscible phases are brought into intimate contact to achieve a prescribed change in concentration of the solute (or key component), determined either by the rate-based HTU-NTU method or the equilibrium-stage HETP method.*

## 1.2 What is "Hold-Up Time"?

**Hold-up time** (also called **residence time** or **contact time**, denoted by the symbol $\theta$ or $t_{res}$) is the **average time a quantity of fluid (liquid or gas) spends inside the processing equipment** before it exits. It is a measure of how long the two phases remain in mutual contact, which directly governs the extent of mass transfer.

In KTU terminology, the two main types of hold-up are:

| Term | Meaning | Typical Equipment |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Liquid hold-up, $h_L$** | Volume of liquid retained per unit volume of packing / per unit cross-section | Packed absorption/extraction columns |
| **Residence time, $\theta$** | Time a fluid element spends in the contactor | Batch reactors, continuous extraction columns |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
> Imagine you are standing in a queue at a canteen. The **Length of the queue** is the distance from the counter to the last person. The **Hold-up time** is the time you personally wait before your turn. In a packed column, the **height of packing** is like the queue length (determines how much contact occurs), and the **hold-up time** is like your wait time (determines how long each fluid element gets to interact).

## 1.3 Why Length and Hold-Up Time Matter in KTU Examinations

In KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2 frequently asks students to:

- Calculate the **height of packing** using the **HTU-NTU approach** (very common — ~7 to 14 mark questions).
- Calculate the **height using the Kremser integrated equation** for dilute systems.
- Determine the **residence time / hold-up time** of the dispersed or continuous phase in extraction/absorption columns.
- Relate the **diameter** (covered in Module 1/2) and **length** of the column to the overall throughput and design specifications.

**Standard physical constants to remember:**

- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$
- Standard atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = 101.325 \; kPa$
- Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314 \; J/(mol \cdot K)$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Length of Packed Column — The HTU-NTU Concept

The **Height of a Transfer Unit (HTU)** concept was introduced by **Chilton and Colburn (1935)** to decouple the equilibrium and rate aspects of mass transfer.

The general expression for the height of a packed section is:

$$Z = \int_{y_1}^{y_2} \frac{G \, dy}{K_y \, a \, (y - y^*)} \; = \; H_{OG} \times N_{OG}$$

Where:

- $G$ = superficial gas mass velocity $(kg/(m^2 \cdot s))$
- $K_y$ = overall gas-phase mass transfer coefficient $(kg/(m^2 \cdot s))$
- $a$ = effective interfacial area per unit volume of packing $(m^2/m^3)$
- $y^*$ = gas composition in equilibrium with the bulk liquid
- $H_{OG} = G / K_y a$ = **Height of an Overall Gas-Phase Transfer Unit** $(m)$
- $N_{OG}$ = **Number of Overall Gas-Phase Transfer Units** (dimensionless)

### 2.1.1 Definitions — HTU and NTU

> [!NOTE]
> **Height of a Transfer Unit (HTU):** A measure of the *difficulty* of mass transfer. Smaller HTU ⇒ easier mass transfer. Numerically, it is the height of packing required to effect one "transfer unit" of composition change.

> [!NOTE]
> **Number of Transfer Units (NTU):** A measure of the *extent* of separation required. Larger NTU ⇒ harder separation (more change in composition required).

### 2.1.2 Log-Mean Driving Force Form (Colburn Equation)

For **dilute systems** (where the gas flow rate $G$ and the liquid flow rate $L$ are essentially constant, and the operating line is straight), Colburn showed that:

$$N_{OG} = \frac{y_1 - y_2}{(y - y^*)_{LM}} \times \frac{1}{1 - m \frac{G}{L}} \times \ln \left[ \frac{1 - m \frac{G}{L}}{1 - \frac{y_2}{y_1}} \right]$$

Where:

- $(y - y^*)_{LM} = \frac{(y_1 - y_1^*) - (y_2 - y_2^*)}{\ln \left[ \frac{y_1 - y_1^*}{y_2 - y_2^*} \right]}$
- $m$ = slope of the equilibrium line (slope of $y^*$ vs $x$ plot)
- $G/L$ = ratio of gas to liquid molar flow rates (on a solute-free basis)

### 2.1.3 Individual HTUs

Sometimes it is convenient to express the overall HTU in terms of individual gas and liquid HTUs:

$$H_{OG} = H_G + \frac{m \, G}{L} \cdot H_L$$

Where $H_G$ and $H_L$ are the individual gas and liquid phase HTUs respectively.

## 2.2 Length of Packed Column — Integrated Kremser Equation (Dilute Case)

For **dilute gas absorption**, the operating line is linear and the integration yields a closed-form (Kremser) expression:

$$Z = \frac{G}{K_y \, a} \cdot \frac{y_1 - y_2}{(y - y^*)_{LM}}$$

But more commonly in KTU questions, the **integrated form** of the height equation when the operating line is straight and the equilibrium line is also straight ($y^* = m x$) gives:

$$Z = \frac{L}{K_x \, a} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \frac{m \, G}{L}} \cdot \ln \left[ \left(1 - \frac{m G}{L}\right) \frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2} + \frac{m G}{L} \right]$$

## 2.3 Length of Tray Column — HETP Method

For **tray columns** (plate columns), the length (height) is given by:

$$Z = N_{act} \times HETP$$

Where:

- $N_{act}$ = actual number of trays (obtained from the number of theoretical stages $N_{th}$ divided by the overall tray efficiency $E_o$)
- $HETP$ = Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate $(m)$

For design estimation, **HETP can be approximated** by correlations such as:

- For small columns (diameter < 0.6 m): $HETP \approx 0.4$ to $0.6 \; m$
- For larger columns: $HETP \approx 0.6$ to $1.0 \; m$
- For absorption: $HETP \approx 1.5$ to $1.8 \times$ packing height per stage

A widely used empirical correlation is:

$$HETP = 1.5 \times d_p \left( \frac{\alpha \, \mu_L}{\rho_L} \right)^{0.15} \cdot \left( \frac{Z_{col}}{3}\right)^{0.5}$$

## 2.4 Hold-Up Time — General Definition

The **mean residence time** of fluid in any continuous equipment is defined by the classic tank-in-series relationship:

$$\theta = \frac{V_{hold-up}}{Q} = \frac{\text{Volume of fluid retained in equipment}}{\text{Volumetric flow rate of that fluid}}$$

Where:

- $V_{hold-up}$ has units of $m^3$
- $Q$ has units of $m^3/s$
- $\theta$ has units of seconds

## 2.5 Liquid Hold-Up in Packed Columns

Liquid hold-up in a packed column is the volume of liquid retained per unit volume of packing:

$$h_L = \frac{\text{Volume of liquid retained}}{\text{Volume of packing}}$$

It is experimentally correlated as the sum of **static hold-up** ($h_s$) and **dynamic hold-up** ($h_d$):

$$h_L = h_s + h_d$$

A commonly used correlation (Stichlmair / Engel) for **random packings**:

$$h_d = 0.93 \left( \frac{\mu_L \, a^2 \, d_p^2 \, g_c}{\rho_L^2 \, g} \right)^{1/6} \cdot Re_L^{2/3} \cdot Ga_L^{-1/3}$$

Where:

- $Re_L = \frac{\rho_L \, u_L \, d_p}{\mu_L}$ = Liquid Reynolds number
- $Ga_L = \frac{d_p^3 \, \rho_L^2 \, g}{\mu_L^2}$ = Galilei number
- $a$ = specific surface area of packing
- $d_p$ = nominal packing size

## 2.6 Hold-Up Time in Extraction Columns

For **continuous extraction columns** (spray, packed, sieve-tray, RDC, Kühni, etc.), the residence time of each phase is critical. For a vertical column with dispersed phase hold-up $\phi_d$ and continuous phase hold-up $\phi_c$:

$$\theta_d = \frac{\phi_d \cdot A_c \cdot H_{col}}{Q_d} \quad ; \quad \theta_c = \frac{\phi_c \cdot A_c \cdot H_{col}}{Q_c}$$

Where:

- $A_c$ = cross-sectional area of the column $(m^2)$
- $H_{col}$ = total column height (length) $(m)$
- $Q_d, Q_c$ = volumetric flow rates of dispersed and continuous phases $(m^3/s)$

By definition, $\phi_d + \phi_c = 1$ (if we ignore the packing volume).

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these are the 10 highest-yield formulas for KTU Module 2.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Z = H_{OG} \times N_{OG}$ | Packed column height from overall gas HTU |
| 2 | $H_{OG} = G / (K_y a)$ | Definition of overall gas HTU |
| 3 | $N_{OG} = \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{(1 - mG/L)(y - y^*)}$ | Integral form of NTU |
| 4 | $N_{OG} = \frac{1}{1 - S} \ln \left[ (1-S)\frac{y_1 - mx_2}{y_2 - mx_2} + S \right]$ | Colburn/Kremser form (dilute) |
| 5 | $H_{OG} = H_G + (mG/L) H_L$ | Combined HTU relation |
| 6 | $Z = N_{act} \times HETP$ | Tray column height |
| 7 | $\theta = V_{hold} / Q$ | General residence/hold-up time |
| 8 | $h_L = h_s + h_d$ | Liquid hold-up in packed column |
| 9 | $\theta_d = \phi_d V_{col} / Q_d$ | Dispersed phase residence time |
| 10 | $Re_L = \rho_L u_L d_p / \mu_L$ | Liquid Reynolds number for HTU corr. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall to Avoid:** In all the formulas above, the absorption factor $A = L / (mG)$ or the stripping factor $S = mG / L$ is **dimensionless** and must be computed on a **solute-free (or inert) basis**, not on the total flow including solute. This is a very common KTU mark-loss point.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petroleum Industry:** Length of *CO₂ absorption* columns using MEA/DEA solutions in natural gas processing.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Hold-up time determines *contact time* for solvent extraction of antibiotics (e.g., penicillin extraction in Podbielniak extractors).
- **Wastewater Treatment:** Stripping columns for *removal of ammonia* from wastewater — height determined by HTU-NTU.
- **Air Pollution Control:** Scrubbers for *SO₂* removal — height determined by the dilute-solution Kremser equation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the HTU Concept

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Differential Mass Balance

Consider a differential height $dZ$ of a packed column. For the gas phase carrying solute $A$, a steady-state mass balance gives:

$$G \, dy = K_y \, a \, (y - y^*) \, dZ$$

**Reasoning:** The gas loses a small amount of solute $dy$ over the height $dZ$. The rate of transfer per unit volume is $K_y a (y - y^*)$. Multiplying by $dZ$ gives the moles transferred per unit cross-section, which must equal the depletion $G \, dy$.

### 3.1.2 Rearrangement

$$dZ = \frac{G \, dy}{K_y \, a \, (y - y^*)}$$

### 3.1.3 Integration Over the Column Height

Integrating from gas inlet composition $y_1$ (bottom of absorber) to gas outlet composition $y_2$ (top of absorber):

$$Z = \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{G \, dy}{K_y \, a \, (y - y^*)}$$

### 3.1.4 Defining HTU and NTU

By definition:

$$H_{OG} \equiv \frac{G}{K_y \, a} \quad \text{(a "constant" for a given system)}$$

$$N_{OG} \equiv \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{(y - y^*)}$$

Therefore:

$$Z = H_{OG} \times N_{OG}$$

**This is the foundational result of the HTU-NTU method.** $\square$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Kremser (Integrated) Equation for Dilute Absorption

### 3.2.1 Operating Line (Dilute System)

For dilute absorption, the gas and liquid flow rates are essentially constant. The operating line is straight:

$$G \, y_1 + L \, x_2 = G \, y + L \, x$$

Solving for $x$:

$$x = \frac{G}{L}(y_1 - y) + x_2$$

### 3.2.2 Equilibrium Line (Linear)

Assume the equilibrium line is also straight:

$$y^* = m \, x$$

### 3.2.3 Expressing $(y - y^*)$ in Terms of $y$ Only

Substituting the operating line into the equilibrium line:

$$y^* = m \left[ \frac{G}{L}(y_1 - y) + x_2 \right] = m \, x_2 + m \frac{G}{L}(y_1 - y)$$

Therefore:

$$y - y^* = y - m \, x_2 - m \frac{G}{L}(y_1 - y)$$

Let $S = \frac{mG}{L}$ (the stripping factor). Then:

$$y - y^* = y(1 + S) - S \, y_1 - m \, x_2$$

### 3.2.4 Substituting into the NTU Integral

$$N_{OG} = \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y(1 + S) - S \, y_1 - m \, x_2}$$

### 3.2.5 Performing the Integration

Let $u = y(1 + S) - S \, y_1 - m \, x_2$, so $du = (1 + S) \, dy$, and $dy = \frac{du}{1 + S}$.

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{1 + S} \int \frac{du}{u} = \frac{1}{1 + S} \ln(u) \Big|_{y_2}^{y_1}$$

### 3.2.6 Applying the Limits

At $y = y_1$:

$$u_1 = y_1(1 + S) - S y_1 - m x_2 = y_1 - m x_2$$

At $y = y_2$:

$$u_2 = y_2(1 + S) - S y_1 - m x_2 = y_2 + S(y_2 - y_1) - m x_2$$

### 3.2.7 Final Colburn-Kremser Equation

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{1 + S} \ln \left[ \frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2 + S(y_2 - y_1)} \right]$$

Factoring $(y_2 - m x_2)$ from the denominator and using $S = mG/L$:

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{mG}{L}} \ln \left[ \left(1 - \frac{mG}{L}\right) \frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2} + \frac{mG}{L} \right]$$

This is the **Kremser equation** for the number of overall gas-phase transfer units. $\square$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:** *[Adapted from KTU University Exam - May 2024 style]*
A gas stream containing $2.0 \; mol\%$ of $SO_2$ is to be scrubbed with pure water in a packed absorption column to reduce the $SO_2$ content to $0.1 \; mol\%$. The gas flow rate is $0.020 \; kmol/s$ and the water flow rate is $0.90 \; kmol/s$. The equilibrium relationship is $y^* = 30 \, x$ (linear, valid at the operating conditions). The overall gas-phase mass-transfer coefficient times the interfacial area is $K_y a = 0.40 \; kmol/(m^3 \cdot s)$.

**Calculate the height of packing required using the HTU-NTU (Kremser) method.**

### Step 1 — Identify the Variables

- $y_1 = 0.020$, $y_2 = 0.001$, $x_2 = 0$ (pure water inlet)
- $G = 0.020 \; kmol/s$, $L = 0.90 \; kmol/s$
- $m = 30$

**Note:** The flows are essentially constant because the solute is dilute. We will use the dilute-solute assumption.

### Step 2 — Compute the Stripping Factor $S$

$$S = \frac{mG}{L} = \frac{30 \times 0.020}{0.90} = \frac{0.60}{0.90} = 0.6667$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Kremser Equation for $N_{OG}$

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{1 - S} \ln \left[ (1 - S) \frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2} + S \right]$$

Substituting (note $x_2 = 0$):

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.6667} \ln \left[ (1 - 0.6667) \frac{0.020 - 0}{0.001 - 0} + 0.6667 \right]$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Bracket

$$\frac{0.020}{0.001} = 20$$

$$(1 - 0.6667) \times 20 + 0.6667 = 0.3333 \times 20 + 0.6667 = 6.6667 + 0.6667 = 7.3333$$

### Step 5 — Compute $N_{OG}$

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{0.3333} \ln(7.3333) = 3.0003 \times 1.9924 = 5.977 \; \text{TU}$$

### Step 6 — Compute $H_{OG}$

$$H_{OG} = \frac{G}{K_y a} = \frac{0.020 \; kmol/s}{0.40 \; kmol/(m^3 \cdot s)} = 0.050 \; m$$

### Step 7 — Compute the Packing Height

$$Z = H_{OG} \times N_{OG} = 0.050 \times 5.977 = 0.299 \; m \approx 0.30 \; m$$

### Step 8 — Sanity Check

- A height of $0.30 \; m$ is reasonable for a laboratory-scale absorber with such a high $K_y a$.
- $N_{OG} \approx 6$ is a typical moderate-difficulty separation.

**Final Answer:** $Z \approx 0.30 \; m$ of packing is required. $\square$

## 3.4 Derivation of Hold-Up Time from First Principles

Consider a column of cross-section $A_c$ and total length $H_{col}$. Let the liquid hold-up (volume fraction of liquid in the column) be $h_L$. The volume of liquid retained is:

$$V_{liq} = h_L \cdot A_c \cdot H_{col}$$

If the liquid volumetric flow rate entering the column is $Q_L$, then by definition of mean residence time:

$$\theta_L = \frac{V_{liq}}{Q_L} = \frac{h_L \cdot A_c \cdot H_{col}}{Q_L}$$

Since the superficial liquid velocity is $u_L = Q_L / A_c$:

$$\theta_L = \frac{h_L \cdot H_{col}}{u_L}$$

This elegant result shows that **hold-up time scales linearly with column length** and inversely with liquid velocity. $\square$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Packed Column — Functional Flow Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram shows how a packed absorption column is functionally constructed and how the parameters (Length, Hold-up, Diameter) interact during the design process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inlet Gas y1] --> B[Gas Distributor]
    C[Inlet Liquid x2] --> D[Liquid Distributor]
    B --> E[Packing Section]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Outlet Gas y2]
    E --> G[Outlet Liquid x1]
    H[Inputs: G, L, y1, y2, x2, m, Kya] --> I[Design Calculation Block]
    I --> J[Compute HOG = G / Kya]
    I --> K[Compute NOG via Kremser]
    J --> L[Compute Length Z = HOG x NOG]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Total Column Length]
    E --> N[Hold-up hL = hs + hd]
    N --> O[Residence Time theta = hL x Hcol / uL]
    P[Column Diameter from flooding] --> M
    O --> M
```

## 4.2 HTU-NTU vs HETP — Method Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Need Column Length] --> S2{Is system dilute?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3{Is rate data available?}
    S2 -- No --> S4{Is equilibrium data reliable?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S5[Use HTU-NTU Method]
    S3 -- No --> S6{Is Kya known?}
    S6 -- Yes --> S5
    S6 -- No --> S7[Use HETP Method]
    S4 -- Yes --> S8[Use Kremser Equation]
    S4 -- No --> S9[Use HETP Method]
    S5 --> S10[Z = HOG x NOG]
    S7 --> S11[Z = Nact x HETP]
    S8 --> S12[Z from integration]
```

## 4.3 Hold-Up Components in Packed Column — Conceptual Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Packing_Volume
        P1[Static Hold-up hs] --> H[Total Liquid Hold-up hL]
        P2[Dynamic Hold-up hd] --> H
    end
    H --> R1[Residence time of liquid]
    H --> R2[Residence time of gas]
    R1 --> MT[Drives Mass Transfer]
    R2 --> MT
    MT --> EFF[Column Efficiency]
```

## 4.4 Mass Transfer Driving Force Schematic

The following schematic shows the gas and liquid composition profiles in a counter-current packed absorber — the basis of the integration leading to NTU.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Zaxis[Z = 0 at TOP, Z = Hcol at BOTTOM]
        A1[Gas in: y1 at Z = Hcol] -->|Rising gas| A2[Gas out: y2 at Z = 0]
        B1[Liquid in: x2 at Z = 0] -->|Falling liquid| B2[Liquid out: x1 at Z = Hcol]
    end
    A1 -.->|Driving force y - y star| X1[Interface]
    B1 -.->|Bulk liquid x| X1
    X1 --> MTR[Mass Transfer Across Interface]
    MTR --> YS[Equilibrium y star = m x]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation of the Diagram:** As the gas rises from $y_1$ to $y_2$ (becoming leaner) and the liquid falls from $x_2$ to $x_1$ (becoming richer), the composition gap $(y - y^*)$ — the **driving force** — shrinks. The integral of $dy/(y - y^*)$ over this profile is the **NTU**, and the rate coefficient reciprocal $G/(K_y a)$ is the **HTU**. Their product is the column length.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q:** Define *Height of a Transfer Unit (HTU)* and *Number of Transfer Units (NTU)*. How are they related to the height of a packed column?

**Model Answer:**

**HTU:** The Height of a Transfer Unit is defined as the height of packing required to effect a unit change in composition based on the local driving force. Mathematically,

$$H_{OG} = \frac{G}{K_y \, a}$$

It has units of length (m) and represents the *difficulty* of mass transfer — a small HTU means the packing is very efficient at transferring the solute.

**NTU:** The Number of Transfer Units is defined as the integral of the composition change over the driving force:

$$N_{OG} = \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y - y^*}$$

It is dimensionless and represents the *extent* of the separation required.

**Relation to column length:** The total height of the packed section is the product of HTU and NTU:

$$Z = H_{OG} \times N_{OG}$$

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**Q:** What is *hold-up time* in a chemical engineering contactor? Write the general expression and explain its significance in mass transfer equipment.

**Model Answer:**

Hold-up time $\theta$ is the average time a fluid element resides inside a process vessel or contactor. The general expression is:

$$\theta = \frac{V_{hold-up}}{Q}$$

Where $V_{hold-up}$ is the volume of fluid retained in the equipment and $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate of that fluid.

**Significance:**
- It determines the **contact time** available for mass transfer between phases.
- Longer hold-up times generally yield greater extent of separation (higher conversion or removal efficiency), but at the cost of larger equipment and possibly degradation of heat-sensitive materials.
- In extraction columns, the dispersed phase hold-up and continuous phase hold-up together control the interfacial area available for mass transfer.

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks with internal choice. The most common Module 2 internal choice pairs are: **(HTU-NTU vs. HETP)** OR **(Packed Column Length vs. Tray Column Length)** OR **(Absorber vs. Stripper)**. We will present the most representative pair below.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Packed Column Using HTU-NTU Method

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**

A counter-current packed absorption column is to be designed to absorb $SO_2$ from a gas mixture using water. The following data are given:

- Inlet gas: $y_1 = 0.04$ (mole fraction of $SO_2$)
- Outlet gas (desired): $y_2 = 0.002$
- Inlet liquid: pure water, $x_2 = 0$
- Inlet gas molar flow (solute-free): $G' = 0.05 \; kmol/s$
- Inlet liquid molar flow (solute-free): $L' = 0.30 \; kmol/s$
- Equilibrium relation (dilute): $y^* = 25 \, x$
- Overall mass transfer coefficient: $K_y a = 0.32 \; kmol/(m^3 \cdot s)$

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Calculate the height of packing required using the Kremser equation.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** If the liquid hold-up on the packing is $h_L = 0.06$ and the column cross-sectional area is $0.80 \; m^2$, determine the residence time of the liquid in the column.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Step 1:** Since the system is dilute, $G \approx G'$ and $L \approx L'$.

**Step 2:** Calculate the stripping factor:

$$S = \frac{mG}{L} = \frac{25 \times 0.05}{0.30} = \frac{1.25}{0.30} = 4.167$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** $S > 1$ implies the operating line is steeper than the equilibrium line. This is acceptable (it is a valid absorber operating condition), but ensure the resulting $N_{OG}$ is positive. If $S = 1$, the denominator becomes zero — physically impossible for finite column heights.

**Step 3:** Apply the Kremser equation for $N_{OG}$:

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{1 - S} \ln \left[ (1 - S) \frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2} + S \right]$$

Substituting (note $x_2 = 0$):

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{1 - 4.167} \ln \left[ (1 - 4.167) \frac{0.04 - 0}{0.002 - 0} + 4.167 \right]$$

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{-3.167} \ln \left[ (-3.167)(20) + 4.167 \right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{-3.167} \ln \left[ -63.33 + 4.167 \right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{-3.167} \ln (-59.17)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Major Validation Check:** A negative argument of the logarithm is physically impossible! This means **the separation is infeasible** with the given $G/L$ ratio. The operator must either increase $L$ (the liquid rate) or decrease $G$ to bring $S < 1$ for a feasible absorption.

**Revised interpretation for the exam:** The question expects students to **recognize the infeasibility**, comment on it, and suggest a corrective action (e.g., increase $L$ until $S < 1$, ideally $S \approx 0.5$ to $0.7$).

**Corrective calculation:** Choose $L' = 0.50 \; kmol/s$ instead. Then:

$$S = \frac{25 \times 0.05}{0.50} = 2.5 \quad \text{(still > 1)}$$

Choose $L' = 0.80 \; kmol/s$:

$$S = \frac{25 \times 0.05}{0.80} = 1.5625 \quad \text{(still > 1)}$$

Choose $L' = 2.0 \; kmol/s$:

$$S = \frac{25 \times 0.05}{2.0} = 0.625 \; (< 1, feasible!)$$

**Step 3 (Revised):** With $L' = 2.0 \; kmol/s$, $S = 0.625$:

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.625} \ln \left[ (1 - 0.625) \frac{0.04}{0.002} + 0.625 \right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.375} \ln \left[ 0.375 \times 20 + 0.625 \right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.375} \ln \left[ 7.5 + 0.625 \right]$$

$$= 2.667 \times \ln(8.125) = 2.667 \times 2.095 = 5.587 \; TU$$

**Step 4:** Calculate $H_{OG}$:

$$H_{OG} = \frac{G}{K_y a} = \frac{0.05}{0.32} = 0.156 \; m$$

**Step 5:** Compute the column length:

$$Z = H_{OG} \times N_{OG} = 0.156 \times 5.587 = 0.872 \; m$$

$$\boxed{Z \approx 0.87 \; m \text{ of packing}}$$

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1:** Use the relation derived in Section 3.4:

$$\theta_L = \frac{h_L \cdot A_c \cdot H_{col}}{Q_L}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the liquid volumetric flow rate. The liquid is essentially water, so we need the molar volume:

$$Q_L = \frac{L' \times M_{water}}{\rho_{water}} = \frac{2.0 \; kmol/s \times 18 \; kg/kmol}{1000 \; kg/m^3} = 0.036 \; m^3/s$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the residence time equation. Note: $H_{col} = Z = 0.872 \; m$ (the active contact length).

$$\theta_L = \frac{0.06 \times 0.80 \times 0.872}{0.036} = \frac{0.04186}{0.036} = 1.163 \; s$$

$$\boxed{\theta_L \approx 1.16 \; s}$$

**Interpretation:** A residence time of about **1.16 seconds** for the liquid on the packing. This is a typical value for laboratory/bench-scale packed absorbers.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Tray Column Using HETP Method

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]**

A bubble-cap tray column is to be designed for the absorption of $CO_2$ from a methane-rich gas using a monoethanolamine (MEA) solution. The number of theoretical stages required is $N_{th} = 8$, the overall tray efficiency is $E_o = 0.40$, and the HETP for the system is $0.60 \; m$. The active cross-sectional area of the column is $1.20 \; m^2$, and the liquid hold-up per tray is $0.0050 \; m^3$ per tray.

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Calculate the active length (height) of the column and the actual number of trays.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** If the liquid flow rate is $0.025 \; m^3/s$, determine the mean residence time of the liquid in the column.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the actual number of trays:

$$N_{act} = \frac{N_{th}}{E_o} = \frac{8}{0.40} = 20 \; \text{trays}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the active column length using HETP:

$$Z = N_{act} \times HETP = 20 \times 0.60 = 12.0 \; m$$

$$\boxed{Z = 12.0 \; m}$$

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the total volume of liquid held up in the column:

$$V_{hold-up} = N_{act} \times V_{per \; tray} = 20 \times 0.0050 = 0.10 \; m^3$$

**Step 2:** Apply the residence time formula:

$$\theta_L = \frac{V_{hold-up}}{Q_L} = \frac{0.10 \; m^3}{0.025 \; m^3/s} = 4.0 \; s$$

$$\boxed{\theta_L = 4.0 \; s}$$

**Verification by alternative method:**

Using the relation $\theta_L = h_L \cdot H_{col} / u_L$:
- Average liquid hold-up fraction: $h_L = V_{hold} / (A_c \times Z) = 0.10 / (1.20 \times 12.0) = 0.00694$
- Superficial liquid velocity: $u_L = Q_L / A_c = 0.025 / 1.20 = 0.0208 \; m/s$
- $\theta_L = 0.00694 \times 12.0 / 0.0208 = 0.0833 / 0.0208 = 4.0 \; s$ ✓

Both methods agree. $\square$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **5 Most Common Mark-Loss Points in "Length and Hold-Up Time" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the operating line slope:** Students often write $y = (L/G) x + \text{const}$ when they should use solute-free molar flows on both sides. The slope is $L/G$, but $L$ and $G$ are **inert** flows. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Using $m$ on a weight basis instead of mole basis:** Equilibrium data must be on a **mole fraction** basis when $G$ and $L$ are in molar units. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Forgetting to use the $(1-S)$ correction:** The Kremser equation is sometimes quoted as the *log-mean driving force* form, but it must be the **integrated Kremser** form for full credit. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
> 4. **Wrong sign in stripping vs absorption:** In strippers, the equation is *inverted*: $S = L/(mG)$ vs $S = mG/L$ for absorbers. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
> 5. **HETP used for packed columns and HTU for tray columns:** The methods are NOT interchangeable. HETP is for tray columns (or approximate packed); HTU-NTU is the rigorous rate method. **Penalty: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Length and Hold-Up Time**

- **Length of packed column:** $Z = H_{OG} \times N_{OG}$ (rate-based) or $Z = N_{act} \times HETP$ (stage-based).
- **HTU definition:** $H_{OG} = G / (K_y a)$ — measures *difficulty* of transfer.
- **NTU definition:** $N_{OG} = \int_{y_2}^{y_1} dy/(y - y^*)$ — measures *extent* of separation.
- **Kremser/Colburn (dilute) form:** $N_{OG} = \dfrac{1}{1 - S} \ln \left[ (1 - S)\dfrac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2} + S \right]$ where $S = mG/L$.
- **Combined HTU relation:** $H_{OG} = H_G + (mG/L) H_L$ — links individual and overall HTUs.
- **Tray column length:** $Z = N_{act} \times HETP = (N_{th}/E_o) \times HETP$.
- **HETP typical range:** $0.4$ to $1.0 \; m$ (small to industrial columns).
- **Hold-up time (general):** $\theta = V_{hold}/Q$ — independent of column type.
- **Liquid hold-up in packing:** $h_L = h_s + h_d$ (static + dynamic).
- **Residence time in column:** $\theta_L = h_L \cdot H_{col} / u_L$ — scales with column length.
- **Dispersed phase hold-up in extractors:** $\phi_d = Q_d \theta_d / V_{col}$.
- **Physical constants to remember:** $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$, $R = 8.314 \; J/(mol \cdot K)$.
- **Inlet/outlet notation convention:** $y_1, x_1$ = rich end (gas inlet for absorber, liquid inlet for stripper); $y_2, x_2$ = lean end.
- **Validity check:** $N_{OG}$ must be **positive** and **finite**; if not, the operating conditions are infeasible and must be revised.
- **Feasibility:** For absorption, $S = mG/L < 1$ is generally required (or at least $S$ not too large); for stripping, the analogous condition is $S' = L/(mG) < 1$.

> **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Length and Hold Up Time**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITION

### 1.1 Tall Pressure Vessels — Formal Definition

A **tall pressure vessel** is defined by the Indian Standard **IS 2825: 1969 (Reaffirmed 2007) — "Code for Unfired Pressure Vessels"** as a vertical, cylindrical pressure vessel whose unsupported height is sufficiently large that the bending moment generated by environmental lateral loads (Wind + Seismic) becomes a controlling parameter in the mechanical design of the shell courses and the support skirt. In KTU practice, vessels with an unsupported **height-to-diameter ratio H/D > 5** (and absolute height usually above 8–10 m) are categorised as "tall columns" and require a dedicated wind / seismic design check in addition to the internal pressure check.

### 1.2 Multi-Course Vessels

A **multi-course vessel** is fabricated by welding together cylindrical shell courses of *different plate thicknesses*. This is done because:
1. Manufacturing mills supply plates in standard widths (typically 1.5 m, 2.0 m, 2.5 m, 3.0 m). To build a tall shell economically, several courses are welded axially.
2. The bending stress due to wind/seismic is maximum at the base and decreases towards the top. The required shell thickness therefore varies along the height — thicker plates at the bottom, thinner plates at the top.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Multi-course design *permits* course-by-course thickness optimisation so that the *most economical* vessel wall satisfies both the internal pressure hoop-stress criterion (governs lower courses) and the combined-stress criterion (governs upper courses where bending dominates).

### 1.3 Skirt Support — Definition

A **skirt** is a cylindrical shell (or truncated cone) welded to the bottom head of a vertical pressure vessel and bolted to a concrete pedestal via an annular **base ring** and **anchor bolts**. The skirt:
- Transmits the weight of the vessel, fluid and internals to the foundation.
- Resists the **overturning moment** generated by wind and earthquake.
- Provides access for inspection of bottom head welds and pipe connections.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Flag-Pole and the Tree"

Imagine a tall, slender flag-pole planted in the ground. Two things can topple it:
1. **Wind** pushes it sideways, creating a bending moment at the base.
2. **Earthquake** shakes the ground, generating inertia forces proportional to the pole's mass.

A tall pressure vessel is identical — it is a hollow steel "tree" planted on a "skirt-foundation". The **wind is steady**, the **earthquake is dynamic**. The *skirt* is the root system that resists both, while the *shell thickness* must be checked against the bending stresses that arise from these lateral loads *combined* with the membrane stresses from internal pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Governing Reference Codes**
> - **IS 2825 : 1969** — Unfired Pressure Vessels (primary code for shell stress check).
> - **IS 875 (Part 3) : 2015** — Wind loads on structures.
> - **IS 1893 (Part 1) : 2016** — Criteria for earthquake resistant design.
> - **IS 800 : 2007** — General construction in steel (skirt, gusset, base ring).
> - **IS 2062 : 2011** — Hot rolled medium and high tensile structural steel (plate specification).

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants Used

| Constant | Value | Use |
|----------|-------|-----|
| Acceleration due to gravity | **g = 9.81 m/s²** | Wind/Seismic force calculation |
| Air density | **ρ_air = 1.226 kg/m³** | Wind pressure formula |
| Seismic zone factor (IS 1893) | **Z = 0.10 to 0.40** | For Kerala (Zone III) Z = **0.16** |
| Importance factor | **I = 1.0 – 1.5** | Process vessels usually I = 1.0 |
| Response reduction factor | **R = 3.0 – 5.0** | Ductile steel vessel R = 4.0 |

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Why External Loads Matter in Tall Vessels

In a *short* vessel, the wall thickness is governed solely by the **hoop stress** from internal pressure:

$$f_h = \frac{PD}{2t}$$

For a *tall* vessel, the lateral load $W$ (wind or seismic) creates an **overturning moment** $M$ at any horizontal section of height $h$ measured from the base. This moment induces an **axial bending stress** in the shell:

$$f_b = \frac{4M}{\pi D^2 t}$$

The combined longitudinal stress in the shell at any section is therefore:

$$f_L = \frac{PD}{4t} \pm \frac{4M}{\pi D^2 t} \quad \text{(pressure + bending, algebraic sum)}$$

The shell must simultaneously satisfy:
1. **Hoop stress criterion** — usually the governing criterion for lower courses.
2. **Combined longitudinal stress criterion** — governs at locations of high moment (vessel base and just below the top tangent line).
3. **Buckling check (external pressure + axial compression)** — governs upper courses under combined compression and bending.

### 2.2 Procedure Outline (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Establish vessel geometry** — Diameter $D$, height $H$, course layout, top and bottom head types.
2. **Compute wind pressure and force** as per IS 875 (Part 3).
3. **Compute seismic base shear and distribution** as per IS 1893 (Part 1).
4. **Determine the governing lateral force** = MAX(Wind, Seismic) — usually Wind dominates for tall, slender vessels; Seismic dominates for short, heavy vessels.
5. **Compute overturning moment** at the vessel base $M_{max}$ and at the top tangent line.
6. **Determine course-by-course thickness** for combined pressure + bending + axial load.
7. **Design the skirt** — skirt thickness, base ring, gusset plates, anchor bolts.
8. **Check anchor bolt for tension + shear** + check base plate bending + pedestal concrete bearing.

### 2.3 Wind Load — IS 875 (Part 3) : 2015 Logic

The design wind speed at height $z$ is:

$$V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$$

| Factor | Meaning | Typical Value |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| $V_b$ | Basic wind speed (3-second gust at 10 m height) | **39 m/s to 50 m/s** for Kerala (Kottayam map value ≈ **39 m/s**) |
| $k_1$ | Risk coefficient (probability factor) | 0.92 – 1.08 |
| $k_2$ | Terrain, height & structure size factor | 0.67 – 2.10 |
| $k_3$ | Topography factor | 1.0 (flat) to 1.36 (hill) |
| $k_4$ | Importance factor for cyclonic region | 1.0 – 1.30 |

Design wind **dynamic pressure**:

$$p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2 \quad \text{(N/m², with } V_z \text{ in m/s)}$$

Wind **force** on vessel:

$$F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot p_z$$

where $C_f$ is the **force coefficient** for a cylindrical surface. For a finite cylinder ($H/D < 12$), $C_f$ depends on $V_z D$ (vibration number) and surface roughness; for design, **$C_f = 0.7$** is the standard value used for plain steel cylinders.

### 2.4 Seismic Load — IS 1893 (Part 1) : 2016 Logic

The design **horizontal seismic coefficient**:

$$A_h = \frac{Z}{2} \cdot \frac{I}{R} \cdot \frac{S_a}{g}$$

**Design base shear**:

$$V_B = A_h \cdot W_{eff}$$

where $W_{eff}$ is the effective seismic weight (empty vessel + permanent attachments + 25 % of design content for process liquids — per IS 1893).

Distribution of base shear along the height follows the IS 1893 formula:

$$Q_i = V_B \cdot \frac{W_i \cdot h_i^2}{\sum_j W_j h_j^2}$$

The **overturning moment** at the base is:

$$M_{OT} = \sum_i Q_i \cdot h_i$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU high-yield. Memorise the table layout; the substitutions and unit conversions are what examiners test.**

| # | Design Quantity | Governing Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|-----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Hoop stress (internal pressure) | $f_h = \dfrac{P D}{2 t}$ | P = internal pressure, D = inner diameter, t = shell thickness |
| 2 | Longitudinal membrane stress (pressure only) | $f_L^{(P)} = \dfrac{P D}{4 t}$ | For closed-end vessel |
| 3 | Bending stress in shell (wind/seismic) | $f_b = \dfrac{4 M}{\pi D^2 t}$ | M = bending moment at section |
| 4 | Combined longitudinal stress | $f_L = \dfrac{P D}{4 t} \pm \dfrac{4 M}{\pi D^2 t}$ | Use **maximum absolute value** |
| 5 | Wind speed at height z | $V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdot k_4$ | IS 875 (Part 3) |
| 6 | Wind pressure | $p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^2$ | N/m² |
| 7 | Wind force on vessel section | $F = C_f \cdot A_e \cdot p_z$ | C_f = 0.7 for plain steel cylinder |
| 8 | Seismic horizontal coefficient | $A_h = \dfrac{Z}{2} \cdot \dfrac{I}{R} \cdot \dfrac{S_a}{g}$ | IS 1893 |
| 9 | Base shear | $V_B = A_h \cdot W_{eff}$ | W_eff = effective weight |
| 10 | Lateral force at level i (seismic) | $Q_i = V_B \cdot \dfrac{W_i h_i^2}{\sum W_j h_j^2}$ | — |
| 11 | Required shell thickness (combined) | $t = \dfrac{P D}{4 f_a} + \dfrac{6 M}{\pi D^2 f_a}$ (algebraic simplification) | f_a = allowable stress |
| 12 | Required skirt thickness | $t_{skirt} = \dfrac{6 M_{max}}{\pi D_s^2 \, f_b}$ | D_s = skirt outer diameter, f_b = allowable bending stress |
| 13 | Anchor bolt tensile load | $T_b = \dfrac{4 M_{max}}{N_{bolt} \, D_b} - \dfrac{W_{total}}{N_{bolt}}$ | N_bolt = number of bolts, D_b = bolt-circle diameter |
| 14 | Anchor bolt area required | $A_b = \dfrac{T_b}{f_{tb}}$ | f_tb = allowable tensile stress of bolt |
| 15 | Base plate thickness | $t_{bp} = 1.414 \, b \, \sqrt{\dfrac{3 w_m}{f_b}}$ | b = projection width, w_m = max bearing pressure |
| 16 | Bearing pressure on concrete | $w = \dfrac{W_{total}}{A_{bearing}}$ | A_bearing = effective base ring area |
| 17 | Course selection (plate width) | $L_c \le \text{available plate width}$ | Course length per IS 2825 / material availability |

### 2.6 Multi-Course Thickness Optimisation — Logic

The vessel is divided axially into $n$ courses. For each course $i$, calculate:

**Step A** — Thickness from internal pressure (hoop):
$$t_{p,i} = \frac{P D_i}{2 f_a \, E_j} + c$$

where $E_j$ is the joint efficiency (categorised per IS 2825) and $c$ is the corrosion allowance.

**Step B** — Thickness from combined longitudinal stress (pressure + bending moment at bottom of course $i$):
$$t_{b,i} = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \sqrt{ \left(\frac{P}{f_a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{16 M_i}{\pi D_i^2 f_a}\right)^2 } + \frac{P}{f_a} \right] + c$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Practical Note:** The selected thickness for a course is the **maximum** of $t_{p,i}$ and $t_{b,i}$, rounded up to the next standard plate thickness (6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20, 22, 25, 28, 32 mm …). Lower courses use thicker plates; upper courses (away from the base moment) may only need to satisfy the hoop-stress requirement and therefore use thinner plates — this is the **economic incentive** for multi-course construction.
